When a user changes a control, the component updates the relevant URL parameters (using navigation or history APIs) and then relies on a central controller or router listener to re-run getProductFilterOptions (if categoryId changed) and searchProducts.
- Changing categoryId in the URL triggers re-fetching of filter options: getProductFilterOptions(categoryKey = newCategoryId) and a new searchProducts call to update the product list.
- Changing query in the URL triggers a new searchProducts call, keeping categoryId and filters as-is unless modified.
- Changing priceMax, efficiencyRangeId, featureTag, or ratingMin in the URL triggers searchProducts with an updated filters object, without modifying categoryId or query.
- Changing sortBy in the URL triggers searchProducts with the new sort order while keeping query, categoryId, and filters stable.
- Changing page in the URL triggers searchProducts with the new page, leaving all other parameters unchanged.

URL consistency:
- Every interaction that affects results also updates the URL, enabling shareable links and browser back/forward navigation to restore the exact state.
- No state is persisted in localStorage or other local persistence; only URL parameters and in-memory state derived from the three interfaces are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and reacts to URL parameter changes. Reads URL params (categoryId, query, priceMax, efficiencyRangeId, featureTag, ratingMin, sortBy, page), calls getActiveProductCategories to load active categories, calls getProductFilterOptions with the current categoryKey (from categoryId) to obtain filter/sort metadata, and calls searchProducts with the combined parameters to populate the product listings.

Displays a list of active product categories and highlights the currently selected category based on categoryId. Allows users to switch categories, which updates the categoryId URL parameter and reloads filters and products.

Provides an on-page text input for refining the product search keyword. Initialized from the query URL parameter. On submit, updates the query parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchProducts through the page-init-controller.

Shows filter controls derived from getProductFilterOptions (price range metadata, efficiency ranges, feature tags, rating thresholds). Each control reflects the current URL parameters (priceMax, efficiencyRangeId, featureTag, ratingMin). On user interaction, it updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), resets page to 1 when filters change, and lets the controller call searchProducts with the new filters.

Displays a list of currently active filters (derived solely from URL parameters and available filter options). Each chip corresponds to a URL parameter (priceMax, efficiencyRangeId, featureTag, ratingMin). Clicking a chip’s remove action clears that parameter from the URL and triggers a refreshed search.

Displays a dropdown or similar control listing sort options obtained from getProductFilterOptions.sortOptions. The currently selected option is bound to the sortBy URL parameter. When the user changes sort order, sortBy is updated in the URL, page is reset to 1, and searchProducts is invoked via the controller.

Shows a concise summary of the current search state, such as total number of matching products and the current page. Reads total, page, and pageSize from the latest searchProducts response and query/categoryId from the URL to inform the user what they are viewing.

Displays the list of products returned by searchProducts in a grid or list format. Each item shows key product fields (name, shortDescription, price, efficiencyType, efficiencyValue, isHighEfficiency, ratingAverage, ratingCount, featureTags, badges, imageUrl, categoryName). Each product card and product name is clickable and navigates to product.html with id, categoryId, and query set in the URL.

Provides controls for navigating between pages of results. Uses total, page, and pageSize from searchProducts to compute available pages. When a user selects a new page, it updates the page URL parameter and triggers searchProducts with the new page while preserving current query, categoryId, filters, and sortBy.

Handles the case where searchProducts returns an empty products array. Displays a message indicating no products were found given the current query and filters. Provides actions to clear filters or adjust search (e.g., clearAll) by removing filter parameters from the URL and re-triggering searchProducts.

Shows or hides a loading state while getProductFilterOptions or searchProducts requests are in progress. It is controlled by asynchronous request state in the page-init-controller and does not manage its own data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow echoing the mockup: a full-width content container centered on the page, with a horizontal band for category navigation at the top of the main content, a prominent search bar just beneath, and then a large two-column main area. In this main area, the left column is the filter sidebar and the right column is the product listings, mirroring the mockup’s left-text/right-image About section (left filters as the 'controls' text block, right results as the 'hero' content). Within the right column, elements stack in a clear order: active filters row, sort and summary band, product grid, then pagination.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow echoing the mockup: a full-width content container centered on the page, with a horizontal band for category navigation at the top of the main content, a prominent search bar just beneath, and then a large two-column main area. In this main area, the left column is the filter sidebar and the right column is the product listings, mirroring the mockup’s left-text/right-image About section (left filters as the 'controls' text block, right results as the 'hero' content). Within the right column, elements stack in a clear order: active filters row, sort and summary band, product grid, then pagination.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered content container (approx. 1120–1200px wide or about 80–90% of viewport on large screens) sits within full-bleed white sections. The category navigation and search area together take a moderate vertical slice at the top with 32–48px padding above and below. Below that, the main content region uses a two-column layout: roughly 30–35% width for the filter sidebar on the left and 65–70% for the results on the right. Vertical padding between major bands (nav/search vs main content, main content vs bottom of page) sits around 48–64px, while internal spacing between controls (chips, sort bar, grid, pagination) uses 16–24px gaps for a roomy but efficient feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low to moderate: generous white space around the category tabs, search input, filter groups, and product cards. Text blocks (like results summaries and filter labels) remain concise with comfortable line spacing. Controls are not tightly packed; instead, each cluster (filters, chips, sort+summary, grid, pagination) is clearly separated with 16–24px margins, maintaining the friendly, uncluttered look from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mimics the mockup’s loose Z-pattern: the eye lands first on the top-left of the content area at the category navigation, scans right across the category tabs, then drops down to the large, central search bar. From there, attention moves down-left to the filter sidebar (as the main control panel), then sweeps right to the results summary and sort controls, and finally flows downward through the product grid. Active filter chips and the pagination controls act as secondary anchors at the top and bottom of the results column, while a loading or no-results state can temporarily become the central focal point. On scroll, a sticky filter sidebar helps maintain a stable left anchor similar to the About section’s fixed text block opposite the illustration.

layout.overall_description: The Product Listings page sits within a clean, white canvas, framed by the site’s existing announcement bar and header above. Immediately below the header, the main content container is centered and limited to a comfortable width, preserving plenty of white margin on either side. At the top of this content area, a horizontal band of category navigation tabs stretches across, inviting users to choose between heating, cooling, thermostats, and other HVAC categories. Just beneath, a wide search bar, visually similar to a hero input, runs across the center of the page with generous padding above and below, echoing the mockup’s bold but simple section headers. Below this, the page splits into a two-column layout reminiscent of the About section: on the left, a taller filter sidebar, occupying about one-third of the width, stacks filter groups vertically with airy gaps; on the right, a broader results column houses the active filter chips row, a neat sort-and-summary bar, and then a spacious grid of product cards. Each product card is surrounded by ample white space and aligned to a 12-column grid, with 2–3 cards per row depending on viewport width. Pagination controls sit centered at the bottom of the results column, visually closing the layout. When no products match, the grid area is replaced by a playful empty-state illustration and message; when loading, a subtle overlay or skeleton grid covers the product area without disturbing the rest of the layout. Overall, the page feels light, approachable, and structured, using bright accents and rounded controls to echo the brand’s friendly, local-service personality while maintaining a clear, marketing-style flow from top filters to bottom results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render any visible UI; it sits logically at the top of the page structure, initializing data and state before the visible components (category navigation, search, filters, and results) appear. It occupies no space in the visual layout and does not affect spacing or alignment of other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates behind the scenes without any spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category navigation appears at the top of the main content area, directly beneath the global header section. It spans the full width of the centered content container (about 80–90% of the viewport on large screens) and is presented as a horizontal row of pill-shaped tabs or buttons. The left edge of the first tab aligns with the left edge of the main content column, reinforcing a strong left anchor similar to the About section’s heading. Vertical padding above and below this band is in the 24–32px range, so the navigation feels noticeable but not overly tall. Tabs are evenly spaced with 16–24px gaps, and their height is moderate (around 40–48px), echoing the rounded CTAs from the mockup. On smaller screens, the tabs can wrap to a second line or scroll horizontally, still occupying a shallow band at the top of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page content; not fixed, but remains visually prominent due to its position at the top of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search refinement form sits directly below the category navigation within the same centered content container, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It is visually dominant in this zone, with a large search input stretching across about 60–70% of the container width, centered horizontally on the page. The input and its accompanying button (e.g., a cyan "Search" CTA) use rounded corners and 12–16px internal padding to mirror the playful, friendly look from the mockup. Above and below the search form, there is comfortable whitespace (around 24–32px), giving it a hero-like presence before the denser product area. On wider screens, any additional refinement controls (like quick toggles) can appear inline to the right of the main input; on smaller screens, these stack beneath the search field while still staying within the same visual block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top content region; as users scroll, it moves offscreen along with the category navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar begins immediately below the search form, aligned to the left edge of the main content container. It forms the left column of the main two-column layout and occupies roughly 30–35% of the container width on desktop. The top of the sidebar aligns vertically with the top of the results column to its right, echoing the About section’s side-by-side alignment. Inside, filter groups (e.g., Price, Brand, Type, Efficiency Rating) stack vertically with 16–24px gaps between each group. Each group has a concise heading and a compact list of options, with 8–16px internal padding and clear separation lines or subtle cards. The sidebar extends downward, typically taller than the initial viewport, but maintains generous inner padding (24–32px) from its own top and bottom edges to avoid feeling cramped. On tablet and mobile, the sidebar collapses into a "Filters" panel that moves above the results column, spanning nearly 100% width with a card-like container and a toggle control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the sidebar is sticky within the main content area: it remains fixed relative to the viewport once scrolled into place, with a top offset that clears the header and top search area (e.g., 80–120px). On smaller screens, it becomes a collapsible section that scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filter chips sit at the very top of the right-hand results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar. They occupy the full width of the results column (approximately 65–70% of the container) and are placed within a slim band separated by 8–16px above and below. Chips appear as small, pill-shaped elements with subtle outlines or filled backgrounds in the brand’s accent color, spaced 8–12px apart. The chips start at the left edge of the results column and can wrap onto multiple lines if many are active. When no filters are applied, this area shrinks to a minimal height or is replaced by a single subtle helper text like "No filters applied". The chips maintain a light density, with enough padding inside each chip to feel tappable and friendly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results column; scrolls out of view as the user moves down through the product grid. On narrow screens, chips can become horizontally scrollable to avoid excessive vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort bar sits directly below the active filter chips within the right-hand results column, forming a shallow horizontal band. It shares the same width as the results column and is separated from the chips by about 8–16px of vertical space. Within this band, the left side is reserved for the results summary text (e.g., "32 products"), while the right side holds the sort control (e.g., a dropdown for sort by price, popularity, etc.). This pairing creates a left-to-right line that the eye follows naturally, similar to the header’s logo-then-navigation scan in the mockup. The band’s height is around 48–56px, with 12–16px of padding above and below the controls. On smaller screens, the summary can stack above the sort control, both left-aligned, maintaining the same gentle spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the results column; moves out of view as the user scrolls down through the products. It does not stick to the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary text is visually integrated into the sort bar as its left-aligned element. It appears in a single short line (e.g., "32 products found"), starting at the left edge of the results column and aligned vertically with the sort control on the right. The summary uses slightly larger or bolder text than body copy but remains smaller than section headings, matching the mockup’s low-density, readable labeling style. It shares the same vertical band as the sort control, with around 12–16px padding inside the band, and has a small horizontal gap (8–16px) from the active filter chips row above. On narrow viewports, the summary occupies its own line above the sort dropdown, with consistent spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the sort bar band; scrolls with the page and does not have independent sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid is the dominant content block in the right-hand column. It begins directly below the sort bar, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space, and spans the full width of the results column (about 65–70% of the main container). On large screens, products are arranged in a card-based grid with 3 columns when space allows, falling back to 2 columns on mid-sized tablets and 1 column on smaller mobile devices, all aligned to the underlying 12-column grid. Each card has generous padding (16–24px), rounded corners, and a light shadow or border, consistent with the flat, friendly aesthetics. Vertical and horizontal gaps between cards stay in the 16–24px range, creating a spacious, breathable layout rather than a dense catalog. The grid stretches downward and constitutes the main scrolling region, with the filter sidebar visible to its left on larger screens. The entire grid area feels visually analogous to the About section’s illustration block: colorful and content-rich, acting as the main focal area adjacent to the left-side controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically with the page; its top is anchored beneath the sort bar and its bottom is capped by the pagination controls. When loading or when there are no results, this grid area is fully or partially replaced by the corresponding state indicators.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the results column, directly below the last row of the product grid with a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px to clearly separate them as the end of the list. They are horizontally centered within the results column and may present as a row of numbered buttons, next/previous arrows, and possibly a page-size selector. Buttons follow the rounded, friendly CTA style with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. The surrounding whitespace mirrors the mockup’s section separation: sufficient breathing room above and a bit of extra padding below (32–48px) before the page’s footer region. On smaller screens, the pagination stack may simplify to "Previous" and "Next" buttons while maintaining the same centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the results content; appears only when there are enough products to paginate. It scrolls into and out of view as the user navigates the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results state occupies the same spatial region as the product grid and pagination, within the right-hand results column, when a search or filter combination returns zero products. In this state, the area below the sort bar transforms into a centered empty-state card. This card is vertically padded (32–48px top and bottom) and horizontally constrained to about 60–70% of the results column width, centered within it. At the top of the card, a playful illustration (potentially echoing the brand’s penguin character looking confused or searching) takes up roughly the top half of the card, followed by a bold, friendly heading and a short explanatory sentence underneath. Any suggestion actions (like "Clear filters" or "Adjust search") appear as rounded buttons or links below the text, centered with 16–24px spacing. The background remains mostly white to match the rest of the page, with the illustration providing the main color accent, making the no-results state feel approachable and on-brand rather than severe.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the product grid and pagination region when there are zero results. It scrolls normally within the page and does not overlay other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is visually tied to the product grid area. When products are being fetched or refreshed (e.g., after changing filters or category), a semi-transparent overlay or skeleton grid appears over the current product area within the right-hand results column. This overlay begins just below the sort bar and extends to cover the grid region and, if needed, the pagination area. In a skeleton implementation, placeholder cards mimic the product card shapes, arranged in the same grid pattern, maintaining the overall layout and spacing while signaling loading. Alternatively, a centered spinner or playful brand-related animation (such as a small animated penguin icon) appears in the middle of the grid area, with the rest of the grid dimmed. The filter sidebar and controls above (category navigation, search, chips, sort bar) remain visible and stable, preserving the overall page structure while the product content updates.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the product grid portion of the results column without shifting surrounding layout. It is transient and disappears once loading completes, returning the grid and pagination to their normal appearance.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single HVAC product and supports installation quote requests and installation scheduling.

core_features: Load detailed product information using getProductDetails based on the id URL parameter.
Display product attributes such as name, category, descriptions, pricing, efficiency label, feature tags, and customer rating label.
Show any applicable promotions or offers for the current product using the applicableOffers returned by getProductDetails.
Provide a 'Request Installation Quote' action that scrolls to and focuses the installation quote form.
Provide a 'Schedule Installation' action that navigates to schedule_service.html with serviceId, productId, and bookingType=installation, using data from getProductDetails.
Display an installation quote form that collects user details and submits to createInstallationQuoteRequest with the current productId.
Handle loading, success, and error states for both getProductDetails and createInstallationQuoteRequest, surfacing clear status messages to the user.
Provide a 'Back to results' navigation that uses categoryId and query URL parameters (when present) to return to products.html with preserved context.
user_workflows: View product from a listing: 1) User clicks a product on products.html. 2) Browser navigates to product.html?id={productId}&categoryId={categoryId?}&query={query?}. 3) On load, the page reads id from the URL. 4) The page calls getProductDetails with productId=id. 5) While loading, a loading state is shown instead of product details. 6) On success, the product detail components render using the returned data. 7) The user can review descriptions, pricing, specifications, and applicable offers.

Request an installation quote: 1) User is viewing product.html with product details loaded. 2) User clicks 'Request Installation Quote' in the installation CTA panel. 3) The page scrolls to and focuses the quote request form. 4) User completes required fields: name, address, city, ZIP, phone, email, and optional state and notes. 5) User submits the form. 6) The page calls createInstallationQuoteRequest with productId from the current product and the form field values. 7) While waiting for a response, the form is disabled or shows a submitting state. 8) On success (success=true), a success message and any returned message/estimatedResponseTimeHours are shown, and the form may clear or stay filled according to design. 9) On failure (success=false or request error), an error message is shown and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Schedule an installation: 1) User is viewing product.html with product details loaded. 2) The page has received installationServiceId from getProductDetails. 3) User clicks 'Schedule Installation' in the installation CTA panel. 4) The page constructs a URL to schedule_service.html including serviceId=installationServiceId, productId=current product.id, bookingType=installation. 5) Browser navigates to schedule_service.html with those parameters, where scheduling is completed on that page.

Return to product results: 1) User arrived from products.html and the URL contains categoryId and/or query. 2) The page detects these parameters on load and shows a 'Back to results' link. 3) User clicks 'Back to results'. 4) The page navigates to products.html with the same categoryId and query values passed as URL parameters so the listing page can restore its previous filter/search context.

Handle direct product link with no listing context: 1) User opens product.html?id={productId} without categoryId or query. 2) The page loads product data via getProductDetails. 3) Breadcrumb or 'Back to results' either is hidden or falls back to a generic products.html link without additional parameters. 4) All other product detail and quote/schedule functions remain available.
interactions: On page load or URL change, read the id, categoryId, and query parameters and trigger a data load using getProductDetails with productId=id.
If id is missing or invalid, skip the getProductDetails call and show an error state indicating that the product cannot be loaded.
When getProductDetails is in progress, display a loading indicator and hide or disable product-dependent actions ('Schedule Installation', 'Request Installation Quote').
When getProductDetails succeeds, bind the returned product, efficiencyLabel, ratingLabel, applicableOffers, installationServiceId, and installationServiceName to the relevant components.
If getProductDetails fails or returns a non-active product (isActive=false), display an error or 'product not available' state and do not show installation or quote actions.
When the user clicks 'Request Installation Quote' in the CTA panel, scroll the viewport to the quote request form, set focus to the first form field, and ensure the form is ready for input.
In the quote request form, validate required fields (contactName, streetAddress, city, zip, phone, email) on submit. If validation fails, prevent submission and highlight the fields.

On successful client-side validation of the quote form, call createInstallationQuoteRequest with productId from the loaded product and all form values, then show a submitting state until the response returns.

When createInstallationQuoteRequest returns success=true, show a success status message using the returned message and estimatedResponseTimeHours, and optionally reset or lock the form according to chosen behavior.
When createInstallationQuoteRequest returns success=false or an error occurs, show an error status message and keep the user's form inputs so they can correct and resubmit.

When the user clicks 'Schedule Installation', construct and navigate to schedule_service.html?serviceId={installationServiceId}&productId={product.id}&bookingType=installation. If installationServiceId is missing, show a message and prevent navigation.
When the user clicks 'Back to results', navigate to products.html with categoryId and query appended only if they exist in the current URL (e.g., products.html?categoryId={categoryId}&query={query}).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL. The product.html page reads URL parameters id, categoryId, and query on load and whenever the URL changes. The id parameter is required for loading product data. If id exists, the page calls getProductDetails(productId=id). While the request is pending, a loading state is active, which suppresses product-dependent UI. On successful response, the page stores product, efficiencyLabel, ratingLabel, applicableOffers, installationServiceId, and installationServiceName in in-memory state and renders all bound components. If the response indicates the product is not active or if the request fails, an error state is set and product actions are hidden. The categoryId and query parameters are not sent to any interface but are stored in simple in-memory state for navigation purposes; they determine whether a 'Back to results' link is shown and how its target URL is constructed. Quote form state (field values, validation errors, submission status) is maintained in memory and is reset or updated only by user input and the result of createInstallationQuoteRequest; it does not affect the URL. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used; all state is derived from the current URL parameters, interface responses, and transient in-page interactions.

components.functionality: Reads and interprets URL parameters (id, categoryId, query) on initial load and on any URL change. Validates that id is present and passes parsed values to downstream components as initial state.

Calls getProductDetails with productId taken from the id URL parameter. Manages loading, success, and error states for fetching detailed product data and exposes the result to presentation components.

Displays a contextual 'Back to results' link when categoryId or query is present in the URL. On click, navigates back to products.html while preserving the originating categoryId and query parameters.
Shows high-level product information from getProductDetails including product.name, categoryName, ratingLabel, price, and imageUrl. Provides an at-a-glance summary at the top of the page.

Displays detailed product information including shortDescription, longDescription, efficiencyLabel, efficiencyType, efficiencyValue, isHighEfficiency, and featureTags to help the user evaluate the product.
Lists any applicableOffers returned by getProductDetails for the current product, showing each offer's title, shortDescription, discountLabel, promotionalPrice, and discountPercent when available.

Provides primary actions related to installation: a 'Request Installation Quote' button that scrolls the page to the quote form and a 'Schedule Installation' button that navigates to schedule_service.html with serviceId from installationServiceId and productId from the current product. If installationServiceId is not available, the schedule button is disabled or hidden.

Collects installation quote details for the current product, including contactName, streetAddress, city, state, zip, phone, email, and optional notes. On submit, validates required fields and calls createInstallationQuoteRequest with productId from the loaded product and the form data. Handles submission states and response feedback.

Displays success or error messages related to installation quote submissions. Consumes the result from createInstallationQuoteRequest (success flag, message, estimatedResponseTimeHours) and any network or validation errors.

Controls global display states for the product detail page, showing a loading indicator while getProductDetails is in progress, an error message if the request fails or the product is inactive, and the main content only when valid product data is available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The product detail page content sits below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, in a centered content container that follows a 12‑column responsive grid. The page flows top‑to‑bottom in clear, full-width sections: a slim breadcrumb row, then a hero-like product overview section using a two‑column split (left: product summary, right: installation CTA card), followed by a second two‑column band (left: efficiency/features, right: offers), and finally a full‑width quote form section with inline status messages at the top. Non-visual functional components (URL resolver, data loader, state handler) sit logically at the top of the page structure but do not occupy visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The product detail page content sits below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, in a centered content container that follows a 12‑column responsive grid. The page flows top‑to‑bottom in clear, full-width sections: a slim breadcrumb row, then a hero-like product overview section using a two‑column split (left: product summary, right: installation CTA card), followed by a second two‑column band (left: efficiency/features, right: offers), and finally a full‑width quote form section with inline status messages at the top. Non-visual functional components (URL resolver, data loader, state handler) sit logically at the top of the page structure but do not occupy visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Each major content band (overview, details/offers, quote form) uses generous vertical padding of roughly 48–64px top and bottom, matching the spacious About section from the mockup. Within these bands, columns follow an approximate 5/12–7/12 split with a comfortable horizontal gutter of about 24–32px. The hero overview section allocates roughly 55–60% of the width to the left summary and 40–45% to the right CTA card. The breadcrumb row is shallow, with about 16–24px of vertical padding. Status messages and loading/error states appear as full-width banners or blocks that occupy modest yet noticeable vertical space without feeling dense.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is kept low to moderate density: large, readable headings, short paragraphs, and compact lists of attributes or features. Each card or section has 16–24px padding inside, and adjacent elements are separated by 16–24px vertically. Form fields are grouped with sufficient breathing room, using the base 8px spacing scale so nothing feels cramped. The right-hand cards (CTA, offers) are visually strong but not cluttered—few buttons, key facts, and optional small labels only.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a soft Z-pattern like the mockup. The eye starts top-left on the breadcrumb and product name, then moves right to the prominent installation CTA panel in the hero section. From there, it drops down to the left side again to scan the detailed efficiency/features, then moves right to the offers panel. Finally, the flow drops down once more to the full-width quote form, with status messages at the top of that section. A persistent site-wide chat widget in the bottom-left (outside these components) lightly anchors the lower-left corner as a tertiary focal point, consistent with the mockup’s behavior.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s cyan announcement bar and white navigation header, the product detail page begins with a slim, left-aligned breadcrumb row inside a centered content container. Directly beneath it, a spacious hero-style overview section stretches full-width, but its contents are constrained to about 1100–1200px and split into two columns: on the left, a bold product name, key specs, and a short descriptive paragraph; on the right, a colorful, rounded CTA card inviting users to schedule installation or request a quote. This arrangement mirrors the mockup’s About section, with roughly 60% width given to the left text block and 40% to the right card. Generous white space surrounds this band. Scrolling down reveals a second section where the left two-thirds focus on efficiency ratings and product features, while the right one-third to two-fifths houses a compact offers/promotions card, maintaining a consistent horizontal split and clear gutter. Further down, a dedicated quote section spans the full container width, starting with status messages if present, followed by a clean, approachable form that uses a simple, airy layout. All sections maintain consistent vertical rhythm (48–64px padding), rounded buttons, and playful but clean styling, echoing the friendly, local-service feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly at the very top of the page’s logical structure, resolving the product identifier from the URL before any visual content is shown. It has no visual representation, occupies no width or height, and does not affect spacing between any UI elements. Conceptually, it sits above the breadcrumb row in the document flow, but it does not render any block or inline element on the canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height utility that runs before layout; it does not participate in the visible layout or affect the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component also has no direct visual footprint when in an idle or successful state; instead, it orchestrates when loading or error states are shown. Conceptually, it wraps the main product detail content (breadcrumb, overview hero, specs, offers, and quote form). The area it controls spans the full content container width, starting just below the global header and announcement bar, extending down to the end of the product detail content. When the loader is active, it allows the loading/error state component to occupy the hero area where the product summary and CTA would normally appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; it defines which child layout (loading/error vs. loaded state) is displayed but itself does not generate visible spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation appears at the top of the product page content, directly below the site header and announcement bar, inside the centered content container. It forms a shallow horizontal row that spans nearly 100% of the container width but is visually anchored on the left, with the breadcrumb text aligned to the left edge of the grid. The row has about 16–24px of vertical padding above and below, creating a light, airy strip before the more substantial hero section. Below the breadcrumbs, there is an additional 16–24px of vertical space before the product header summary and CTA panel begin, keeping the page feeling open and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow; it scrolls with the page and aligns to the same centered content container as the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product header summary forms the left column of the main hero-like overview section. This section begins approximately 16–24px below the breadcrumb row and spans the full width of the page background, though the content is constrained to a centered container. Within that container, the summary occupies roughly 7 of 12 columns (about 55–60% of the width) on desktop and tablet, starting from the left edge of the grid. It includes a large product title at the top, followed by a short, readable paragraph and a compact list of key specifications or badges, each separated vertically by 8–16px to maintain low-to-moderate density. The summary column has internal padding of about 24–32px and a right margin/gutter of 24–32px separating it from the CTA panel on the right. On smaller screens (mobile), this component expands to full width, stacked above the installation CTA panel with 16–24px vertical space between them, preserving the same breathing room and typographic hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-aligned column in a two-column responsive grid. It collapses to full-width above the CTA on narrow viewports while keeping consistent horizontal padding from the container edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installation CTA panel is the right-hand column of the hero overview section and serves as a co-primary focal point with the product summary. On desktop and larger tablets, it occupies roughly 5 of 12 columns (about 40–45% of the container width), aligned to the right with a clear gutter separating it from the summary on the left. It is rendered as a prominent, rounded card with a subtle shadow or solid accent background color that stands out against the white canvas, echoing the bold illustration card in the mockup. The card’s top edge aligns vertically with the product-header-summary title, and it extends downward to fit its content: typically a concise heading (e.g., “Install this unit”), a short line of reassurance text, pricing/high-level info, and one or two large, rounded buttons (e.g., “Schedule Installation” and “Request Quote”), stacked or side-by-side with 8–16px spacing. Internal padding is in the 24–32px range. On mobile, this CTA panel stacks directly below the product summary, expands to full width, and remains visually strong with extra top margin (~24px) to avoid crowding. It maintains consistent left/right margins aligned with the summary text for a cohesive look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right column of the hero section. On wide screens, it stays aligned to the right within the grid; on smaller screens, it reflows to a full-width block below the summary. Optionally can be made sticky within the viewport on tall screens, but its default behavior is to scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the left side of the second major content band, directly below the hero section containing the product summary and CTA. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px separating this band from the one above, maintaining the site’s section rhythm. Inside the centered content container, the efficiency and features area occupies roughly 7 of 12 columns (about 55–60% width) starting at the left grid edge, mirroring the width of the product summary above. At the top, a clear section heading (e.g., “Efficiency & Features”) is aligned left, with 16–24px of space before detailed elements like SEER/HSPF ratings, energy star badges, and a short list of feature bullets. Elements inside are spaced by 8–16px vertically, keeping content readable and uncluttered. This column’s right side sits about 24–32px away from the product-offers-panel, forming a consistent gutter. On mobile, this component becomes a full-width block, stacked above the offers panel with about 24px of vertical space in between.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static left column in a two-column section. It maintains alignment with the left edge of the container and reflows to full-width when the viewport becomes too narrow for two columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product offers panel occupies the right side of the same section as the efficiency and features content. On desktop, it spans roughly 5 of 12 columns (about 40–45% of the container width) aligned to the right, starting just after a 24–32px gutter from the left column. Visually, it is a tidy, card-like block with rounded corners and subtle background differentiation (soft tint or light border) that suggests secondary but valuable information, such as promotions, rebates, or financing offers. The card’s top edge aligns with the heading of the efficiency section to reinforce a shared row. Inside, the content is low-density: a short title (e.g., “Current Offers”), a small list of 2–4 offers each separated by 8–12px, and possibly a small link or button such as “See details.” Internal padding is around 16–24px. On mobile, this panel becomes full-width and stacks immediately below the efficiency/features block, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, while staying centered within the content container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right column card that scrolls with the page. It aligns with the right side of the grid on larger screens and reflows to a full-width block beneath the efficiency section on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The installation quote form lives in its own full-width section that begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom of the efficiency/offers band. The section background remains white for continuity with the rest of the page, but the form is visually grouped inside the centered content container with a clear heading (e.g., “Request an Installation Quote”) aligned to the left. Directly beneath the heading, and above the first form field, there is reserved space for any status messages component so that feedback is seen immediately. The form fields are arranged in a simple, approachable layout: on desktop, fields may be placed in one or two columns within the same container width, but with generous horizontal padding from the container edges and 12–16px vertical spacing between fields. The form overall occupies roughly 60–80% of the container width and is centered horizontally, leaving comfortable white margins on both sides to maintain low content density. At the bottom, large, rounded primary and secondary buttons span about 40–60% of the form width (or full width on mobile), with 16px spacing between them. On mobile, the form becomes a single-column stack, using the full container width with 16–24px side padding and clear vertical spacing to avoid feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section within the content container that scrolls normally. It uses an internal grid to manage field alignment and gracefully collapses to a single-column form layout on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-form-status-messages component appears inside the same section as the installation quote form, anchored at the top of the form area immediately below the section heading and above the first input field. It spans nearly the full width of the form (matching its inner content width) and appears as a horizontal banner or card with subtle background color changes (e.g., soft green for success, amber or red for warnings/errors) and rounded corners, consistent with the site’s playful, friendly aesthetic. Vertical padding inside the banner is about 12–16px, and there is 8–12px of space between the banner and the heading above, and another 12–16px between the banner and the form fields below. When no messages are present, this space collapses, allowing the form fields to shift upward so the section remains compact and clean.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block nested within the quote form section. It occupies its own row above the form fields and scrolls with the page; it appears and disappears dynamically without altering the overall section’s alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this component temporarily replaces the main product content area controlled by the loader. During loading, it occupies the same general vertical slice as the hero overview section (product summary + CTA), spanning the full width of the content container starting just below the breadcrumb row. It can present skeleton placeholders: a wide, left-aligned rectangle mimicking the product title (about 50–60% width), several shorter lines for summary text, and a right-aligned rectangular block hinting at the CTA panel’s size—all separated by 16–24px of vertical and horizontal space to mimic the final layout. In an error state, it renders a centered error card or banner across most of the container width, with a short message and a retry button, placed with ample padding (24–32px inside the card and 32–48px top and bottom) so it feels consistent with the spacious design. When this component is visible, the underlying product summary, specifications, offers, and form are hidden or visually pushed below, ensuring the loading/error state is the primary focus for that region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional full-width block that occupies the main content area beneath the breadcrumb when active. It scrolls like normal content but replaces or overlays the hero section until data has successfully loaded.

name: Maintenance Plans
title: Maintenance Plans
description: List and filter HVAC maintenance plans by coverage, billing term, price range, and sorting, then navigate to a specific plan detail for enrollment.

core_features: Initialize page state from URL parameters (coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, page) and keep the UI in sync with the URL.
Load available filter and sort options for maintenance plans using getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions and bind them to coverage, billing term, price range, and sort controls.

List maintenance plans using getMaintenancePlans with the current filters and sortBy parameters, showing key fields such as name, coverageType, billingTerm, pricePerYear, and visitsPerYear when available.

Allow users to filter maintenance plans by coverage (e.g., plans that include both heating and cooling, including whole-home options) via coverageType options returned from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions.
Allow users to filter maintenance plans by billing term via billingTerm options returned from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions (e.g., annual or monthly).
Allow users to filter maintenance plans by annual price range using priceRanges returned from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions and mapped to priceRangeId URL parameter.
Allow users to sort maintenance plans using sortOptions returned from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions (e.g., price_low_to_high) so cheaper eligible plans can be shown first.
Support pagination of maintenance plans controlled via the page URL parameter, using the total count returned by getMaintenancePlans.
Enable navigation to a maintenance plan detail page (plan_detail.html) by clicking a plan name or a dedicated View Details link, passing the selected planId via URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on Maintenance Plans from navigation or homepage
1. User navigates to plans.html, optionally with pre-set query parameters (e.g., plans.html?coverageType=heating_cooling&billingTerm=annual&sortBy=price_low_to_high).
2. On page load, the controller component parses coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, and page from the URL query string.
3. The page calls getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions to retrieve coverageTypes, billingTerms, priceRanges, and sortOptions.
4. The filter bar initializes its controls to match the URL parameters where values exist; otherwise the controls remain in their default 'no filter' state.
5. The page calls getMaintenancePlans with a filters object derived from the URL parameters and the sortBy value.
6. The plans list displays the returned plans and a results summary (e.g., count of plans) based on getMaintenancePlans.total.
7. The user can then refine filters, change sorting, or navigate to a plan detail.

Workflow 2: Finding an annual whole-home heating and cooling plan
1. User lands on plans.html, possibly without any query parameters.
2. getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions is called and coverageTypes and billingTerms are bound to the filter controls.
3. User selects a coverageType option representing combined heating and cooling coverage (for example, a value that corresponds to whole_home_heating_cooling or similar, as provided by coverageTypes).
4. User selects a billingTerm option corresponding to an annual plan from the billingTerms options.
5. Optionally, user selects a price range from priceRanges and a sort option (such as a lower-price-first option) from sortOptions.
6. Each time the user changes a control, the controller updates the URL query parameters (coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, page) and resets page to the first page.
7. The controller calls getMaintenancePlans with the updated filters and sortBy.
8. The plans list updates to show only plans that match the selected coverageType and billingTerm (and price range, if selected), ordered by the chosen sort option.
9. User scans the list and clicks the plan name or View Details link of the desired plan.
10. Browser navigates to plan_detail.html?planId=<selected_plan_id>.

Workflow 3: Paging through results and returning to the list from a detail page
1. User has applied some filters and sorting, and is currently on a certain page of results (e.g., page=2), reflected in the URL.
2. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number).
3. The controller updates the page URL parameter to the selected value, leaves other parameters unchanged, and calls getMaintenancePlans with the updated filters including the new page number.
4. The plans list updates to show the plans corresponding to that page.
5. User clicks a plan name to open plan_detail.html?planId=<id>. The browser navigates away.
6. When the user clicks the browser back button from the detail page, they return to plans.html with the previous URL parameters intact.
7. On re-load, the controller parses the URL (including page) and calls getMaintenancePlans with those filters; the list, filters, sort selection, and current page state are restored without additional user input.
interactions: On initial page load, parse URL search parameters (coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, page), call getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions, then call getMaintenancePlans with filters and sortBy based on these parameters.

When the coverage filter control value changes, update the coverageType parameter in the URL (removing it if the user clears the filter), reset page to the first page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans with the updated filters.
When the billing term filter control value changes, update the billingTerm parameter in the URL, reset page to the first page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans.
When the price range filter control value changes, update the priceRangeId parameter in the URL (or remove it if cleared), reset page to the first page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans.
When the sort control value changes, update the sortBy parameter in the URL, reset page to the first page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans using the newly selected sort option.

When a pagination control is used (e.g., next, previous, or numeric page button), update the page parameter in the URL and call getMaintenancePlans with the same filter and sortBy settings but the new page value.
When the user clicks on a maintenance plan name or its View Details link, read that plan's id and navigate the browser to plan_detail.html?planId=<id> without modifying other parameters.

When the user triggers a 'Clear all filters' action (if implemented), remove coverageType, billingTerm, and priceRangeId from the URL query string, reset sortBy and page as appropriate, and re-call getMaintenancePlans with no filters (or with backend-defined defaults).

If getMaintenancePlans returns zero results for the current filters, display a functional 'no results' state and allow the user to adjust or clear filters; no hardcoded suggestions are used, only the active filter options from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters and API responses, without use of localStorage or other client persistence. On load, the controller reads coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, and page from the URL. If a parameter is absent, it is treated as undefined (no filter or default behavior). These values are used to construct a filters object passed to getMaintenancePlans, together with sortBy. Changes in any filter or sort control update the URL via the History API or a full navigation, ensuring the visible query string always reflects the active state. When a filter changes, the page parameter is reset to the first page (implementation-defined, typically page=1 or omitted) to avoid landing on an out-of-range page. When pagination controls are used, only the page parameter is changed, leaving other parameters intact. The total result count returned by getMaintenancePlans, plus any implicit backend pagination settings, determines how many pages exist; the pagination component uses this to determine which page values are valid. Because the URL is the source of truth, using browser navigation (e.g., back button) restores previous filter, sort, and pagination states simply by re-reading the query parameters and re-invoking getMaintenancePlans. Navigation from this page to plan_detail.html includes only the selected planId in the target URL; returning to the list via the browser back button restores the prior list state.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter parsing, filter/sort/pagination state, and orchestrates child components for the Maintenance Plans listing.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL parameters (coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, page). Call getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions with no parameters. After filter options are loaded, call getMaintenancePlans with filters derived from URL parameters and sortBy. Provide results and filter options to child components.

onFilterChange: Called by filter components when coverageType, billingTerm, or priceRangeId changes. Update the corresponding URL parameters (adding, replacing, or removing them), reset the page parameter to the initial page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans with the updated filters and current or default sortBy.
onSortChange: Called by sort control when the user selects a new sort option. Update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page to the initial page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans.

onPageChange: Called by pagination component when the user chooses a new page. Update the page URL parameter and re-call getMaintenancePlans with the unchanged filters and sortBy but the new page value.
onPlanSelect: Called when a user clicks on a plan name or View Details in the list. Read the selected MaintenancePlan.id and navigate the browser to plan_detail.html?planId=<id>.

onClearAllFilters: Optional handler triggered by a clear/reset UI action. Remove coverageType, billingTerm, and priceRangeId from the URL, optionally reset sortBy to a default, reset page to the initial page, and re-call getMaintenancePlans with minimal or no filters.

components.functionality: Displays and manages the main filter and sort controls for maintenance plans, including coverage type, billing term, price range, and sort selection.
event_handlers: onCoverageTypeChange: Triggered when the user selects or clears a coverage option. Sends the selected coverageType value (or undefined) to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onFilterChange.
onBillingTermChange: Triggered when the user selects or clears a billing term. Sends the selected billingTerm value (or undefined) to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onFilterChange.
onPriceRangeChange: Triggered when the user selects or clears a price range. Sends the selected priceRangeId (or undefined) to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onFilterChange.
onSortOptionChange: Triggered when the user selects a different sort option from sortOptions. Sends the selected sortBy value to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onSortChange.
onClearAllClick: Optional button that, when clicked, calls maintenance-plans-page-controller.onClearAllFilters to remove all filters at once.

components.functionality: Shows a summary of the current results (e.g., count of plans returned) based on the latest getMaintenancePlans response and the active filter/sort state.
event_handlers: onStateUpdate: Invoked by the page controller whenever new getMaintenancePlans results are received or URL parameters change. The component updates the visible count and any textual indication of active filters based solely on current data and parameters.

components.functionality: Renders the list of maintenance plans returned by getMaintenancePlans and exposes click targets that navigate to the corresponding plan detail pages.
event_handlers: onPlanNameClick: Triggered when a user clicks a plan's name. Emits the selected MaintenancePlan.id to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onPlanSelect.
onViewDetailsClick: Triggered when a user clicks a dedicated View Details control on a plan row/card. Emits the selected MaintenancePlan.id to maintenance-plans-page-controller.onPlanSelect.

onPlansDataUpdate: Called by the page controller when a new getMaintenancePlans response is available. Re-renders the list based on the updated plans array without assuming any static ordering or content.

components.functionality: Provides pagination controls to move between pages of maintenance plans based on the total count returned from getMaintenancePlans and the current page URL parameter.
event_handlers: onPageNumberClick: Triggered when the user selects a specific page. Calculates the new page value and calls maintenance-plans-page-controller.onPageChange with that value.

onNextPageClick: Triggered when the user clicks a 'next' control. Increments the current page value if the next page is within the valid range derived from getMaintenancePlans.total, then calls maintenance-plans-page-controller.onPageChange.

onPreviousPageClick: Triggered when the user clicks a 'previous' control. Decrements the current page value if greater than the initial page, then calls maintenance-plans-page-controller.onPageChange.
onDataUpdate: Triggered when getMaintenancePlans.total or the current page changes. Recomputes which pagination controls are enabled or visible based on available pages.

components.functionality: Abstracts reading and writing of URL query parameters for the Maintenance Plans page to keep filters, sorting, and pagination synchronized with browser navigation.
event_handlers: onReadInitialState: Called by maintenance-plans-page-controller.onInit to read coverageType, billingTerm, priceRangeId, sortBy, and page from the current window.location.search and return them in a structured state object.

onUpdateFilters: Called when filters change. Updates coverageType, billingTerm, and priceRangeId in the URL, removes parameters when filters are cleared, resets or updates page, and either pushes or replaces the history entry.
onUpdateSort: Called when sortBy changes. Updates the sortBy parameter and resets page in the URL, then notifies the page controller.
onUpdatePage: Called when the page number changes. Updates the page parameter only and notifies the page controller.

onPopState: Listens for browser back/forward navigation events. When triggered, re-parses the URL parameters and notifies maintenance-plans-page-controller so it can re-call getMaintenancePlans with the restored state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup. Above this page’s components, the global cyan announcement bar and white header/nav remain unchanged. Immediately below that, the Maintenance Plans main area behaves like the About section: a full-width white section with a centered content container. Inside that container, the page-controller uses a two-column hero-style layout (left text, right supporting content), followed by a shallow, full-width filter toolbar band, then a slim results summary row, the vertical plans list, and finally pagination controls at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup. Above this page’s components, the global cyan announcement bar and white header/nav remain unchanged. Immediately below that, the Maintenance Plans main area behaves like the About section: a full-width white section with a centered content container. Inside that container, the page-controller uses a two-column hero-style layout (left text, right supporting content), followed by a shallow, full-width filter toolbar band, then a slim results summary row, the vertical plans list, and finally pagination controls at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered content container (roughly 1040–1200px wide) sits on a white background. The page-controller hero section gets generous vertical padding (about 48–64px top and bottom), making it visually substantial. The filter bar beneath it is a shallower horizontal band (about 24–32px vertical padding) that spans the full container width but feels compact. The results summary is a slim line with small margins. The plans list occupies most of the vertical space, with roomy gaps (24–32px) between cards. Pagination has 32–48px of breathing room above and below to clearly separate it from the list and any footer content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low to moderate. The page-controller focuses on a strong title and 1–2 short lines of copy with plenty of white space around. The filter bar groups controls but avoids clutter, keeping 16–24px horizontal gaps between filter elements. The results summary is a single, concise line. Plan cards in the list use generous internal padding (16–24px) and spacing between them so the page feels light and approachable rather than crowded. Typography remains large and readable, with comfortable line-height and minimal fine print.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow echoes the mockup’s loose Z-pattern. The user’s eye lands first on the page-controller’s left-aligned heading and description, then moves right to any key helper element (such as a plan highlight or simple icon). It then drops down to the filter bar, scanning the primary filter controls from left to right, ending on a right-aligned sort or reset CTA. From there, the eye moves slightly down-left to the results summary line, then straight down through the vertical stack of plan cards. Pagination centered at the bottom provides a clear final focal point, with the persistent chat widget (outside this spec) anchoring the lower-left as a subtle tertiary element.

layout.overall_description: Within the existing site frame of a cyan announcement strip and white navigation header, the Maintenance Plans page opens into a bright, spacious white section that mirrors the About layout. A centered content container sits on a 12-column grid. At the top of this container, a hero-like page-controller spans the full width, split into two columns: a left block holding the bold “Maintenance Plans” title and short explainer copy, and a right block reserved for a simple, friendly supporting element (such as an icon or quick plan highlight) with plenty of breathing room. Below this hero, a shallow, tinted filter bar stretches horizontally across the container, with grouped filters on the left and a sort control and reset action towards the right, forming a clear control band. Just beneath it, a single-line results summary is left-aligned and unobtrusive, anchoring the start of the listing. The main body is a vertical list of generous, rounded plan cards that fill most of the content width and are separated by ample white space, reinforcing the approachable, marketing-style feel. At the bottom of the list, centered pagination controls sit on their own island of space, clearly separated from both the last card and any footer, providing a clean end to the scroll. The whole composition reads as a series of simple, stacked sections with low visual noise, guiding the user smoothly from understanding the page, through filtering options, into comparing plans, and finally advancing through additional pages of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the first main section of the Maintenance Plans content area, directly beneath the global header. It sits within a full-width white band that matches the About section’s treatment, with the content constrained to a centered container about 80–90% of the viewport width (e.g., max 1040–1200px). Inside this container, it uses a two-column layout aligned to a 12-column grid: the left column spans roughly 5/12 of the width and the right column spans about 7/12. In the left 5/12, place the page title (e.g., “Maintenance Plans”) at the top left with a large, bold type size, followed by a short, 1–2 line descriptive paragraph beneath it, left-aligned and with a 16–24px gap below the heading. The right 7/12 column aligns on the same vertical baseline but is more open, with a single, visually light element centered vertically within the column—this could be a simple illustration, a badge like “Most popular in your area,” or a summarized key benefit. Plenty of negative space surrounds this right-hand content so it feels co-primary but not crowded. The section has generous vertical padding—approximately 48–64px on top and bottom—keeping the overall density low and establishing a calm, marketing-style introduction to the listing and filters underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float. The two columns collapse into a single column on small screens, with the title and description stacking on top and the supporting visual or highlight block stacking below, maintaining 24–32px vertical gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is placed immediately below the page-controller section, with a clear visual separation: about 24–32px of vertical gap from the hero content above. It appears as a shallow, full-width horizontal band inside the same centered content container, optionally using a very light tinted background or a subtle card-style outline to echo the strong cyan announcement bar while staying softer. Vertically, it uses around 16–20px padding top and bottom, making it noticeably slimmer than the hero but still comfortable. Within the 12-column grid, filter controls are arranged in a left-to-right flow: the left two-thirds (approximately 7–8/12) are used for primary filters like coverage type and billing term, aligned to the left edge, each control separated by 16–24px horizontally. The right third (about 4–5/12) is right-aligned and holds secondary controls like price range and a sort dropdown, plus a compact “Clear all” or “Reset filters” textual CTA near the far right, visually mirroring the top-right CTA behavior from the navigation. Controls are aligned vertically in a single row on larger screens; on narrower screens they may wrap to a second line while maintaining 8–16px vertical spacing between wrapped rows. The overall effect is a tidy, approachable toolbar that visually anchors the top of the plans list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky toolbar within the viewport: once the user scrolls past it, it pins to the top of the viewport, sitting directly beneath the global header/announcement bar. This ensures filters remain accessible while scrolling through the list. On mobile, the sticky behavior persists, but controls may compress into fewer visible items or a collapsible group while maintaining the same horizontal alignment logic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary sits directly beneath the filter bar, sharing the same white content background and container width. It is a slim horizontal row with a relatively small vertical footprint—about 8–12px margin above and 8–16px margin below. The summary text is left-aligned, beginning at the same left margin as the page title and filters, and typically reads as a single line (e.g., “Showing 6 plans • Sorted by Price (low to high)”). The line height is generous, so it doesn’t feel compressed, but the component itself remains visually understated. On larger screens, the right side of this row (toward the far right of the container) may optionally contain a subtle secondary control such as a list/grid view toggle, but the primary emphasis is on the concise summary at the left. The narrow vertical spacing above and below the summary makes it feel like a label that introduces the upcoming list rather than a standalone section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, scrolling away with the page. On smaller screens where text might wrap to a second line, the component simply grows vertically, maintaining 8–12px padding from adjacent elements to preserve readability and consistent rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans list is the dominant central content block and begins immediately below the results summary, with about 16–24px of vertical gap from the summary line to the first plan card. It occupies the full width of the centered content container, aligned to the same left and right margins as the header and filters. Inside, individual plan cards are stacked vertically in a single column, each card spanning roughly 100% of the container width on small and medium screens and around 10–12/12 of the grid on larger screens (with any additional width simply becoming outer margin). Each card uses a white background, slightly rounded corners, and gentle shadow or border consistent with the brand’s soft, friendly style. Card interiors have 16–24px padding on all sides. Vertically, there is generous space—about 24–32px—between cards, so they feel like distinct, tappable objects rather than a dense table. Plan titles, key benefits, pricing, and CTAs inside each card are arranged in a left-to-right hierarchy that respects the same Z-pattern: plan name and key tag on the upper left, price and a primary “Enroll” or “View details” button aligned toward the right. On narrow screens, these elements stack vertically, maintaining 8–16px spacing between text blocks and buttons. The list continues down the page until the current page’s set of plans is exhausted, leading naturally into the pagination component below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable flow. The list simply extends as long as needed for the current page of results. It responds fluidly to container width: cards shrink and then stack inner content as the viewport narrows, while outer margins and inter-card spacing remain consistent with the spacing scale.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the plans list section, providing a visual endpoint to the content. There is a clear vertical gap of about 32–48px between the last plan card and the pagination row, ensuring the controls do not feel glued to the last card. The pagination row is centered horizontally within the content container and is fairly compact in height—roughly 16–24px padding above and below the controls themselves. Page numbers, next/previous arrows, or a load-more button are arranged in a single horizontal line, with equal spacing between individual items and a slightly stronger visual emphasis on the current page indicator. The controls are kept simple and friendly, in line with the brand: rounded pills for numbers or buttons, with adequate whitespace around them. This row sits above any site footer or global bottom content, with at least 32–48px of additional whitespace below it so it doesn’t collide visually with footer elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered within the main scroll, not sticky. On smaller screens, if there are multiple numbered pages, the pagination elements may wrap or compress to a minimal pattern (e.g., previous/next plus current page) while remaining horizontally centered and maintaining at least 8–12px padding around tap targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is a non-visual, behavioral layer tied to the Maintenance Plans page rather than a rendered UI control. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, listening to changes from the page-controller, filter bar, results summary (where relevant), plans list interactions, and pagination. It ensures that selections such as coverage filters, billing term, price range, sort order, and current page are reflected in the browser’s URL (e.g., query parameters) and that reloading or sharing the URL rehydrates the UI into the same state. It has no visible footprint, takes up no layout space, and does not affect the visual arrangement of elements, but it guarantees consistency between the layout’s interactive components and the URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered and effectively fixed at the page scope. It attaches to the window and routing context rather than occupying physical layout space, updating state as users interact with filters, list items, and pagination without shifting or resizing any visible components.

name: Maintenance Plan Detail
title: Maintenance Plan Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected HVAC maintenance plan and allows eligible plans to be added to the online cart.

core_features: Load maintenance plan details based on the planId URL parameter by calling getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId).
Display key plan information: name, coverage label, billing term label, annual price (pricePerYear), visits per year, included checks, and whether heating and cooling are included.
Highlight the billing term as annual using billingTermLabel and clearly show the yearly price from plan.pricePerYear so users can confirm the annual cost.
Show eligibility for online purchase based on isEligibleForOnlinePurchase, enabling or disabling the enrollment button accordingly.
Allow the user to add the displayed maintenance plan to the cart via addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId, quantity) with a clear 'Add Plan to Cart' / 'Enroll Now' action.

After a successful add-to-cart operation, show a confirmation state using the returned message and cartSummary, and provide navigation actions to either view the cart (cart.html) or stay on the plan detail page.
Display full terms, limitations, and disclaimers using plan.terms and disclaimers, with optional expand/collapse behavior for long content.
Offer navigation back to the list of plans (plans.html) and allow switching to related plans using relatedPlans from getMaintenancePlanDetails.
user_workflows: Viewing a maintenance plan: 1) User navigates to plan_detail.html?planId=... from plans.html or index.html. 2) On page load, the controller reads the planId URL parameter. 3) The page calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId). 4) While loading, a loading state is shown in place of the plan content. 5) On success, the page displays plan name, coverageLabel, billingTermLabel, pricePerYear, includesHeating/includesCooling indicators, visitsPerYear, includedChecks, terms, and disclaimers. 6) The user can review the coverage information, visit frequency, and checks included before deciding to enroll.

Enrolling in a maintenance plan and adding it to the cart: 1) On the loaded plan detail, if isEligibleForOnlinePurchase is true, the 'Enroll Now' (add-to-cart) button is enabled with a default quantity of 1. 2) The user clicks the button. 3) The page triggers addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId, quantity). 4) While the request is in progress, the button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions and a loading state is shown for the button area. 5) On success, the component reads the response (success, message, cartId, cartItemId, cartSummary). 6) A success message is displayed along with a simple cart summary (e.g., total items and total cost from cartSummary). 7) The user can click a link or button to go to cart.html or dismiss the prompt to remain on plan_detail.html. 8) On failure (success=false), an error message is displayed and the button is re-enabled.
