Handling ineligible plans: 1) User loads plan_detail.html?planId=... as usual. 2) getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId) returns isEligibleForOnlinePurchase=false. 3) The add-to-cart/enroll button is not shown or is disabled with an informative message indicating online purchase is unavailable. 4) The user can still view coverage details, terms, disclaimers, and navigate back to plans.html or open related plans.

Navigating between related plans: 1) On a loaded plan, the page displays a list of relatedPlans. 2) The user clicks a related plan item. 3) The browser navigates to plan_detail.html?planId={relatedPlanId}. 4) The page-init controller detects the new planId, calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(relatedPlanId), and updates the content accordingly without relying on any stored state.

Returning to plan list and proceeding to cart: 1) At any time, the user can click 'Back to all plans' to navigate to plans.html (no extra parameters required). 2) After successfully adding a plan to cart, the user can click a 'View Cart' action which navigates to cart.html for checkout.
interactions: On page load, read the planId query parameter from the URL. If it is missing or empty, show an error state and do not call getMaintenancePlanDetails.
On page load when planId is valid, call getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId) and show a loading indicator until the response is received or an error occurs.
If getMaintenancePlanDetails returns an error (network or API-level), show an error message with a retry action that re-calls getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId).
When getMaintenancePlanDetails returns successfully, bind the returned plan, coverageLabel, billingTermLabel, includedChecks, disclaimers, and relatedPlans to their respective components.
If isEligibleForOnlinePurchase is false, disable or hide the add-to-cart/enroll button and show a message indicating that this plan cannot be purchased online.

When the user clicks 'Enroll Now' (or equivalent add-to-cart button), call addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId, quantity) with quantity defaulting to 1 (editable if the design allows), and disable the button while awaiting the response.

If addMaintenancePlanToCart returns success=true, show the returned message, display cartSummary (such as item count and totals), and show options to: go to cart.html, or close the confirmation while staying on the page.
If addMaintenancePlanToCart returns success=false, show the returned message as an error and re-enable the button so the user can try again.
When the user clicks a related plan link, update the URL to plan_detail.html?planId={relatedPlan.id} and trigger the same initial data-load logic for the new planId.
When the user clicks 'Back to all plans', navigate to plans.html.
When the user clicks to expand or collapse terms/disclaimers, toggle the visibility of the detailed text without additional API calls.
When the browser back/forward buttons are used, the controller listens for URL changes and, if planId changed, re-runs getMaintenancePlanDetails with the new planId to keep the view in sync.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the planId URL query parameter (plan_detail.html?planId=...). On initial load, the controller reads planId: if present and non-empty, it triggers getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId) and stores the response in in-memory state for this page instance (plan data, eligibility, relatedPlans, etc.). If planId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state prompting the user to navigate back to plans.html. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage; all state is ephemeral and tied to the current page instance and URL. When the user clicks a related plan or navigates directly with another planId in the URL, the browser loads plan_detail.html with the new planId, or the controller detects the URL change (e.g., via history/navigation events), re-calls getMaintenancePlanDetails with the new planId, and replaces all bound data. After addMaintenancePlanToCart is called, the page does not need to encode cart information into the URL; navigation to cart.html does not carry query parameters in this design. Any confirmation or error messages from addMaintenancePlanToCart live only in current in-memory state and disappear when the user navigates away or reloads the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the planId from the URL, calling getMaintenancePlanDetails(planId), managing loading and error states, and distributing the loaded data to child components.

Shows high-level plan information including plan name, coverageLabel, billingTermLabel, and the annual price from plan.pricePerYear. Emphasizes that billing is annual and makes the yearly price clear for user verification.

Displays the plan's coverage details, such as coverageType, includesHeating, includesCooling, and visitsPerYear, in a concise summary so users can see which systems are covered and how often visits occur.
Lists the includedChecks items returned by getMaintenancePlanDetails so users can see what tasks are performed during maintenance visits.
Indicates whether the plan is eligible for online purchase using isEligibleForOnlinePurchase and optionally explains when online enrollment is not available.

Allows the user to enroll in the maintenance plan by adding it to the cart. Handles quantity selection (if needed), calls addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId, quantity) on submit, and manages loading, success, and error states.
Displays the result of the addMaintenancePlanToCart operation, showing success or error messages and a simple summary of the cart using the cartSummary object returned by the interface.

Shows plan.terms and the disclaimers string from getMaintenancePlanDetails. Supports expand/collapse to handle longer content and includes a link or anchor for users who need to reference detailed policy information.
Displays relatedPlans from getMaintenancePlanDetails as navigable items. Each item links to plan_detail.html with that plan's id in the planId URL parameter to allow quick comparison between plans.
Provides navigation links to go back to plans.html and optionally to view the cart at cart.html after adding a plan.

Displays loading indicators while getMaintenancePlanDetails is in progress and user-friendly error messages when API calls fail or when planId is missing or invalid. Offers a retry action that reruns getMaintenancePlanDetails with the current planId.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header/navigation, the page uses a centered content container on a white background. The main body follows a top-to-bottom flow made of clear, full-width sections: a slim plans navigation row, a visually strong hero-style plan detail section using a two-column split (left: plan info and add-to-cart, right: coverage summary), followed by stacked, full-width sections for included checks, terms/disclaimers, and related plans. A notification layer for add-to-cart feedback and a loading/error overlay sit above this flow when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header/navigation, the page uses a centered content container on a white background. The main body follows a top-to-bottom flow made of clear, full-width sections: a slim plans navigation row, a visually strong hero-style plan detail section using a two-column split (left: plan info and add-to-cart, right: coverage summary), followed by stacked, full-width sections for included checks, terms/disclaimers, and related plans. A notification layer for add-to-cart feedback and a loading/error overlay sit above this flow when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 1080–1200px) and is centered. The hero section for the plan detail uses a 12-column-like split: approximately 5/12 of the width for the left text/form column and 7/12 for the right coverage summary. Vertical padding between major sections follows the mockup’s spacious rhythm: roughly 48–64px top and bottom per section, with 16–24px between headings, text blocks, and lists. The notification and loading/error layers are shallow but visually strong bars or cards, sitting near the top of the content area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low to moderate: main sections avoid walls of text, instead favoring short paragraphs, key-value blocks, and bullet lists. Generous whitespace separates each section, and elements within sections have comfortable breathing room (16–24px). The hero section is visually rich but not cramped, with the coverage summary card occupying ample space similar in visual weight to the left column. Fine-print content like terms is denser but still broken into readable chunks with clear headings.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s loose Z-pattern. The eye starts near the top-left with the plans navigation links, then moves right across any secondary links. It drops down to the left side of the hero section where the plan name, price, and eligibility indicator sit, then moves right to the larger coverage summary card. From there, the flow continues downward through the included checks section, then to the terms/disclaimers, and finally to the related plans at the bottom. The add-to-cart form and its primary CTA sit in the left hero column and are a key focal point, while add-to-cart feedback briefly appears in a top-centered notification that catches attention without feeling heavy. A global chat widget in the bottom-left remains a subtle, tertiary anchor and is not visually competed with by these components.

layout.overall_description: Under the existing cyan announcement bar and white header with logo/nav, the Maintenance Plan Detail page unfolds within a centered, white canvas. At the very top of the main content, a slim row of text-like navigation links helps users move between plans or back to the plans list. Beneath this, a hero-style section fills much of the first viewport, split into two columns: on the left, the plan title, pricing, eligibility indicator, and a rounded add-to-cart card form; on the right, a large, playful coverage summary card that balances the left column in visual weight, echoing the mockup’s About section. The layout feels open, with generous vertical padding framing this area. Scrolling down, the content snaps back to full-width, single-column sections: first a clean, well-spaced list of included checks, then a slightly denser but still legible terms and disclaimers block, and finally a roomy related plans strip with card-like options. A subtle notification bar can slide in just below the main header when the user adds a plan to the cart, and a full-page, lightly dimmed overlay appears in front of everything for loading or error states. The overall impression is friendly, local, and approachable, with playful accents and rounded CTAs sitting in a clear, breathable layout that makes complex plan details easy to digest.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller is not directly visible but governs the initial layout. It sits conceptually at the root of the main content area beneath the global header. When the page is first loaded or when the selected plan changes, it temporarily replaces the main content sections with the loading-and-error-state overlay. Once data is ready and no errors are present, it reveals the full vertical stack of sections (navigation links, hero section, checks list, terms, related plans) within the centered content container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural component that toggles visibility of other components; it causes the loading-and-error-state to cover the viewport and hides other content while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-detail-header sits near the top-left of the main hero section, within the centered content container and beneath the slim plans-navigation-links row. It occupies the top of the left column in a two-column split that spans the full width of the container. On desktop, this left column takes roughly 40–45% of the container width (about 5/12), starting aligned with the same left margin as the site logo above. The header includes the plan name as a bold, large type element, with a short descriptive line below; there is 16–24px of vertical spacing between these pieces. The bottom of this header block sits about 16–24px above the add-plan-to-cart-form in the same column, creating a clean vertical stack. On narrower screens, the plan-detail-header shifts to full-width above the form and above whatever content follows, maintaining 24–32px of padding from the container’s top edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable hero section; on smaller viewports it reflows to full-width at the top of the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-coverage-summary is the primary element in the right column of the hero section, visually echoing the large illustration card from the mockup. On desktop, it takes roughly 55–60% of the container width (about 7/12), aligned to the right edge of the content container. Its top edge aligns roughly with the top of the plan-detail-header on the left, and it extends downward so that it visually balances the combined height of the header plus add-to-cart form on the left. The component is styled as a prominent card with rounded corners and generous internal padding (around 24–32px), surrounded by white space—at least 32px from the left column and 32–48px from the top and bottom of the hero section’s padding. Coverage items inside can be arranged in 2–3 columns on desktop while still preserving airy spacing between them. On mobile and tablet, this component drops below the left column content and becomes full-width, with 32–48px of vertical space separating it from the add-to-cart form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; its position is responsive, shifting from right column on larger screens to stacked full-width content below the left column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-included-checks-list begins a new full-width section directly beneath the hero section (plan-detail-header, add-plan-to-cart-form, plan-coverage-summary block). There is a clear vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the hero and the top of this section, preserving the mockup’s spacious section separation. Within the centered container, a section heading appears near the top-left with 16–24px margin below it. The checks themselves are organized in a lightly structured layout: on desktop, they may appear in two balanced columns of bullet points or checklist items across the 12-column grid, while still leaving at least 24px of horizontal space between columns. The overall width matches the content container (70–80% of viewport), leaving generous side margins. Vertical spacing between individual items is around 8–12px, keeping the list readable but not dense. On smaller screens, the list collapses to a single column with maintained 16–24px vertical spacing between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section within the scrollable page, stacking below the hero section and above the terms-and-disclaimers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-eligibility-indicator is a compact, visually distinct badge or label positioned close to the plan-detail-header in the left column of the hero section. On desktop, it sits either to the right of the plan title on the same line or directly beneath the title with about 8–12px vertical spacing. It aligns to the left edge of the header text, maintaining a tight relationship that visually communicates the eligibility status as a property of the plan. The badge uses a rounded, pill-like shape with a subtle background color (e.g., soft green for eligible, muted gray for ineligible), sized so that it does not dominate the heading but remains easily noticeable. A small amount of white space (16px) separates this indicator from the next block below (price or add-to-cart form) to keep the column airy. On mobile, the eligibility indicator remains directly adjacent to or just under the title, scaling down slightly so as not to crowd the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored within the left hero column as part of the header block; reflows naturally with text when the layout collapses to single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-plan-to-cart-form appears as a rounded, card-like block directly below the plan-detail-header (and eligibility indicator) in the left column of the hero section. It spans the full width of this left column (about 40–45% of the container on desktop), with generous internal padding of 16–24px on all sides and a subtle outline or shadow to differentiate it from the white background. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the header block and the top of the form, keeping the grouping visually clear. Inside the form, fields such as service address selectors, frequency options, or quantity controls are arranged vertically with 12–16px spacing. The primary CTA button sits at the bottom of the form, spanning close to the full width of the card, with a bold, rounded style consistent with the site’s bubbly CTAs. The bottom of this form sits roughly aligned with the mid-to-lower height of the plan-coverage-summary on the right, creating a balanced hero area. On mobile, the form becomes full-width and appears directly under the plan-detail-header and eligibility indicator, with 24–32px spacing above and below to maintain breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left hero column. Optionally, on very tall desktop viewports, the form can become sticky within its column, staying visible while the user begins to scroll, but it does not cover other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-feedback is a brief notification that appears over the main content area when a plan is successfully added to the cart or when an error occurs in the form submission. It is positioned just below the global white header band, horizontally centered within the viewport. The notification takes the form of a shallow bar or pill-like card that spans roughly 40–60% of the content width, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. It has rounded corners and a solid background color (e.g., cyan or green for success, warm red/orange for error) while preserving the playful aesthetic. There is about 16–24px of space between this bar and the top of the main content (plans-navigation-links) so it does not feel cramped against other elements. The rest of the page content shifts slightly downward if the notification is in-flow, or remains in place if the notification is overlaid with a subtle drop shadow. The notification auto-dismisses after a short time or when manually closed, reverting the page’s vertical spacing to its usual state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or inserts just below the fixed/sticky header at the top of the viewport. It is fixed-position while visible, staying in view as the user scrolls until it disappears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-terms-and-disclaimers section appears beneath the plan-included-checks-list as another full-width content block in the vertical stack. There is a distinct 48–64px gap above its heading to mark it as a new section. Within the centered content container, a clear heading is aligned to the left with 16–24px spacing to the body copy below. The text is more dense than the other sections but still broken into short paragraphs or bullet points, with roughly 12–16px spacing between lines or subsections. Margins on the left and right align with all other sections, ensuring a consistent column. Text remains within the same maximum width as the rest of the page to maintain readability, avoiding edge-to-edge long lines. Compared to the hero and checks sections, this area is visually quieter—minimal use of color, relying on typographic hierarchy to organize the content. The bottom of this section ends with 48–64px of padding before the related-plans-list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width content section that scrolls like normal text; it expands vertically as needed depending on the amount of legal copy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-plans-list sits near the bottom of the page as the final main section, below the plan-terms-and-disclaimers. A generous 48–64px top margin separates it from the terms, giving it the feel of a new, lighter area. Within the centered container, a friendly heading (e.g., 'You might also like') appears at the top-left, with 16–24px spacing before the list of related plan cards. On desktop, related plans appear as 2–3 horizontally arranged cards, each occupying roughly 3–4 columns of the implicit grid, with 24–32px of horizontal spacing between cards. Vertically, each card has 16–24px internal padding and rounded corners, echoing the add-to-cart form styling but with less visual weight. On smaller screens, these cards stack vertically with 16–24px spacing between them, or can be presented in a horizontally scrollable row while still maintaining generous padding. The section ends with 48–64px of bottom padding before the global footer, maintaining the overall spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the main content area; cards reflow responsively from multi-column layout on desktop to stacked or horizontally-scrollable layout on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans-navigation-links appear as a slim, subtle row at the very top of the main content area, directly under the global white header band and any add-to-cart-feedback notification. This row stretches the full width of the centered content container. Links such as 'Back to all plans' or other plan categories are left-aligned in a simple horizontal arrangement with 16–24px spacing between each link. The row itself is relatively shallow, with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the text, and a total of about 16–24px of space separating it from the hero section below. The styling is lightweight—text links possibly with small icons or chevrons—to keep the focus on the hero content while still providing orientation. On smaller screens, the links may wrap to a second line or collapse into a simple breadcrumb-style line, but they remain at the top of the stacking order for the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content, always at the top of the main content column beneath the global header; it does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-state component appears as a full-screen overlay that sits on top of all other page elements (including the hero, lists, and related plans) when triggered by the page-init-controller. The overlay covers the entire viewport with a translucent white or light-colored scrim, subtly dimming the underlying content while keeping the overall brand feel light and friendly. At the center of the screen, a compact card or panel contains either a playful loading indicator (e.g., simple spinner with brand colors) or a clear error message. This card is horizontally and vertically centered and sized to roughly 30–50% of the viewport width on desktop, with 24–32px internal padding and rounded corners. On mobile, it shrinks to about 80–90% of the viewport width but retains central alignment. When active, scrolling of the underlying content is disabled to keep the user’s focus on the state message. Once loading completes or the error is resolved/dismissed, the overlay fades out to reveal the standard vertical layout of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay covering the full viewport, sitting above all other components; it blocks interaction with underlying content while visible.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: The Cart page displays the user’s current cart contents (e.g., maintenance plans), allows quantity changes and item removal, shows an order summary (subtotal, tax, total), and provides navigation to checkout or back to maintenance plans.

core_features: Load the current cart contents and pricing summary on page load using getCartSummary.
Display each cart item with name, description, quantity, unit price, and line total.
Allow users to change the quantity of individual cart items using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the cart using removeCartItem.
Display an order summary section with subtotal, tax, total, and currency based on getCartSummary and interface responses.
Disable or adjust behaviors (e.g., checkout button) based on cart status (active vs non-active) returned by getCartSummary.
Provide a 'Proceed to Checkout' button that navigates to checkout.html, passing the current cartId via URL parameters when available.
Provide a 'Continue shopping' link that navigates to plans.html, optionally passing context via URL parameters (e.g., return source).
Handle and display error and success messages for quantity updates and item removal using messages returned from updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View current cart and summary
1. User navigates to cart.html (via header cart link or direct URL).
2. On page load, the controller component calls getCartSummary with no parameters.
3. The page enters a loading state until getCartSummary returns.
4. On success, the returned cartId, status, items, subtotal, tax, total, and currency are stored in page state.
5. Cart items list and order summary are rendered from the returned data.
6. If the items array is empty, an empty-cart state is shown and the checkout button is disabled.
7. If the cart status is not active, a message is shown and the checkout button is disabled.

Workflow: Update item quantity
1. User edits a quantity field for a specific item or uses plus/minus controls in a cart item row.
2. The quantity update control triggers an onQuantityChange event with cartItemId and the new quantity.
3. The controller calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).
4. While waiting, the specific item row and order summary show a pending state (e.g., disabled controls for that row).
5. On success, the response.cartSummary (items, subtotal, tax, total) replaces the current cart state.
6. The updated quantities, line totals, and order summary values are re-rendered.
7. On failure, an error message from response.message is shown, and the UI reverts to the last known valid quantity.

Workflow: Remove item from cart
1. User clicks a 'Remove' action for a given cart item.
2. A confirmation step may be shown (handled purely in the front-end state; no API calls yet).
3. On confirmation, the controller calls removeCartItem(cartItemId).
4. While waiting, the affected item row shows a pending/removing state.
5. On success, the response.cartSummary replaces the current cart state.
6. The removed item disappears from the list; subtotal, tax, and total are updated accordingly.
7. If the cart becomes empty, the empty-cart state appears and checkout is disabled.
8. On failure, the item remains visible and the page displays the error message from response.message.

Workflow: Proceed to checkout
1. User reviews items and order summary on cart.html.
2. If cart status is active and items.length > 0, the 'Proceed to Checkout' button is enabled.
3. On click, a navigation event builds the URL checkout.html with a cartId query parameter (e.g., checkout.html?cartId=<current cartId>), using the cartId from the latest cart state.
4. Browser navigates to checkout.html; no interfaces are called from cart.html for this navigation.

Workflow: Continue shopping for maintenance plans
1. User is on cart.html and wants to add more maintenance plans.
2. User clicks the 'Continue shopping' link.
3. The link navigates to plans.html with an optional query parameter indicating the return source (e.g., plans.html?from=cart) so that plans.html can optionally show a contextual message or return path.
4. No cart APIs are called during this navigation; the cart remains as last updated on the server.

Workflow: Handle invalid or non-active cart
1. getCartSummary returns a cart status that is not active (for example, checked_out or abandoned).
2. The controller sets an internal flag indicating the cart cannot be modified.
3. The page displays a message that the cart cannot currently be changed.
4. Item-level controls for quantity update and removal are disabled and do not call updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem.
5. The 'Proceed to Checkout' button is disabled.
6. The user may still use the 'Continue shopping' link to navigate to plans.html.
interactions: onPageLoad (Cart page): Triggered when cart.html is loaded. Calls getCartSummary, updates page state with cartId, status, items, subtotal, tax, total, and currency, and renders components accordingly.

onQuantityChange (per item): Triggered when the quantity input or plus/minus control changes. Calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity, then updates the cart state from the returned cartSummary.
onRemoveClick (per item): Triggered when the user confirms removal of a cart item. Calls removeCartItem with cartItemId, then updates the cart state from the returned cartSummary.
onProceedToCheckoutClick: Triggered when the 'Proceed to Checkout' button is clicked. If cart status is active and items exist, navigates to checkout.html, including cartId as a query parameter.
onContinueShoppingClick: Triggered when the 'Continue shopping' link is clicked. Navigates to plans.html, optionally including a from=cart query parameter.

onCartApiError: Triggered when any of the cart-related interfaces (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem) return success=false or fail. Displays an error message and keeps or reverts the current UI state as appropriate.

onStateRefreshAfterUrlChange: Triggered if the page is reloaded or accessed with different URL parameters (e.g., specific item highlight). Calls getCartSummary again to ensure displayed cart data is current.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Cart page does not require incoming URL parameters to function; it always loads the current cart using getCartSummary. However, optional URL parameters can influence how the page behaves without changing which interfaces are called:

- Optional parameter: highlightItemId
  - Usage: cart.html?highlightItemId=<cartItemId>
  - Behavior: After getCartSummary resolves, the controller checks whether any returned item.cartItemId matches highlightItemId. If found, the cart-items-list component may scroll to and visually highlight that item row using front-end state. No additional API calls are made based on this parameter.

- Optional parameter: messageType and messageCode
  - Usage: cart.html?messageType=info|error&messageCode=<code>
  - Behavior: The controller interprets these parameters to display a contextual message (for example, that the user was redirected from another page after an action). This does not change how getCartSummary is called or how cart modifications work.

- Optional parameter: returnTo
  - Usage: cart.html?returnTo=plans
  - Behavior: Used only to adjust the behavior or labeling of the 'Continue shopping' link (e.g., always send the user to plans.html, potentially with additional context). No interface behavior changes.

Internal page state (not in URL and not persisted via localStorage) tracks: loading status, current cart summary (cartId, status, items, subtotal, tax, total, currency), UI flags such as which item is being updated or removed, and current error/success messages. On any quantity or removal change, the page state is replaced with the cartSummary returned by updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem, ensuring pricing information always comes from the backend interfaces and is not hardcoded on the page.

components.functionality: Initializes the cart page, manages page-level state, calls getCartSummary on load, and coordinates responses from updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem to keep the cart display in sync with the backend.

Renders the list of cart items returned in getCartSummary.items or in cartSummary from updateCartItemQuantity/removeCartItem, and delegates per-item quantity changes and removal actions to child components or events.
Represents a single cart item row that displays item name, description, quantity, unitPrice, and lineTotal from the interface data, and exposes controls for updating quantity and removing the item.
Handles user input for changing the quantity of a specific cart item and triggers updateCartItemQuantity with the corresponding cartItemId and new quantity.
Provides a remove action for a specific cart item, optionally with a front-end confirmation, and calls removeCartItem with the associated cartItemId on confirmation.

Displays the current order summary including subtotal, tax, total, and currency, based entirely on data from getCartSummary or the latest cartSummary returned by updateCartItemQuantity/removeCartItem.

Shows the 'Proceed to Checkout' button and 'Continue shopping' link, enabling or disabling the checkout button based on cart status and item count, and handling navigation to checkout.html and plans.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Displays a message and a prominent 'Continue shopping' call-to-action when the cart has no items (items array is empty), with the checkout button disabled.
Displays informational or error messages about the cart state, such as non-active cart status or errors returned from updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem.
Shows a loading state while getCartSummary is in progress and, optionally, a more localized loading state for item-level updates during updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, the cart page uses a single, centered content container on a white background. Inside this container, the main body is arranged as a two-column layout: the cart items list on the left and the order summary panel on the right, mirroring the mockup’s left-content / right-feature pattern. A slim cart-status banner sits at the very top of the content area, and a full-width checkout actions bar runs along the bottom of the viewport, echoing the linear, stacked sections seen in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, the cart page uses a single, centered content container on a white background. Inside this container, the main body is arranged as a two-column layout: the cart items list on the left and the order summary panel on the right, mirroring the mockup’s left-content / right-feature pattern. A slim cart-status banner sits at the very top of the content area, and a full-width checkout actions bar runs along the bottom of the viewport, echoing the linear, stacked sections seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content container spans roughly 80–88% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 1120–1200px), centered. The top status banner is shallow but full-width within this container. The main two-column zone takes up most of the vertical space, with the cart items list occupying about 60–65% of the container width (roughly 7/12) on the left and the summary panel about 35–40% (roughly 5/12) on the right. Vertical padding between header and main content, and between main content and footer/checkout bar, follows the 48–64px pattern. Each section and card uses 24–32px internal padding. In the empty-state, the multi-column layout collapses into a single, centered card occupying about 40–50% of the content width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low to moderate. Each cart item row has comfortable vertical padding and clear separation. The list and summary are contained in roomy cards with 24–32px padding, generous line spacing, and distinct spacing between headings, lines, and totals (typically 8–24px gaps). Text blocks are kept short and scannable, with key values (totals, item names, CTA buttons) given extra breathing room. The overall feel is open and approachable, avoiding cramped tables or dense data grids.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a loose Z-pattern. After scanning the top cyan bar and header, the eye drops into the cart content at the top-left: the cart-status banner and section heading. From there, attention moves horizontally to the right toward the cart-summary panel where the order total and main monetary CTA live. As users scroll, attention zig-zags down the cart items list, with quantity and remove controls aligned to the right for quick editing. Finally, the gaze is drawn down to the bottom full-width checkout actions bar where the primary checkout button sits on the right, balancing the earlier top-right focal points and keeping a clear end to the flow of actions.

layout.overall_description: The cart page sits beneath the existing cyan announcement bar and white navigation header, continuing the same centered, 12-column content structure on a white background. A slim, colored cart-status banner runs across the top of the main content area, immediately signaling how many items are in the cart or whether there are any issues. Below it, the page opens into a broad, two-column layout: on the left, a tall, friendly card lists each cart item with generous vertical spacing, big, readable labels, and right-aligned quantity controls and remove buttons; on the right, a slightly narrower but visually prominent summary panel sits like a clean card, showing subtotal, tax, and total in a tidy stack. Both columns are framed with ample white space above, below, and between them, echoing the spacious About section of the mockup. At the bottom of the viewport, a full-width, softly elevated checkout actions bar anchors the experience, with a playful but clear primary checkout button on the right and a gentler secondary option to return to maintenance plans on the left. When the cart is empty, the layout simplifies to a single, centered empty-state card with an illustration and a primary CTA, maintaining the same open, friendly tone. A subtle loading overlay can temporarily float over this entire content zone when operations are in progress, keeping the structure intact while making the page feel responsive and modern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller defines the main content region between the existing header band above and any global footer below. It positions a centered content container that aligns with the rest of the site’s grid, spanning roughly 80–88% of the viewport width with a max-width around 1120–1200px. There is generous top padding of about 48–64px from the bottom edge of the header to the first cart-specific element (the cart-status-banner), and similar bottom padding before the footer area, except where the checkout-actions-bar visually overlaps as a sticky bottom element. Within this controller, the vertical order of elements is: cart-status-banner at the top of the container, then the main two-column body with cart-items-list on the left and cart-summary-panel on the right, followed by any natural page spacing and the checkout-actions-bar pinned to the bottom of the viewport. The controller also manages state layouts: when the cart is empty, it centers the cart-empty-state card in place of the two-column layout; when loading, it allows a full-width cart-loading-overlay to sit above all these elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main layout wrapper using a centered, responsive grid. It controls stacking order, toggling between two-column and single-column (empty) layouts, and accommodates a sticky/fixed checkout-actions-bar and an overlaying cart-loading-overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-status-banner is positioned at the top of the cart content area, directly beneath the main site header and within the centered content container. It spans the full width of the container (aligning to the same left and right edges as the cart items and summary), but is relatively shallow in height—roughly 48–64px tall. The banner uses a cyan-tinted background or subtle accent to echo the strong color of the top announcement bar, making it immediately noticeable without feeling heavy. There is approximately 16–24px of margin below the banner before the main two-column layout begins, preserving a clear separation. Text inside the banner (e.g., “You have 2 plans in your cart” or error/warning messages) is left-aligned with 16–24px padding on all sides, and if there is an inline link or small icon, it appears to the right of the text but still within the banner’s boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline at the top of the main content stack. It scrolls away with the page content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-list sits on the left side of the main content zone, forming the larger portion of a two-column layout. On desktop, it occupies roughly 60–65% of the content container width (about 7 of 12 columns), starting flush with the container’s left edge. There is a comfortable 24–32px gap between the right edge of the list and the left edge of the cart-summary-panel, preserving the airy feel from the mockup’s About section. The list itself is presented as a tall, soft-cornered card or panel on a white background, with a light shadow or subtle border to define it without feeling heavy. It has 24–32px internal padding on all sides and 24px spacing under its section heading (e.g., “Your Cart”) before the first cart-item-row. Each subsequent row is stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them or separated by light dividers. The overall panel extends downward based on the number of items, and in long carts it scrolls with the page, while the right summary panel can stay partially visible if sticky behavior is used.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main grid as the left column. On narrower viewports, it should naturally stack above the cart-summary-panel, occupying close to 100% of the width with consistent horizontal padding and maintaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a horizontally oriented content block within the cart-items-list card. It spans almost the full width of the list, with 16–24px padding on the top and bottom and 0–8px padding on the left and right that sits inside the list’s own padding. The row is divided into three main horizontal zones: on the left (taking roughly 50–60% of the row’s width), the item name, short description, and plan details are stacked in 1–3 succinct lines; in the middle (about 15–20% of the width), the per-item price is right-aligned but visually close to the left content; and on the right (the remaining 20–30%), the quantity-update-control and remove-item-button are grouped as inline elements, aligned to the row’s right edge. Vertical alignment keeps all controls centered so the row feels balanced. There is a 8–16px gap between the quantity control and the remove button. Rows are separated from each other by a 16–24px vertical gap or a subtle divider line, maintaining low-to-moderate density rather than creating a tight table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, stacked vertically within the cart-items-list card. On smaller screens, the row’s layout can reflow into a two-line stack (text on top, controls and price on a second line) while preserving padding and clear separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-update-control sits within the rightmost portion of each cart-item-row, just inside the row’s right padding. It is a compact, pill-shaped or softly rounded control with a plus and minus button flanking a numeric quantity display. It occupies approximately 96–128px in width and about 32–40px in height, making it easy to tap or click without overwhelming the row. The control is horizontally aligned so its right edge lines up with other controls in the column, and vertically centered within the row’s height. There is a small 8–16px gap between this control and the remove-item-button to its right, giving each action its own hit area. On narrower viewports, the quantity control can appear above the remove button, still right-aligned, with 8–12px vertical spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the cart-item-row, right-aligned with flexible width. It remains attached to its corresponding row and moves with the row as the page is scrolled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-button is placed in the far right of each cart-item-row, adjacent to the quantity-update-control. It is relatively small but clearly tappable, typically styled as a text-based button or icon-text combination with a friendly but noticeable color (e.g., a soft red or muted accent) to signal removal without feeling harsh. The button is vertically centered in the row and right-aligned so its right edge aligns with the right padding of the row. There is an 8–16px horizontal gap between it and the quantity control, offering clear separation. The visual weight is lighter than the quantity control so it doesn’t dominate the row. On smaller screens or tightly constrained widths, the remove button can wrap beneath the quantity control, maintaining right alignment and an 8–12px vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the cart-item-row, positioned at the far right of the row’s interactive cluster. It scrolls normally with the list and does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel is positioned to the right of the cart-items-list as the secondary column in the main content area. On desktop, it occupies about 35–40% of the content container width (roughly 5 of 12 grid columns), with its left edge set approximately 24–32px to the right of the cart-items-list card, echoing the gap seen between text and illustration in the mockup’s About section. Vertically, the top of the summary panel aligns with or sits just slightly below the top of the cart-items-list, creating a cohesive top line. The panel is styled as a visually prominent card with a white or very light tinted background, soft corners (border-radius around 12–16px), and a light shadow to elevate it. Inside, it uses 24–32px padding on all sides. Content is stacked top-to-bottom: a bold heading (e.g., “Order Summary”), then a vertical list of line items (subtotal, estimated tax, discounts), each with left-aligned labels and right-aligned amounts, separated by 8–12px gaps. A clear horizontal separator and a slightly larger font size distinguish the final total near the bottom. Below the total, there is 16–24px of space and optionally a small supportive note (e.g., “Tax calculated at checkout”) or a secondary CTA. The panel’s height remains relatively compact compared to the items list, enhancing its card-like, at-a-glance feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline as the right column, with optional sticky behavior: on larger screens and longer carts, it can remain sticky within the viewport as the user scrolls the items list, staying pinned near the top-right within the main content area while respecting the container’s top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-actions-bar runs along the bottom of the viewport as a full-width horizontal band that sits above the main page background but below the centered content container edges, aligning visually with the rest of the layout. It spans 100% of the viewport width, but its internal content is constrained to the same centered width as the main container. The bar is about 64–80px tall, with 16–24px vertical padding, and uses a white or very light background with a soft top shadow or subtle top border to distinguish it from the underlying content. Inside the bar, content is laid out in a horizontal row: on the left side, a secondary action such as a text button or outlined button (“Back to maintenance plans”) and possibly a small note about secure checkout; on the right side, the primary, rounded CTA button (“Proceed to Checkout”), visually emphasized with a bold accent color. The space between left and right groups is flexible and expands to fill the bar, giving a calm, open appearance. On smaller screens, the bar content can stack, with the primary CTA centered on top and the secondary action below or vice versa, maintaining at least 16px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky or fixed to the bottom of the viewport, remaining visible as the user scrolls. It floats above the main content (with a slight elevation effect) but respects the horizontal bounds of the centered layout for its internal alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the cart-empty-state replaces the two-column layout within the main content area. It is centered horizontally in the content container and vertically positioned with generous breathing room: roughly 64–96px of margin above (from the cart-status-banner or top padding) and at least 64px below before any footer or the checkout-actions-bar. The empty-state appears as a friendly, medium-sized card occupying about 40–50% of the container width on desktop, with a white background, soft corners, and possibly a subtle illustration (e.g., a playful mascot) aligned above the text. Inside the card, elements are stacked vertically and center-aligned: illustration at the top, then a bold heading (e.g., “Your cart is empty”), a short explanatory line, and finally a primary CTA button (e.g., “Browse maintenance plans”) centered at the bottom. Spacing between these elements follows a 16–24px rhythm. The cart-summary-panel is hidden or collapsed in this state, so the empty-state becomes the main focal point. On smaller screens, the card stretches closer to 80–100% of the container width, keeping the same centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main content container, centrally positioned. It appears instead of the cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel when the cart has no items and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-loading-overlay sits above all cart-specific content when active, subtly dimming and softening the underlying layout while an operation (such as updating quantities or recalculating totals) is in progress. It covers the entire cart content region—including the cart-items-list, cart-summary-panel, cart-empty-state, and checkout-actions-bar—and may optionally extend up to the bottom of the main header for consistency. The overlay is a semi-transparent white or light-tinted layer with 40–60% opacity, maintaining the bright, friendly aesthetic while clearly signaling that interactions are temporarily paused. At the center of the visible content area (horizontally centered, and vertically centered within the viewport minus header and bottom bar), a simple loading indicator (spinner or playful brand-related animation) appears, roughly 48–64px in size, with a short loading message beneath it if desired. Underlying elements remain visible but unclickable, preserving the mental model of the layout while focusing attention on the loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a full-screen or full-content-area overlay with a high z-index. It is fixed relative to the viewport, remaining in place as the user scrolls, and intercepts interactions until it is dismissed.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Secure checkout page that loads the current cart summary, collects billing and payment details, and completes the order for maintenance plans or related items.

core_features: Load the current cart summary (items, quantities, pricing, totals, and billing field configuration) using getCheckoutSummary when the page initializes or when the URL state changes back to review mode.
Display an order review section that lists each cart item and shows subtotal, tax, total, and currency from the cart summary.
Render a billing and contact information form whose required fields and labels are driven by the billingFields configuration returned from getCheckoutSummary.
Provide a payment method selector and associated payment detail inputs (including card number, expiration, CVV) for card-based payments, binding the selected paymentMethod to the placeOrder payload.
Validate all required billing fields, payment details, and the terms consent checkbox before allowing submission.
Submit the checkout form via placeOrder, passing billing information, payment method, card details, and the agreeToTerms flag.

Handle placeOrder responses, showing confirmation details when success is true (including orderNumber, status, total, and currency) and error feedback when success is false or the status indicates failure.
After a successful order, switch the page into a confirmation state, hide the editable checkout form, display the confirmation summary, and expose a 'Return to Home' navigation link to index.html.
user_workflows: Standard checkout workflow: 1) User arrives on checkout.html (typically from a cart page). 2) Page initializes and calls getCheckoutSummary with no parameters. 3) The order summary section displays cart items and totals based on getCheckoutSummary.cartSummary. 4) The billing form renders fields based on getCheckoutSummary.billingFields, marking required fields appropriately. 5) User fills in billing/contact information (first name, last name, address, city, ZIP, email, phone, and any other fields provided by the API). 6) User chooses a payment method (e.g., credit card) and enters card details (cardNumber, cardExpiration, cardCvv) when applicable. 7) User checks the terms-and-conditions consent checkbox, which controls the agreeToTerms value. 8) User clicks the primary submit button (e.g., 'Place Order' or 'Complete Enrollment'). 9) Frontend validation runs; if required fields or card details are missing/invalid, inline errors are shown and submission is blocked. 10) If validation passes, the page disables the submit button, marks a 'submitting' state, and calls placeOrder with the collected data. 11) On a successful response (success=true), the page stores the returned orderId, orderNumber, status, total, currency, and message in in-memory state, updates the URL parameters to reflect a confirmation view, re-renders the page in confirmation mode, and shows a success message and summary. 12) User may click 'Return to Home' to navigate to index.html.

Error handling workflow for validation errors: 1) User attempts to submit the form by clicking the primary button. 2) The page checks each field described in billingFields where required=true and verifies that a non-empty value is provided. 3) The page verifies that paymentMethod is selected and that cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv are provided when the selected paymentMethod requires card payment. 4) The page verifies agreeToTerms is true. 5) For any validation failures, the submission is cancelled, an error state is associated with the corresponding form controls, and error messages are shown near the inputs and optionally in a summary banner. 6) Once the user corrects the errors and re-submits, validation runs again before calling placeOrder.

Error handling workflow for placeOrder failures: 1) User submits a valid form, triggering a placeOrder call. 2) The page enters a loading state for the submission. 3) placeOrder returns with success=false or with a status that indicates a failed or pending payment. 4) The page exits the loading state, keeps the form visible, and shows an error banner using the message value from the response. 5) The user can adjust billing or payment details and submit again, repeating the process until success=true or they abandon the checkout.

Confirmation view workflow: 1) After a successful placeOrder response, the page switches into a confirmation view and updates URL parameters (for example, setting a view flag and including the orderId or orderNumber as query parameters). 2) The confirmation component reads in-memory order state (derived from the placeOrder response) to display key details such as orderNumber, status, total, and currency, along with a brief confirmation message. 3) The editable checkout form and payment fields are hidden or disabled, and only read-only confirmation details are shown. 4) A 'Return to Home' link is available and navigates to index.html when clicked. 5) If the page is loaded directly with URL parameters indicating confirmation but without in-memory order context (for example, via a bookmark), the page shows a generic confirmation acknowledgment based on available query parameters and does not attempt to re-load order details, since there is no interface for fetching an order by ID.

Revisiting checkout in review mode workflow: 1) If the URL indicates review mode (for example, no explicit view parameter or view=review), the page calls getCheckoutSummary on load. 2) If the cart is still active and contains items, the summary and form are displayed normally; the user can complete checkout as in the standard workflow. 3) If getCheckoutSummary indicates there are no items in the cart (based on its returned items structure), the page shows an informational message that checkout cannot proceed without items and may hide or disable the submit button until the cart is updated on another page.
interactions: Page load interaction: On initial page load or when URL parameters change to indicate review mode, the page triggers an onLoad handler that calls getCheckoutSummary. Once the promise resolves, the order summary and billing form components are populated with the returned cartSummary and billingFields data.

Billing field input interaction: For each field described in billingFields, the user can input or edit values. onChange handlers update the local form state for that field and clear any error flags when the user modifies a previously invalid field.

Payment method selection interaction: The payment method component exposes options mapped to allowed values for the paymentMethod parameter sent to placeOrder. When the user selects a payment method, an onChange handler updates the paymentMethod in form state and conditionally shows or hides cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv fields based on whether the selected method requires card details.

Payment detail input interaction: For visible card fields, the user can enter cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv. onChange handlers update the corresponding values in form state and may perform basic client-side formatting or masking (without hardcoding any specific sample card numbers).

Terms consent interaction: The user toggles a terms-and-conditions checkbox that controls the agreeToTerms boolean. An onChange handler updates this value in state; if the checkbox is not checked when the user attempts to submit, validation will fail and an error message is shown.

Submission interaction: When the user clicks the primary submit button, an onClick or onSubmit handler runs front-end validation using the current form state and the billingFields configuration. If validation passes, the handler sets a 'submitting' flag, disables the submit button, and calls placeOrder with the form values: firstName, lastName, email, phone, streetAddress, city, state, zip, paymentMethod, cardNumber, cardExpiration, cardCvv, and agreeToTerms.

placeOrder response interaction: After placeOrder resolves, an onResponse handler examines success and status. If success=true, it stores the returned orderId, orderNumber, status, total, currency, and message in local state and updates the URL query string to reflect confirmation mode. If success=false or status indicates failure, it displays an error banner with the message field and leaves the page in review mode, allowing the user to correct inputs.

Confirmation display interaction: In confirmation mode, the user sees read-only confirmation information and may click the 'Return to Home' link. An onClick handler on this link navigates to index.html without passing additional parameters.

Mode toggling via URL interaction: If the URL query parameter for view changes (for example, view=review vs. view=confirmation) through client-side navigation or a link, a handler re-evaluates which components to render. In review mode, the form and summary are shown and getCheckoutSummary may be called again; in confirmation mode, only the confirmation component is displayed with the latest order data available in state or from URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is a URL query parameter that indicates the current view mode, combined with in-memory state populated from getCheckoutSummary and placeOrder. By default, when checkout.html is loaded without query parameters, the page behaves as if view=review, meaning it will call getCheckoutSummary and render the order summary and checkout form. A query parameter such as view=review explicitly forces the review mode and triggers a fresh getCheckoutSummary call on load or when the view parameter transitions back to review. When placeOrder returns success=true, the page updates the URL to include view=confirmation and may also append orderId and orderNumber as additional query parameters. In this confirmation mode, the page suppresses the checkout form and displays confirmation information using the latest in-memory order state from the placeOrder response; if the page is reloaded with view=confirmation but has no in-memory order context (such as when accessed via a shared link), it uses only the data present in the URL query parameters (if any) to show a generic confirmation acknowledgment, without attempting to fetch an order from the backend. The ZIP, city, state, and other fields entered during checkout are not persisted to the URL by default to avoid exposing sensitive information, but the implementation may optionally use URL parameters for non-sensitive prefill values (e.g., a prefillEmail parameter) when linking from other pages. All multi-step transitions on this page (review to confirmation) rely on URL parameters for view selection and do not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage.

components.functionality: On page initialization or when the URL indicates review mode, this component invokes getCheckoutSummary to retrieve the current cart summary and billingFields configuration, then populates shared state used by the order summary and billing form components.

Displays a read-only summary of the items in the cart, including each item's name, quantity, unitPrice, lineTotal, subtotal, tax, total, and currency, using the data returned by getCheckoutSummary. It is rendered in review mode and may also show a simplified summary in confirmation mode based on the last known cartSummary or total from placeOrder.

Renders billing and contact inputs (such as first name, last name, street address, city, state, ZIP, email, phone) based on the billingFields configuration from getCheckoutSummary. Required fields are determined by billingFields.required. As the user types, this component updates local form state and handles validation feedback for missing or invalid required fields.

Provides a control for choosing the paymentMethod value sent to placeOrder (for example, credit_card or debit_card). When the user selects a method that requires card payment, this component signals the payment details form to display cardNumber, cardExpiration, and cardCvv inputs. It keeps the selected paymentMethod in sync with the checkout form state.

Shows and manages card inputs (cardNumber, cardExpiration, cardCvv) when the selected payment method requires card details. It stores the values used by placeOrder, performs basic client-side validation (such as non-empty checks and simple format checks), and exposes validation results back to the parent checkout form component.

Displays a checkbox or equivalent control for the user to confirm agreement with the terms and conditions. It drives the agreeToTerms boolean parameter required by placeOrder and participates in validation when the user submits the checkout form.

Shows aggregated error messages for form validation failures and for errors returned by placeOrder (using the response message field). It becomes visible when the checkout form detects invalid input or when placeOrder responds with success=false or a failing status.

Coordinates the billing-contact-form, payment-method-selector, payment-details-form, and terms-consent-control into a single form. On submit, it gathers all field values, runs combined validation logic (including required billing fields and payment details), and if valid, calls placeOrder with the aggregated data. It also manages a 'submitting' state, disabling inputs and the submit button while the request is in progress.

Encapsulates the call to placeOrder. It receives validated form data from the checkout-form-wrapper, invokes placeOrder with the exact parameter set required, and returns the response to the calling context. It also normalizes the response into a shared order state used by the confirmation component and updates the URL query parameters to reflect confirmation mode when success=true.

Rendered when the URL indicates confirmation mode or when placeOrder has just returned success=true. It shows a confirmation message using the latest order data from the place-order-action (orderNumber, status, total, currency, and message) and hides the editable checkout form. If confirmation mode is entered via URL without in-memory order data, it falls back to a generic message based on URL parameters without attempting a new API call.
Provides a link from the confirmation view back to the site’s home page at index.html after successful checkout.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The checkout page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: any global announcement/header sit above, then the main checkout content runs as a centered, full-width section. Inside the checkout section, the core layout mimics the About two-column split: the left column hosts the main interactive form (billing and payment), and the right column hosts the order summary as a co-primary block. Status messaging (error banner, confirmation state) runs full-width at the top of this section, with supporting actions (return home) at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The checkout page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: any global announcement/header sit above, then the main checkout content runs as a centered, full-width section. Inside the checkout section, the core layout mimics the About two-column split: the left column hosts the main interactive form (billing and payment), and the right column hosts the order summary as a co-primary block. Status messaging (error banner, confirmation state) runs full-width at the top of this section, with supporting actions (return home) at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A single, prominent checkout section spans the full page width with a centered content container using an implicit 12-column grid. The form column occupies roughly 7/12 of the width on desktop (about 55–60%), while the order summary occupies about 5/12 (40–45%), echoing the About section’s asymmetric balance. Vertical padding around the main checkout section is generous (48–64px top and bottom), with 16–24px spacing between internal elements such as form groups and panels. Full-width elements like the error banner and confirmation panel stretch across the content container, with comfortable breathing room above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to moderate: short form groups, clearly separated blocks, and ample whitespace, avoiding cramped fields. Each logical block (billing information, payment method, payment details, terms, order summary) is visually chunked with clear 16–24px gaps. Text is concise, with large labels and enough spacing to keep the playful, approachable feel of the brand. Key CTAs remain visually bold but are not surrounded by dense content, ensuring they stand out without clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow maintains a loose Z-pattern: the eye starts at any top error/status messaging, then drops to the left column at the top of the checkout form (billing/contact), scans down through payment selection and details to the Place Order button, then moves right to validate the order in the summary panel. On success, attention is pulled first to a centered confirmation panel, then down to the return-home link. The right-side order summary acts like the penguin illustration in the mockup: a visually strong, secondary focal point that balances the form and reassures the user.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing announcement bar and header, the checkout page presents a single, broad white section dedicated to completing the purchase. This section uses a centered content container that feels airy and modern, with 48–64px padding above and below. At the very top of this container, a full-width error/status banner appears when needed, followed by the main checkout body: a two-column layout where the left 7/12 of the width holds a clean, stacked checkout form, and the right 5/12 hosts a tidy order summary card. The left form column walks the user step-by-step from billing/contact details to payment method, into payment details, then down to terms consent and a bold, rounded Place Order button. The right column stays visually compact and slightly taller than wide, surfacing plan details, pricing, and totals with comfortable margin around it. During loading, a centered overlay loader temporarily sits above this entire area; after a successful payment, the form and summary are replaced or overlaid by a friendly confirmation panel centered in the same container, with a low-friction link back to the home page beneath it. The overall effect is a focused, trustworthy checkout experience that still feels light and local, with clear breathing room between all elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout init loader appears as an overlay centered on the main checkout section. It covers the entire content area that would otherwise contain the form and order summary, spanning 100% of the content container width and at least the current viewport height within the main section. A semi-transparent white backdrop dims the underlying layout, with a cyan-accented spinner or friendly loading indicator positioned dead-center both horizontally and vertically. When active, it visually sits on top of the checkout-form-wrapper, order-summary-panel, and any banners, clearly signaling that the page is preparing data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-screen (within the main content area) overlay; fixed relative to the viewport while visible so it remains centered even when the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary panel is placed in the right column of the main checkout two-column layout, mirroring the illustration card from the About section. It occupies roughly 5/12 of the content container width on desktop and is vertically aligned with the top of the left form column. The panel appears as a card with rounded corners and generous internal padding (16–24px), floating in whitespace with at least 24px margin to the left column. Its height is determined by its content, typically forming a tall-ish rectangle that visually balances the taller form on the left. On smaller screens, this panel stacks below the checkout-form-wrapper, spanning nearly 100% of the width with 24px top margin to keep separation clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page as part of the right column; on wide screens it remains aligned to the top of the form column but does not fix or stick by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the left column of the main checkout area, the billing/contact form appears at the very top of the column, acting as the first step in the vertical flow. It spans the full width of the left column (about 7/12 of the container on desktop) with 24px margin below before the next section. Fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on space, but overall remain visually open, with 16px vertical spacing between individual inputs and 24px above the section title. The entire block reads as a clean, self-contained segment of the form, with enough top margin (24–32px below any error banner) to feel comfortably separated from global messaging.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; forms the top-most content block of the main checkout form stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector sits directly below the billing-contact-form within the left column, separated by about 24px of vertical spacing. It stretches across the full width of the form column and presents its methods as horizontally aligned, rounded buttons or cards, echoing the playful CTA styling from the mockup. On larger screens, these options arrange side by side, typically occupying 25–33% width each with 16px gaps; on smaller screens they stack vertically. The entire selector block is moderately tall, but surrounded by whitespace that clearly distinguishes it from both the billing form above and the payment details form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the stacked form content, expanding vertically as needed to accommodate multiple payment options while preserving internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment details form follows the payment-method-selector, separated by roughly 24px of vertical space to maintain a clear step-like flow. It occupies the full width of the left column, with fields arranged in a simple top-to-bottom stack: card number or account fields first, then expiry, security code, and any additional data. Each row has 12–16px of vertical spacing and comfortable side padding within the form container. The block’s bottom edge sits 24–32px above the terms-consent-control, creating a cohesive but breathable sequence of form sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; height adjusts to its fields while preserving uniform spacing between this and neighboring sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms consent control is positioned near the bottom of the left form column, directly under the payment-details-form with around 24px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the column but is visually compact: a single checkbox with concise text, aligned left. There is 16px spacing below this control before the Place Order button, allowing the checkbox and copy to breathe and stay legible without crowding the CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the bottom of the form stack, expanding slightly in height if terms text wraps on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout error banner appears at the top of the main checkout content container, directly below any global header/announcement region and above both the left form and right order summary columns. It spans 100% of the content container width, forming a shallow but visually strong horizontal band with a colored background (e.g., soft red or orange) and rounded edges matching the playful style. There is 16–24px of whitespace below the banner before the two-column layout begins, ensuring the message feels prominent yet not cramped. The banner does not scroll independently; it simply pushes the main content downward when visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block at the top of the checkout section; appears and disappears dynamically but does not fix or float over other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-form-wrapper defines the main two-column structure for the page’s core content. It sits below the checkout-error-banner (or top padding if no banner) with about 32px of vertical space. The wrapper spans the full width of the centered content container, then splits into a left column (~7/12 width) for the stacked billing and payment forms and a right column (~5/12 width) for the order summary panel. Internally, it maintains 24–32px padding on the sides to keep both columns away from the container edges, and at least 24px gutter between the columns. On tablet and mobile, this wrapper gracefully collapses into a single column: the left form content appears first, with the order summary stacking beneath it, each spanning nearly the full width and separated by around 32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive container that manages column layout: two columns on wider viewports, single column stack on narrower ones; otherwise scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The place-order-action is rendered as a bold, rounded primary button positioned at the very bottom of the left form column, directly beneath the terms-consent-control with about 16px of vertical spacing. It aligns to the left edge of the form content but is large enough to feel like a key action, spanning roughly 40–60% of the left column width on desktop, with generous vertical padding (12–16px) and horizontal padding (24–32px). There is 32–40px of whitespace below the button before the end of the section or the next content block, ensuring it stands alone as the main call-to-action. On narrow screens, the button can stretch closer to full width for emphasis while maintaining consistent margins to the sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static button at the bottom of the form stack; does not float, but remains the last element in the left column’s vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an order is successfully placed, the checkout-confirmation-panel appears in the same main content area that previously housed the form and order summary, visually taking over the primary focus. It is centered within the content container and typically spans about 60–70% of the container width, forming a prominent card with rounded corners, soft shadow, and generous internal padding (32–40px). The panel is vertically spaced 48–64px from the top of the checkout section to give it an open, celebratory feel, and contains concise confirmation copy and key details. The underlying form and order summary are either hidden or visually de-emphasized to keep the confirmation as the clear focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered block that replaces or overlays the main two-column layout in the document flow after success; scrolls naturally if content exceeds the viewport height.
