component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home-link is placed directly below the checkout-confirmation-panel, aligned to the center of the content container and separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. It appears as a simple text link or subtle secondary button with the same playful, rounded aesthetic but less visual weight than the primary CTA. It occupies minimal height and width, sitting within the same central column as the confirmation panel, and has 48–64px of padding below before the end of the main section to keep the layout feeling light and complete.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element beneath the confirmation panel; remains centered and does not float or stick.

name: Special Offers
title: Special Offers
description: Displays current HVAC promotions and allows users to filter, sort, and browse offers (including duct-related discounts) with state persisted in URL parameters.

core_features: Load available special-offer filter options (categories and discount types) using getSpecialOfferFilterOptions and populate filter controls.
Read current filter and pagination state from URL parameters (category, discountType, maxPrice, sortBy, page).
Retrieve a filtered, sorted, and paginated list of special offers using getSpecialOffers with parameters derived from the URL.
Display each special offer with title, short description, discount information (percentage, promo price, or amount off), and end date when available.
Allow users to change filters (category, discount type, maximum promotional price) and sort order, then update the URL and refresh the offer list.
Support pagination through a page parameter, allowing users to move between pages of offers with state reflected in the URL.
Enable navigation to a detailed offer page (offer_detail.html) when a user clicks an offer title or 'View Offer' action, passing the offerId parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View all active special offers
1. User opens specials.html without query parameters.
2. The page controller parses the URL and detects no filter parameters, so it constructs a default filter and sort state.
3. The page calls getSpecialOfferFilterOptions to obtain available categories and discount types and binds these to the filter controls.
4. The page calls getSpecialOffers with a filters object (based on default state) and a default sortBy value.
5. The offer list displays the returned offers and shows pagination controls if the total exceeds the page size.
6. User scrolls and optionally navigates to another page using the pagination controls, updating the page parameter in the URL and triggering another getSpecialOffers call.

Workflow: Focus on duct-related offers and limit promo price
1. User arrives on specials.html with a preselected category in the URL, such as category set from another page (e.g., service_detail.html linking to duct-related specials).
2. The page controller parses URL parameters (including category and any others) into the internal filter state.
3. getSpecialOfferFilterOptions is called to populate category and discount-type controls; the category control is preselected based on the URL parameter.
4. getSpecialOffers is called with a filters object that includes the category from the URL and any other URL-derived filters, plus sortBy.
5. User sets a maximum promotional price using the maxPrice control to find lower-price offers and optionally selects a discount type.
6. When the user applies the filters, the controller updates the URL parameters (category, discountType, maxPrice, page reset to the first page) and calls getSpecialOffers again with the new filters.
7. The offer list refreshes, showing only offers matching the category and price constraints.

Workflow: Sort offers and navigate to offer detail
1. User is viewing a list of offers on specials.html.
2. User selects a different sort option (for example, by expiry date or by discount size) using the sort control.
3. The page controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets the page parameter to the first page.
4. The page calls getSpecialOffers with the existing filters and the updated sortBy value.
5. The list of offers re-renders in the new sort order.
6. User clicks on an offer title or 'View Offer' action in an offer row/card.
7. The browser navigates to offer_detail.html with a query parameter offerId set to the selected SpecialOffer.id.

Workflow: Use browser navigation to restore prior filter state
1. User adjusts filters and pagination on specials.html; each change updates the URL parameters (category, discountType, maxPrice, sortBy, page).
2. User then navigates away to another page.
3. When the user clicks the browser Back button to return to specials.html, the page initializes using the URL parameters present in the history entry.
4. The page controller parses these parameters, reconstructs the filter and pagination state, and calls getSpecialOfferFilterOptions and getSpecialOffers accordingly.
5. The user sees the same filtered and sorted offers list as before navigating away, without needing to reapply filters.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters (category, discountType, maxPrice, sortBy, page) into the internal state and then fetch filter options and offers.

On category selection/change in the filter controls, update the internal filter state, update the category query parameter in the URL, reset the page parameter to the first page, and trigger a new getSpecialOffers call.
On discount type selection/change, update the internal filter state, update the discountType query parameter in the URL, reset the page, and trigger a new getSpecialOffers call.

On maximum promotional price input/change, update the internal filter state, update the maxPrice query parameter in the URL (clearing it if the input is empty), reset the page, and trigger a new getSpecialOffers call.
On sort option change, update the internal sortBy state, update the sortBy query parameter in the URL, reset the page parameter, and trigger a new getSpecialOffers call.
On pagination control click (next page, previous page, or specific page number), update the page parameter in the URL and call getSpecialOffers with the same filters but a different page value.
On clicking an offer title or 'View Offer' link in the offers list, navigate to offer_detail.html with offerId set to the corresponding SpecialOffer.id.

On browser back/forward navigation, listen for history changes (e.g., popstate), re-parse URL parameters into the internal state, and re-trigger getSpecialOffers to keep the displayed offers synchronized with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query parameters are the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state on specials.html. On initialization and whenever the browser's address bar changes (including via back/forward), the page controller parses the following parameters: category (string), discountType (string), maxPrice (number), sortBy (string), and page (number). If a parameter is present, it overrides any default in-memory state. If a parameter is absent, the controller uses an implementation-defined default for that piece of state and does not introduce any hard-coded filter values in this specification. The controller then builds a filters object for getSpecialOffers that includes the current filter criteria derived from the URL (such as category, discountType, maxPrice, and pagination information including page) and a sortBy argument set from the URL parameter. After calling getSpecialOfferFilterOptions to populate filter controls, the controller sets the UI control values to match the parsed state. Whenever the user changes filters, sort order, or pagination through UI controls, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters (adding, updating, or removing them as needed), modifies the browser history entry, and triggers a new getSpecialOffers call with filters and sortBy derived from the updated URL. This keeps the displayed offers, filter controls, and URL synchronized without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Coordinates initialization, URL parameter parsing, data fetching via getSpecialOfferFilterOptions and getSpecialOffers, and state propagation to child components for filters, offers list, and pagination.

Displays dynamic filter controls for category, discount type, maximum promotional price, and sort order. Initializes control options using getSpecialOfferFilterOptions data passed from the controller. Emits filter and sort changes so that the controller can update the URL and refresh the offers list.

Receives an array of SpecialOffer records and renders them as a list of offer items. Each item exposes title, shortDescription, discount details derived from discountType and related fields, and endDate when present. Provides interactive elements (title and 'View Offer' link) that navigate to the offer_detail page with the appropriate offerId.

Represents a single SpecialOffer within the offer list. Formats the discount area based on discountType and associated fields (e.g., percentage, promotional price, amount off) and shows the expiration date if endDate is available. Handles click interactions on the item title or 'View Offer' link to trigger navigation.

Displays pagination based on the total count from getSpecialOffers and the current page derived from the URL. Allows the user to navigate between pages; emits events that cause the controller to update the page parameter in the URL and request a new set of offers.

Shows a textual summary of currently applied filters and sort order based on the parsed URL parameters and selected options (e.g., selected category, discount type, maximum price, and current sort). Offers clearable filter tokens or a 'clear all' interaction, which triggers the controller to remove corresponding query parameters and reload offers.

Encapsulates logic for reading from and writing to the browser URL query string. On load and on popstate, it parses category, discountType, maxPrice, sortBy, and page into a state object consumed by the controller. On filter/sort/pagination changes from child components, it updates the URL parameters (and optionally the history stack) and notifies the controller so getSpecialOffers is called with filters matching the current URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a global cyan announcement bar at the very top (outside this page scope), followed by the white header with logo and navigation, then the Special Offers content as a single, full-width white section controlled by the specials-page-controller. Inside this section, the top portion behaves like the About section in the mockup: a two-column hero band where the left side contains the Special Offers heading and brief explanatory copy, and the right side contains a prominent illustration or featured-offer visual. Directly beneath this hero band, within the same content container, the filter bar spans the width, followed by the active filters summary, then the vertically stacked offer list, and finally the centered pagination controls at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a global cyan announcement bar at the very top (outside this page scope), followed by the white header with logo and navigation, then the Special Offers content as a single, full-width white section controlled by the specials-page-controller. Inside this section, the top portion behaves like the About section in the mockup: a two-column hero band where the left side contains the Special Offers heading and brief explanatory copy, and the right side contains a prominent illustration or featured-offer visual. Directly beneath this hero band, within the same content container, the filter bar spans the width, followed by the active filters summary, then the vertically stacked offer list, and finally the centered pagination controls at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The specials-page-controller uses a centered content container (roughly max-width 1100–1200px) on a white background, with generous vertical padding (48–64px) above the hero band and 48–64px below the pagination to keep the page airy. The hero band itself takes up a substantial vertical slice near the top, about 40% of the initial viewport height. Within it, the left hero column (heading + text) occupies about 40–45% of the width (5/12), and the right column illustration/featured offer occupies about 55–60% (7/12), echoing the About section ratio. The filter bar, active-filters-summary, and pagination are moderate in height; they use vertical padding in the 16–24px range. The offer list consumes most of the remaining vertical space, with each offer card spanning the full content width (within the container) and separated by 24–32px vertical gaps.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low-to-moderate. The hero text uses large, readable typography with comfortable line height and generous spacing around the heading and paragraph. The filter bar groups controls horizontally but keeps them visually simple and well-separated, avoiding a cluttered form feel. Offer list items are card-based, with 24px internal padding, clear typographic hierarchy (title > key savings > supporting details), and only a few critical data points per card. Between the filter bar, active filters, and the first offer card, there is 16–24px vertical spacing to keep each block visually distinct. Overall the layout uses plenty of white space to feel approachable and marketing-focused rather than like a dense tool.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s loose Z-pattern. Users first notice the Special Offers heading in the top-left of the hero band, then scan to the right toward the illustration or featured deal highlight. From there, the eye drops down to the left-aligned filter bar, moves right across its controls and main filter button, then down to the active filters chips beneath. Next, attention flows vertically through the centered column of offer cards, each with a left-anchored title and a right-side CTA/button, subtly reinforcing the left-to-right reading flow. The pagination controls at the bottom center serve as the natural terminal point of the scroll before users either move to another page or scroll back up to adjust filters.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s cyan announcement strip and white navigation header, the Special Offers page opens into a bright, spacious white section that fills the viewport width but keeps content centered in a medium-width column. At the top of this section, a hero band mimics the About layout from the mockup: on the left, a bold, friendly “Special Offers” heading sits above a short, two- to three-line description explaining current HVAC deals; on the right, a large, colorful illustration or featured-offer card anchored within a light card frame reinforces the playful brand. Generous top and bottom padding gives this hero room to breathe. Immediately underneath, the hero text and image end, but the white background continues as the filter layer begins: a full-width filter bar stretches across the content container with dropdowns, toggles, and a primary-colored filter/apply button, all horizontally aligned and comfortably spaced. Just below, a compact row of active filter chips appears, left-aligned, summarizing the current state. The main body of the page is a vertical stack of rounded offer cards, each full-width within the container and separated by clear white gaps. Each card uses a left-right split with the offer title and brief description on the left and the savings amount, validity dates, and a rounded primary CTA button on the right. At the very bottom of the list, the pagination controls are centered, with previous/next and page numbers sized to be tappable but not heavy. The whole layout feels clean and local-service friendly, using playful color accents and rounded shapes while preserving a simple, linear browsing experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specials-page-controller defines the main Special Offers content region beneath the global cyan announcement bar and the white header/navigation band. It occupies 100% of the viewport width but constrains its inner content to a centered container roughly 1100–1200px wide, aligned on a 12-column grid. There is generous vertical padding of about 56px at the top (between the header and the hero band) and 56px at the bottom (after pagination), maintaining the airy marketing feel from the mockup. Within this region, the first visible block is the hero band: a two-column layout spanning the full content width, with the left column taking roughly 5/12 of the width. This left column starts near the container’s left edge and contains the “Special Offers” title (styled similarly in scale to the ‘About°’ heading) with 16–24px space below it, followed by a concise paragraph explaining the types of promotions available. The right column, occupying roughly 7/12 of the width, aligns its content to the right edge of the container and contains either a large illustration or a featured special card, visually substantial and vertically centered relative to the left text. The bottom edge of this hero band is separated from the filter bar below by a 32px vertical gap, creating a clear break before the filters begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container that scrolls with the page; it defines the section’s content width and vertical rhythm but does not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The special-offer-filter-bar sits directly below the hero band, within the same centered content container, separated from the hero by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace. It spans nearly the full width of the inner container (leaving standard horizontal padding margins of 16–24px from the viewport edges on smaller screens) and is visually expressed as a low-profile horizontal band. The bar itself is a simple, flat surface on the white background, possibly with a very subtle bottom border or faint shadow to delineate it without adding heaviness. Controls are arranged left-to-right: for example, a primary category dropdown on the far left, followed by additional dropdowns or toggles (e.g., type of service, price order), and finally a prominent rounded primary CTA button (e.g., “Apply Filters” or “Reset Filters”) aligned toward the right side. The controls are vertically centered within a bar height of roughly 56–64px, with each input given 16–24px horizontal spacing to keep the density low-to-moderate. On wide screens, everything fits on a single row; on narrower screens, controls gracefully wrap to a second row while maintaining at least 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. There is about 16–20px of vertical space between the bottom of the filter bar and the active-filters-summary below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band within the flow. On desktop it scrolls away with the page (not sticky by default), but it remains visually strong due to its central position and the presence of a primary-colored button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-summary is positioned immediately below the special-offer-filter-bar, aligned to the same centered content container and spanning its full width. There is about 16–20px of vertical spacing from the filter bar above, and about 24px of spacing before the first offer card below. The component’s content is left-aligned to echo the text alignment of the hero heading and maintain consistent visual anchoring. It typically appears as a horizontal row (or wrapping rows) of pill-shaped chips representing each active filter. Each chip is small but readable, with 8px vertical and 16px horizontal internal padding and rounded corners, and might include a small ‘x’ icon for removal. When multiple filters are active, chips wrap to the next line, with 8px vertical spacing between rows. A faint label like “Active filters:” may precede the chips, lightly bolded, occupying the leftmost part of the row. The presentation stays visually light so it does not compete with the offer cards but remains clearly visible and scannable as a status line for the current filter state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/row-like block within the normal scrolling flow, expanding vertically as chips wrap but otherwise unconstrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The special-offer-list begins directly beneath the active-filters-summary, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It shares the same centered content width, aligning its left edge with the hero text and filter bar. The list is structured as a single vertical column of offer cards (special-offer-list-item components), each occupying the full usable width of the container minus standard side padding. There is a consistent vertical gap of about 24–32px between each card, ensuring a light, scannable layout rather than a dense grid. Optionally, a small row above the first card (within this list container) can display metadata like “12 offers found” aligned to the left and a sort dropdown aligned to the right, but this row remains low-profile so it doesn’t overshadow the cards. As the user scrolls, this offer list forms the main vertical content area, often exceeding the height of the viewport. On very wide screens, the list remains centered with generous empty space on both sides, reinforcing the airy, marketing-style composition seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrollable content stack within the page; it extends as long as there are offers, pushing the pagination controls down. It does not use internal scrolling; it relies on the browser’s main scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each special-offer-list-item appears as a distinct, card-like block within the special-offer-list. The card sits on the white page background but is visually separated with a light shadow or soft border and rounded corners (e.g., 8–12px radius), reflecting the playful, friendly brand language. The card spans nearly the full width of the content container, with internal padding around 24px on all sides to maintain low-to-moderate density. Internally, the card uses a two-column layout on desktop: the left column occupies about 65–70% of the card width and is left-aligned, while the right column takes the remaining 30–35% and aligns its content toward the right edge. In the left column, the offer title is prominent at the top, followed by 8–12px spacing, then a short one- to two-line description and key notes (e.g., service type, eligibility). Small badge elements (e.g., “Duct Cleaning”, “Limited Time”) can appear under or beside the title in accent colors, with modest spacing so they don’t overcrowd the text. In the right column, the savings amount or main value proposition (e.g., “Save $150”) is emphasized near the top, possibly in a larger or bolder type, with the expiration date and any brief legal summary beneath it in smaller text. A rounded primary CTA button (e.g., “Claim Offer” or “Schedule Now”) anchors the bottom-right area of the card, sized with 10–12px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding, in a bright brand color, so it stands out but still feels friendly. On narrower screens, these two columns stack: the left content appears first, followed by the right content and CTA, each separated by around 16px of vertical spacing while maintaining consistent internal padding. Overall, the card remains simple and clean, focusing on a few key pieces of information and a clear action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level card within the list that expands vertically with content; it respects the responsive grid by switching from a two-column horizontal layout on wider screens to a stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specials-pagination-controls component is positioned at the bottom of the special-offer-list, separated from the last offer card by about 32–40px of vertical space. It sits within the same centered content container but is horizontally centered within that container, creating a strong visual anchor at the end of the page. The pagination UI appears as a compact horizontal row: previous and next buttons flanking a sequence of page numbers, or a minimal set of numbers if there are many pages. Each button/number has comfortable tap targets (at least 32px height) with 8–12px horizontal spacing between them, maintaining the page’s airy feel. The current page number is highlighted using the brand’s primary accent color and possibly a subtle pill shape or filled circle; other page numbers remain low-key with a border or simple underline on hover. There is an additional 48–56px of vertical padding beneath the pagination before the end of the section (and before any footer), mirroring the top padding and framing the list visually. The controls remain visually lighter than the hero and cards but clearly accessible, signaling the user’s ability to browse more offers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored at the natural end of the list; it scrolls with the content and does not exhibit sticky behavior. It reflows responsively, wrapping the page numbers onto additional lines if space is constrained on narrower screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specials-url-state-sync component is not directly visible in the UI; instead, it underpins the behavior of the filter bar, active filters summary, offer list, and pagination. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the Special Offers page logic, monitoring URL parameters and updating the state of filters, current page, and sorting, while also pushing any changes from these components back into the URL. Because it has no visual representation, it does not consume any explicit space in the layout and does not affect the visual spacing or alignment of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint component that operates in the background. It does not occupy layout space or alter the flow; it simply synchronizes state with the URL while the page structure remains as described above.

name: Offer Detail
title: Offer Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected special offer and routes the user into the scheduling flow with the promotion attached.

core_features: Load full details for a specific special offer using getSpecialOfferDetails based on the offerId URL parameter.
Display offer information including title, descriptions, discount type, discountLabel, discountPercent, promotionalPrice, and validity status (isCurrentlyActive).
List all applicable services for the offer (from applicableServices) and allow the user to choose which service to book when more than one is available.
Clearly show how the discount will be applied to the booking, using termsSummary and offer.discountType/discount fields.
Provide a primary call-to-action to navigate to schedule_service.html with serviceId, offerId, and bookingType=promotion_booking in the URL.
Provide a secondary navigation option to return to specials.html filtered by the current offer.category.
Handle error and edge states (missing offerId, offer not found, offer inactive) with appropriate messages and adjusted availability of the booking CTA.
user_workflows:
1. User opens Offer Detail from specials.html or index.html:
   - 1.1 The URL contains offer_detail.html?offerId={id}.
   - 1.2 On page initialization, the page reads offerId from the URL query string.
   - 1.3 The page triggers getSpecialOfferDetails with the extracted offerId.
   - 1.4 While data is loading, a loading state is shown.
   - 1.5 When data is received, the page stores the offer object, discountLabel, isCurrentlyActive, applicableServices, and termsSummary in page state.
   - 1.6 The page determines an initial selectedServiceId (for example, the first item in applicableServices, if present).
   - 1.7 The offer title, discount details, terms, and applicable services are rendered.

2. User reviews the offer details and eligibility:
   - 2.1 The user sees the offer title and short/long descriptions.
   - 2.2 The user sees discountLabel and, when provided, discountPercent and/or promotionalPrice.
   - 2.3 The user sees isCurrentlyActive reflected in a status indicator (e.g., 'Active' or 'Expired/Unavailable').
   - 2.4 The user reviews termsSummary and the list of applicable services, including each serviceName and category.

3. User selects which service to book (if multiple applicable services):
   - 3.1 If applicableServices contains more than one service, the page displays a selector (such as a dropdown or radio list) listing each serviceName.
   - 3.2 The default selection is the initially determined selectedServiceId.
   - 3.3 When the user changes the selection, the selectedServiceId in page state is updated.
   - 3.4 The booking CTA will use the currently selected serviceId when clicked.

4. User proceeds to schedule with offer attached:
   - 4.1 If isCurrentlyActive is true and there is at least one applicable service, the 'Schedule with Offer' button is enabled.
   - 4.2 When the user clicks the button, the page reads the current selectedServiceId and the loaded offer.id.
   - 4.3 The browser navigates to schedule_service.html with URL parameters:
        - serviceId={selectedServiceId}
        - offerId={offer.id}
        - bookingType=promotion_booking
   - 4.4 The schedule_service.html page can then preselect the service and apply the offer using these parameters.

5. User navigates back to all offers in the same category:
   - 5.1 A 'Back to all offers' link is shown once offer details are loaded.
   - 5.2 When clicked, the page reads offer.category.
   - 5.3 The browser navigates to specials.html?category={offer.category}.

6. Error and inactive-offer workflows:
   - 6.1 If offerId is missing from the URL:
        - 6.1.1 The page shows an error message indicating the offer cannot be loaded.
        - 6.1.2 The booking CTA is hidden or disabled.
        - 6.1.3 A link to specials.html (without category) may be shown so the user can select another offer.
   - 6.2 If getSpecialOfferDetails indicates an error (e.g., offer not found):
        - 6.2.1 An error state is displayed.
        - 6.2.2 The booking CTA is hidden or disabled.
        - 6.2.3 A link back to specials.html is provided.
   - 6.3 If isCurrentlyActive is false:
        - 6.3.1 The page shows that the offer is no longer active.
        - 6.3.2 The booking CTA is disabled or hidden.
        - 6.3.3 The user can still navigate back to specials.html to view other offers.
interactions:
Page load (onInit):
   - Trigger: Browser loads offer_detail.html.
   - Action: Read offerId from the URL query string. If present, call getSpecialOfferDetails(offerId). If not present, set an error state.

Service selector change:
   - Trigger: User changes the selected service in the service selector (when multiple applicableServices exist).
   - Action: Update selectedServiceId in page state to the newly selected serviceId. Reflect the change in any dependent UI (e.g., labels near the CTA).

Click 'Schedule with Offer' CTA:
   - Trigger: User clicks the main booking button when isCurrentlyActive is true and a selectedServiceId is defined.
   - Action: Navigate to schedule_service.html with query parameters serviceId={selectedServiceId}, offerId={offer.id}, bookingType=promotion_booking.

Click 'Back to all offers' link:
   - Trigger: User clicks the back link.
   - Action: Navigate to specials.html?category={offer.category}. If offer.category is not available, navigate to specials.html without query parameters.

Retry loading offer details:
   - Trigger: In an error state (e.g., network error), user clicks a 'Retry' button.
   - Action: Re-read offerId from the URL and call getSpecialOfferDetails(offerId) again.

Internal state updates on API response:
   - Trigger: getSpecialOfferDetails resolves successfully.
   - Action: Store the returned offer, discountLabel, isCurrentlyActive, applicableServices, and termsSummary in page state. Set selectedServiceId to a default based on applicableServices (if any). Update view components bound to these values.

Handling inactive or non-applicable offers:
   - Trigger: API response indicates isCurrentlyActive is false or applicableServices is empty.
   - Action: Display appropriate status and disable or hide the booking CTA while keeping navigation back to specials.html available.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the offerId query parameter in the page URL. On initialization, the page reads offerId from window.location (e.g., offer_detail.html?offerId={id}). If offerId exists, the page calls getSpecialOfferDetails(offerId); otherwise, it sets a 'missingOfferId' error state.

The response from getSpecialOfferDetails updates in-memory page state with:
- offer: the SpecialOffer object (including id, title, descriptions, category, discount fields, dates, etc.).
- discountLabel: a preformatted string describing the discount.
- isCurrentlyActive: boolean controlling whether the booking CTA is enabled.
- applicableServices: array of objects containing serviceId, serviceName, and category, which represent the services to which this offer applies.
- termsSummary: human-readable summary of terms.

The page maintains a derived state property selectedServiceId. Its value is set after data load:
- If applicableServices has at least one item, selectedServiceId defaults to the serviceId of the first item.
- If the user interacts with the service selector, selectedServiceId is updated to the chosen serviceId.
- If applicableServices is empty or undefined, selectedServiceId remains undefined, and the booking CTA is disabled.

The combination of offerId from the URL, isCurrentlyActive from the API, and selectedServiceId from user choice determines the availability and behavior of the 'Schedule with Offer' CTA. When the CTA is clicked, client-side navigation generates a new URL for schedule_service.html that includes:
- serviceId={selectedServiceId}
- offerId={offer.id}
- bookingType=promotion_booking

The 'Back to all offers' navigation uses offer.category (when available) to construct specials.html?category={offer.category}. No state is persisted in localStorage or other long-term storage; all state is either in-memory for the current page session or encoded directly in URL query parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the offerId from the URL and fetching the offer details via getSpecialOfferDetails. Manages loading, success, and error states for the rest of the page.
event_handlers: onInit: Read offerId from URL query parameters and, if present, call getSpecialOfferDetails(offerId).
onLoadSuccess: Receive { offer, discountLabel, isCurrentlyActive, applicableServices, termsSummary } and update shared page state with these values.
onLoadError: Set an error flag and error message in page state to be consumed by offer-status-messages and to disable booking actions.
onRetryClick: Re-trigger getSpecialOfferDetails using the same offerId when the user requests a retry.

components.functionality: Displays contextual status messages about the offer, including loading, error, inactive, and success states. Controls whether primary actions should appear enabled to the user.
event_handlers: onStateChange: When loader state (loading, error, loaded) or isCurrentlyActive changes, update displayed status text and styling.
onMissingOfferId: When offerId is not present in the URL, render a specific message and suggest navigation back to specials.html.

components.functionality: Shows the main identifying information of the offer, including title, shortDescription, discountLabel, and the active/inactive state. Helps the user quickly confirm what the promotion is.
event_handlers: onOfferDataChange: When offer or discountLabel change, re-render the header content.

components.functionality: Displays the detailed discount information for the offer, including discountType, discountPercent, promotionalPrice, minimumPurchaseAmount, and any associated date range, allowing the user to verify the value of the promotion.
event_handlers: onOfferDataChange: Update the displayed discount fields whenever the bound offer object changes.

components.functionality: Lists all services to which the offer applies and allows the user to choose one when multiple options exist. Maintains the selectedServiceId in page state for use by the booking CTA.
event_handlers: onOfferDataChange: When applicableServices changes, populate the selector options and set default selectedServiceId based on the first available service (if any).
onServiceSelectionChange: Update selectedServiceId in shared page state when the user selects a different service.
onNoApplicableServices: If applicableServices is empty or undefined, display a message that this offer cannot currently be scheduled and ensure the CTA is disabled.

components.functionality: Shows the termsSummary and any additional conditions derived from the offer object so the user understands how and when the discount will be applied to their booking.
event_handlers: onOfferDataChange: Render or update the termsSummary and related conditions whenever offer or termsSummary change.

components.functionality: Provides the primary 'Schedule with Offer' action. Uses selectedServiceId and offer.id to construct navigation to schedule_service.html with the correct query parameters and bookingType=promotion_booking.
event_handlers: onRender: Enable or disable the scheduling button based on isCurrentlyActive and whether selectedServiceId is defined.

onClickScheduleWithOffer: When clicked, read selectedServiceId and offer.id from page state and navigate to schedule_service.html?serviceId={selectedServiceId}&offerId={offer.id}&bookingType=promotion_booking.
onInactiveOffer: If isCurrentlyActive is false, prevent navigation and optionally show an explanatory message instead of triggering the click navigation.

components.functionality: Provides navigation options related to the offer, including a link back to the list of offers filtered by category.
event_handlers: onBackToOffersClick: Navigate to specials.html?category={offer.category} when offer.category is defined; otherwise, navigate to specials.html without parameters.
onOfferDataChange: Update the target URL of the back link if the offer.category changes in state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global cyan announcement bar and white header band (site-wide) at the very top, then this Offer Detail content sits in a centered container beneath. Within the Offer Detail area, the hero-style section mirrors the About layout with a two-column split: the left column focuses on the core offer summary and primary booking actions, while the right column carries the richer detail (discount and applicable services) as a visually substantial card. Below this hero band, more text-heavy content (offer terms) is stacked full-width, followed by a compact navigation links strip at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global cyan announcement bar and white header band (site-wide) at the very top, then this Offer Detail content sits in a centered container beneath. Within the Offer Detail area, the hero-style section mirrors the About layout with a two-column split: the left column focuses on the core offer summary and primary booking actions, while the right column carries the richer detail (discount and applicable services) as a visually substantial card. Below this hero band, more text-heavy content (offer terms) is stacked full-width, followed by a compact navigation links strip at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All Offer Detail components sit inside a centered content container that spans roughly 80–88% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~1100–1200px), aligned to a 12-column grid. The hero section (offer-header-summary + booking actions on the left vs. discount details + services selector on the right) uses approximately a 5/12 left column and 7/12 right column. Vertical padding between major sections (hero band, terms, footer links) follows the mockup: 48–64px of top and bottom padding, with 16–24px gaps between related elements. The cyan announcement and header area above are shallower but visually strong, ensuring the Offer Detail hero still occupies a large, comfortable slice of the viewport.
layout_strategies.content_density: Information is grouped into a few clear, roomy blocks rather than many small ones. The hero area keeps text concise with ample breathing room between the offer title, key badges/status messages, and booking CTAs. The discount and services details are presented in a single, large card on the right rather than scattered pieces. The terms section is text-heavy but uses generous line-height, short paragraphs, and 16–24px spacing between subsections to maintain a light, marketing-style feel rather than a dense legal wall. Overall density stays low to moderate, with whitespace used to separate and emphasize sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s loose Z-pattern. After the user scans the global top bar and header, their eye lands on the top-left of the Offer Detail hero: the offer title and key summary. From there, attention moves right to the prominent discount card and applicable services selector in the right column. The gaze then drops back down and left to the booking actions group, which sits just below the summary and aligns visually with the primary CTAs in the header. Finally, the eye moves downward through the full-width offer terms section and ends on the navigation links at the bottom. Status messages appear just beneath the header summary, briefly capturing attention without disrupting the main Z-pattern. A persistent chat widget (global) in the bottom-left acts as a tertiary, low-contrast anchor.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s cyan announcement strip and white navigation bar, the Offer Detail page content is centered in a wide but contained column on a clean white background. The top portion of the page forms a hero-like band that echoes the original About section: a two-column layout with generous vertical padding. On the left, the offer header summary sits near the top of this band, presenting the offer name and key highlights with status messages tucked just below it, followed by the primary booking actions. This left stack occupies slightly less than half the page width, leaving the right side for a large, visually prominent card that combines the discount details and applicable services selector, framed with plenty of white space to feel co-primary with the heading. Below this hero band, the layout collapses back into a single centered column: a roomy offer terms section spans most of the container width, formatted like a friendly, readable content block. At the very bottom of the main content area, a slim navigation links row provides secondary navigation or related links, aligned with the content container and separated by clear vertical spacing. The overall impression matches the mockup’s approachable, playful brand: bright accents, rounded CTAs, and air between elements create a light, local-service marketing feel rather than a dense application screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay on top of the entire Offer Detail content area (starting just below the global header). It covers the full width of the centered content container and extends from the top of the hero section down to the bottom of the page content. At the center of the viewport, a compact, rounded loading card (about 280–320px wide) with a spinner and short loading message is vertically and horizontally centered, dimming the underlying two-column hero and lower sections without fully obscuring them. When active, it temporarily becomes the primary focal point, but it respects the existing page framing by not covering the global cyan bar or main navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay relative to the viewport (but constrained to the content area), centered both horizontally and vertically, appearing above all other components with a translucent backdrop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Status messages sit near the top of the Offer Detail hero, directly beneath the offer header summary’s main title block in the left column. They align flush with the left edge of the content column and follow the 5/12 width of that column on desktop. Each message appears as a full-width pill or banner within that column, with 8–16px vertical spacing separating multiple messages. There is roughly 12–16px of vertical gap between the bottom of the header summary text and the first status message, and about 16–24px of gap below the last message before the booking actions group. This placement keeps them close to the core offer information while not displacing the primary discount illustration on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column flow, expanding vertically as needed and pushing the booking actions downward while preserving the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The offer header summary anchors the top-left of the hero section, echoing the mockup’s bold 'About°' heading area. It sits inside the centered content container, starting roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the global header band. It spans about 5/12 of the container width, aligned to the left grid edge. The main offer title appears near the top of this block with generous whitespace above and below (16–24px), followed by a short, readable description or key bullets. Overall, this summary block occupies roughly the upper half of the left column’s vertical space in the hero band, visually balancing the larger illustration/detail card on the right. Its right edge aligns with the internal grid gutter, creating a clean vertical line separating it from the right-hand card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned as the first component in the Offer Detail content flow, defining the top-left anchor of the two-column hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discount details are placed in the right column of the hero section, visually echoing the large penguin illustration card from the mockup. This component appears as the upper portion of a tall, rounded card that spans roughly 7/12 of the container width on desktop and is aligned to the right edge of the content grid. It starts vertically aligned with the offer header summary’s first text baseline, giving a tidy horizontal alignment across the hero. Inside the card, the discount headline (e.g., 'Save 20%') is positioned toward the top with ample padding (24–32px) on all sides, and supporting details follow beneath with comfortable line spacing. The card has generous white padding and possibly a light shadow or border to feel substantial yet airy. Visually, it occupies about 60% of the hero section’s height, with space below for the applicable services selector to appear as a continuation or lower segment of the same card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the right side of the centered content container, sharing a vertical card structure with the services selector on larger screens and stacking above it on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applicable services selector is positioned directly beneath the discount details within the same right-hand zone, reading as either the lower half of a single tall card or as a closely stacked secondary card with a small 8–16px gap. It maintains the same 7/12 width and right alignment as the discount details. On desktop, its top edge sits about 16–24px below the last line of the discount text (or the bottom of the discount card section), sharing consistent side padding to create a unified vertical block. Controls (chips, checkboxes, or a dropdown) are laid out with plenty of breathing space, avoiding overcrowding. This makes the entire right column feel like a structured 'offer configuration' area that balances against the textual summary and actions on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, expanding vertically based on options; on narrow viewports it stacks below the discount details and eventually below the left column in a single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The offer terms section sits below the hero band as a full-width content block within the centered container, much like a second, simpler section following the About area. It begins roughly 48–64px beneath the lowest element of the hero (typically the bottom of the right-hand card or booking actions), with a clear horizontal separator implied by whitespace rather than heavy rules. The section spans roughly 80–88% of the page width, centered, and uses a single-column text layout. A small heading (e.g., 'Terms & Conditions') is aligned to the left, with 16–24px of space before the body copy begins. Paragraphs and bullet lists have generous line-height and 12–16px spacing between them, resulting in a low-density, readable legal/terms block. The vertical height of this section can grow substantially on longer terms pages but still feels airy due to padding and whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly below the hero section and above the navigation links, expanding vertically as needed while maintaining consistent side margins with the hero content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking actions group is placed in the left column of the hero section, immediately beneath the status messages (or directly under the header summary if no messages are present). It maintains the same 5/12 width and left alignment as the header summary, forming a clean vertical column. The primary CTA button (e.g., 'Schedule with this offer') sits on the left within this area, spanning roughly one-third to one-half of the column width, with generous padding and a rounded shape that echoes the mockup’s 'CALL NOW' and 'SUBSCRIPTIONS' buttons. Secondary actions (e.g., 'Call now', 'Save for later') are aligned to the right of or below the primary button with 8–16px horizontal or vertical spacing. There is 24–32px of padding above the top button, separating it from the text/status, and 32–40px of space below, which helps visually anchor the left column and balance the height of the right-hand card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, following the header summary and status messages; no sticky behavior, but its prominent styling and position near the fold keep it highly visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links appear at the bottom of the Offer Detail content as a slim, low-density strip. Positioned roughly 40–56px beneath the end of the offer terms section, they are aligned within the same centered content container and span the full width of that container. Links are arranged horizontally where space allows, starting from the left (e.g., 'Back to offers', 'View all promotions', 'Contact us'), with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between items. On smaller screens, these links wrap into one or two lines while maintaining left alignment. Vertical padding is modest—about 16–24px top and bottom—so this area reads as a light footer-like navigation band rather than a heavy section, visually signaling the end of the Offer Detail page but staying within the playful, uncluttered style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the final block in the main document flow above any global site footer, with responsive wrapping behavior for the link list.

name: Service Detail
title: Service Detail
description: Displays details for a specific HVAC service and supports standard scheduling, inline emergency requests, and consultation requests, driven entirely by API data and URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for the selected service based on the serviceId URL parameter using getServiceDetails.
Conditionally show standard booking call-to-action that navigates to schedule_service.html with the current service preselected.

Provide an inline emergency request workflow for services where emergencyOptions.isEmergencyAvailable is true, including ZIP validation, date selection, after-hours time-slot selection, and submission via createBooking.

Provide an inline consultation request workflow for services where consultationOptions.hasConsultationOption is true, with a constrained date picker (using consultationOptions) and time-of-day selection, and submission via createBooking.
Fetch and display related special offers and related articles for the current service using getServiceDetails, with navigation to specials.html and article.html via URL parameters.
Use the optional mode URL parameter to open the appropriate interaction view by default (standard, emergency, or consultation).
Use the optional bookingId URL parameter to fetch and display booking confirmation details via getBookingDetails after an inline emergency or consultation request.
Validate serviceability of ZIP codes before showing or submitting emergency or consultation bookings using validateServiceAreaByZip.
Fetch available time slots for the selected service, date, and ZIP (for emergency and consultations) using getAvailableTimeSlots, and bind the user’s time-slot selection into createBooking.
user_workflows:
View service information:
1. User lands on service_detail.html with a serviceId URL parameter.
2. Page-state logic reads serviceId (and optional mode and bookingId).
3. The page calls getServiceDetails(serviceId).
4. On success, the service information (name, descriptions, pricing guidance, duration) is shown.
5. Related special offers and related articles from the getServiceDetails response are listed.
6. The active interaction view (standard, emergency, consultation) is set based on the mode URL parameter or a default.

Standard scheduling (via unified booking page):
1. User is viewing a service with hasStandardBooking = true.
2. User clicks the standard scheduling call-to-action button.
3. Browser navigates to schedule_service.html with URL parameters serviceId = current serviceId and bookingType = 'service'.
4. The schedule_service.html page (not defined here) handles the rest of the booking flow.

Inline emergency request workflow:
1. User lands on service_detail.html with a serviceId whose getServiceDetails response indicates emergencyOptions.isEmergencyAvailable = true.
2. The mode URL parameter may be 'emergency', or the user manually switches to the emergency view.
3. The emergency form is displayed, including fields for ZIP, date, time-slot selection (driven by emergencyOptions and/or getAvailableTimeSlots), problem description, and contact information.
4. When the user enters or changes ZIP, the page calls validateServiceAreaByZip(zip).
5. If validateServiceAreaByZip returns isServiceable = false, an error state is shown and the form submission is disabled for that ZIP.
6. Once the ZIP is serviceable and a date is selected, the page calls getAvailableTimeSlots with parameters derived from the current service and form state (serviceId, date, bookingType = 'emergency', serviceLevel chosen for this flow, zip, durationMinutes from service, and any applicable productId/offerId values).
7. Available timeSlots from getAvailableTimeSlots are displayed to the user, typically limited to evening or after-hours labels based on the business rules and emergencyOptions.
8. User selects a time slot and completes required fields.
9. On form submission, the page calls createBooking with bookingType = 'emergency', serviceLevel corresponding to the emergency flow, serviceId = current service, appointmentStart/appointmentEnd/timeWindowLabel derived from the selected time slot, address/contact fields, and problemDescription.
10. If createBooking returns success = true, the page updates its internal booking confirmation state and may update the URL to include bookingId = returned bookingId (without reloading).
11. The emergency form view is replaced or augmented with a confirmation summary bound to the returned summary/confirmationNumber/status.
12. If the user reloads the page with the bookingId parameter present, the booking confirmation workflow below applies.

Consultation request workflow (indoor air quality or other consultable services):
1. User lands on service_detail.html for a service whose getServiceDetails.consultationOptions.hasConsultationOption = true.
2. The user either arrives with mode = 'consultation' in the URL or switches to the consultation view via a tab/toggle.
3. The consultation form is shown with a date picker constrained according to consultationOptions (maxDaysAhead and weekdaysOnly) and an afternoon time-of-day dropdown sourced from consultationOptions.timeOfDayOptions.
4. User enters ZIP; the page calls validateServiceAreaByZip(zip).
5. If isServiceable = false, an error is shown and submission is disabled until a valid ZIP is entered.
6. When the user selects a valid date and a time-of-day option, the page calls getAvailableTimeSlots with parameters (serviceId, date, bookingType = 'consultation', serviceLevel = 'standard' or another value appropriate for consultations, zip, durationMinutes from service, and any relevant productId/offerId).
7. The timeSlots returned are filtered or highlighted to respect the selected preferred time of day (using the time-of-day option value and timeSlots.isPreferred flags, if provided).
8. User selects a specific time slot and enters name, phone, email, and message.
9. On submission, the page calls createBooking with bookingType = 'consultation', serviceLevel appropriate for consultations, serviceId, appointmentStart/appointmentEnd/timeWindowLabel from the selected slot, preferredTimeOfDay based on the selected time-of-day option, ZIP and address/contact data, and message.
10. On success, the page updates booking confirmation state and may update the URL with bookingId.
11. The consultation form view is replaced or augmented with confirmation information from the createBooking response.

View related special offers from a service:
1. User is viewing a service.
2. The page already has relatedOffers from getServiceDetails.
3. A 'View related offers' interaction or item is available, using the current service.category (or a mapped value) as the specials.html category parameter.
4. When the user clicks this interaction, the browser navigates to specials.html with category set appropriately for the current service (for example, using a mapping from Service.category to SpecialOffer.category).
5. specials.html (not defined here) displays the offers filtered by that category.

Open related blog articles:
1. User is viewing a service and sees a list of relatedArticles returned by getServiceDetails.
2. The user clicks a related article title.
3. Browser navigates to article.html with articleId set to the selected BlogArticle.id.
4. article.html (not defined here) loads the requested article.

Booking confirmation via bookingId URL parameter:
1. After a successful inline emergency or consultation booking, the page may update the URL to include bookingId without reloading.
2. If a user later returns to service_detail.html with both serviceId and bookingId set, the page calls getBookingDetails(bookingId) in addition to getServiceDetails(serviceId).
3. The booking confirmation component displays booking details (booking fields, serviceName, productName, offerTitle) while still showing the service information.
4. The user can review their booked time window and status without creating a new booking.
5. If the user starts another booking from the same page, new calls to getAvailableTimeSlots and createBooking are made without affecting the existing bookingId unless explicitly updated.
interactions: On page load, read serviceId, mode, and bookingId URL parameters and call getServiceDetails(serviceId). Optionally call getBookingDetails(bookingId) if bookingId is present.
Click on a mode/tab control (e.g., 'Service Info', 'Emergency Service', 'Consultation') updates the active view state and updates the mode URL parameter using history APIs without reloading the page.
Click on the standard 'Schedule Service' button navigates to schedule_service.html, including serviceId and bookingType parameters in the URL.

Typing or changing ZIP in emergency or consultation forms triggers validateServiceAreaByZip(zip); the result controls whether the form can be submitted and whether date/time-slot selection is enabled.

Selecting a date and other required context (serviceId, booking type, serviceLevel, zip) in emergency or consultation forms triggers getAvailableTimeSlots, populating a time-slot selector with returned timeSlots.
Selecting a time slot in emergency or consultation forms binds that slot’s start, end, and label values into the booking payload for createBooking.
Submitting the emergency form triggers validation of required fields and, if valid and ZIP is serviceable, calls createBooking with bookingType and serviceLevel for emergency/after-hours service.

Submitting the consultation form triggers validation of required fields and, if valid and ZIP is serviceable, calls createBooking with bookingType and serviceLevel for consultations, including preferredTimeOfDay.

On successful createBooking, the page updates its internal confirmation state and optionally updates the URL with bookingId, then renders a confirmation area using data from the createBooking response.

On detection of a bookingId parameter (either at page load or after updating the URL), a call to getBookingDetails(bookingId) is made and the resulting booking data is bound to a booking confirmation component.
Clicking any related offer’s 'view offers' link navigates to specials.html with a category parameter derived from the current Service.category via a mapping function.
Clicking a related article title navigates to article.html with the corresponding articleId parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. The serviceId parameter is mandatory for the main view; if absent or if getServiceDetails fails, the page shows an error state and does not display booking flows. The mode parameter controls which interaction view is active by default: when mode = 'emergency', the emergency booking tab/section is activated; when mode = 'consultation', the consultation section is shown; otherwise a standard service information view is active. When the user manually switches between modes via tabs or toggles, the page updates the mode parameter in the URL using history.pushState or history.replaceState so that a reload or shared link retains the chosen view. The optional bookingId parameter indicates that an existing booking should be loaded for confirmation: if bookingId is present, the page calls getBookingDetails(bookingId) and binds the resulting booking to a confirmation component, regardless of the current mode. After a successful createBooking call from either the emergency or consultation forms, the page may add or update the bookingId parameter in the URL to the new bookingId without reloading, ensuring that refresh or navigation back to this URL reconstructs the confirmation via getBookingDetails. Internal UI state (for example, which time slot is selected or whether ZIP is validated) is kept in in-memory variables or component state; it does not rely on localStorage or sessionStorage. All data required for display is loaded dynamically from the assigned interfaces without hardcoded datasets, and any navigation to schedule_service.html, specials.html, or article.html relies only on parameters derived from current API responses and URL state.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (serviceId, mode, bookingId), coordinates initial data loading via getServiceDetails and getBookingDetails, manages which main view (standard, emergency, consultation) is active, and synchronizes these states back into the URL when user interactions occur.

Calls getServiceDetails with the current serviceId to fetch the Service entity, emergencyOptions, consultationOptions, relatedOffers, and relatedArticles. Exposes this data to child components and handles error states if the service cannot be loaded or is inactive.

Displays key details of the selected service, including the service name, short and long descriptions, typical base pricing guidance, estimated duration, and metadata needed by other components (e.g., durationMinutes, category, flags for hasStandardBooking, isEmergencyAvailable, hasConsultationOption). Does not handle booking itself but surfaces data to other components.

Provides a 'Schedule Service' button when Service.hasStandardBooking is true. On activation, it navigates to schedule_service.html with URL parameters serviceId = current Service.id and bookingType = 'service'. It does not call any APIs directly but uses the service information already loaded.

Provides controls (such as tabs or toggles) to switch the current view between standard service info, emergency booking (if emergencyOptions.isEmergencyAvailable), and consultation booking (if consultationOptions.hasConsultationOption). It uses the mode URL parameter as the source of truth on initial load and updates the URL as the user switches modes.

Shared helper component for the emergency and consultation forms. Accepts user-entered ZIP, calls validateServiceAreaByZip, and exposes validation status, service area name, city/state, and any returned message. Other components use its state to enable/disable date/time-slot selection and booking submission.

Given a serviceId, selected date, bookingType, serviceLevel, validated ZIP, service.durationMinutes, and optional productId/offerId, this component calls getAvailableTimeSlots and maintains a list of available timeSlots, including their start, end, label, isAvailable, and isPreferred flags. It is reused by both emergency and consultation booking forms.

Shows and processes the emergency/after-hours booking form when emergencyOptions.isEmergencyAvailable is true and the current mode is emergency. It collects ZIP, date, time-slot selection (based on emergencyOptions and the available-time-slots-loader), problem description, street address (if required), and contact information. It uses zip-validation-control to ensure the ZIP is serviceable and available-time-slots-loader to fetch time slots with bookingType set to the emergency type and appropriate serviceLevel. On submission, it calls createBooking and, on success, notifies the page-state-manager to update bookingId and confirmation state.

Displays and processes the consultation request form when consultationOptions.hasConsultationOption is true and the current mode is consultation. It enforces date selection rules (weekdays only and maxDaysAhead) and uses consultationOptions.timeOfDayOptions for the time-of-day dropdown. It uses zip-validation-control for ZIP validation and available-time-slots-loader for getAvailableTimeSlots with bookingType corresponding to consultations and an appropriate serviceLevel. The user selects a concrete time slot and enters name, phone, email, and message. On submission, it calls createBooking with bookingType and serviceLevel for consultations, including preferredTimeOfDay based on the selected time-of-day option, and then passes the resulting bookingId and summary to the page-state-manager.

Displays booking confirmation details for either emergency or consultation bookings. It can consume data directly from the createBooking response (summary, confirmationNumber, status) immediately after a booking, or from getBookingDetails when a bookingId URL parameter is present on load. It shows the serviceName, bookingType, serviceLevel, appointment time window, status, and any applied offer title or discount label, without creating new bookings.

Displays special offers related to the current service using the relatedOffers array from getServiceDetails. It also contains a control (for example, 'View all offers for this service') that navigates to specials.html with a category parameter derived from the current Service.category via a configurable mapping logic.

Lists relatedArticles from getServiceDetails for the current service. Each item is clickable and navigates the user to article.html with articleId set to the selected BlogArticle.id. This enables flows from service details into educational content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow that assumes the global cyan announcement bar and white header band are already present. Immediately below the header, the Service Detail page begins with a full-width hero-like section: a two-column layout where the left column contains the service information (title, description, key details) and the right column functions as an interactive booking panel (primary CTA, tabs, forms). Below this main band, secondary content such as related offers and articles is grouped into another full-width section with a two-column split (offers on the left, articles on the right), mirroring the About section’s side-by-side layout from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow that assumes the global cyan announcement bar and white header band are already present. Immediately below the header, the Service Detail page begins with a full-width hero-like section: a two-column layout where the left column contains the service information (title, description, key details) and the right column functions as an interactive booking panel (primary CTA, tabs, forms). Below this main band, secondary content such as related offers and articles is grouped into another full-width section with a two-column split (offers on the left, articles on the right), mirroring the About section’s side-by-side layout from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Content is constrained to a centered max-width container on larger screens (e.g., ~1120–1200px), sitting within full-bleed white sections. The hero/service section uses roughly a 5/12 (40–45%) left column for text and 7/12 (55–60%) right column for the booking card. Vertical section padding is generous—around 48–64px top and bottom—while internal spacing between components (headlines, forms, lists) uses 16–24px increments. The booking panel is visually substantial so it feels co-primary with the service title, but its height is managed via grouping and whitespace to avoid looking dense.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low to moderate, echoing the mockup: short, readable text blocks, comfortable line spacing, and plenty of white space around all interactive elements. Forms are broken into clear, vertically separated fields with 16–24px gaps. Tabs, CTAs, and related content cards are given breathing room with 24–32px padding inside their containers. No element is edge-to-edge within the content container; everything sits in rounded cards or open white space with consistent padding, keeping the playful, friendly feel intact.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern. The user’s eye starts in the upper-left with the service title and summary, then moves right to the bold standard booking CTA at the top of the booking panel. From there, the eye travels down across the interaction-mode tabs and into the active form (schedule, emergency, or consultation). When a booking is completed, a bright, shallow confirmation banner appears near the top of the content area, briefly taking over as the primary focal point similar to the cyan promo bar in the mockup. Scrolling down, the eye drops left again to related offers and then sweeps right to related articles, maintaining the left-to-right rhythm and reinforcing the idea of next-best actions after viewing or booking the service.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global cyan announcement bar and white header band, the Service Detail page opens into a spacious white hero section centered on the screen. Within a wide but bounded content column, the layout splits into two: on the left, a bold service title and a concise, friendly description anchor the page, with key service details stacked beneath. On the right, a rounded, card-like booking panel in a subtle light accent sits at equal visual weight, topped by a large, rounded primary CTA and a horizontal tab strip for switching between standard scheduling, emergency help, and consultation requests. Each mode reveals a clean, low-clutter form framed by padding and white space. When a booking succeeds, a bright, slim confirmation banner appears just under the header across the content width, echoing the visual strength of the top cyan bar without overwhelming the page. As the user scrolls past the booking area, the layout transitions into another full-width section with a two-column split: related offers appear as a small set of playful, card-like tiles on the left, while a vertically stacked list of related articles lines up on the right. The page maintains generous vertical spacing between all sections and components, a calm white background, and rounded, colorful CTAs that echo the approachable, local-service character of the brand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not render a visible UI. Conceptually, it wraps the entire Service Detail content area that starts just below the global header and any cyan promo bar. It spans the full available page width but has no visual footprint—no padding, borders, or background. All other visible components (hero section, booking panel, related content) are children within this logical container and inherit its centered, max-width layout behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural only; behaves as an invisible top-level wrapper with no spatial impact on the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this component appears as a semi-translucent white overlay covering the entire Service Detail content area from just below the header down to the bottom of the viewport. It stretches the full width of the centered content container and vertically at least one viewport height, dimming underlying components. In the center of this overlay, a playful, brand-aligned loading indicator (e.g., a small penguin-themed spinner or skeleton blocks that mimic the two-column hero layout) appears, with generous padding around it so it feels light and unobtrusive. When loading completes, the overlay fades out to reveal the two-column hero layout underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily positioned as a fixed or absolute overlay above all other page components within the content area, without changing their underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service info section is the topmost visible content block of the Service Detail page. It sits inside a full-width white band starting approximately 48–64px below the global header, with 48–64px padding at the top and bottom to create a roomy hero feel. Within the centered content container, it aligns to the left and occupies roughly the left 5 of 12 grid columns (around 40–45% of the container width). At the top of this column, a large, friendly service title spans the full column width, followed by a short paragraph or two of description, each with 16–24px vertical spacing. Below that, compact service metadata (e.g., duration, price range, rating, or service badges) is laid out either as a single row of labeled chips or a short list, again with comfortable breathing room. Visually, this column feels balanced against the booking panel on the right, with a clear gutter of at least 32px between them. On smaller screens, this section stacks above the booking column, expanding to nearly 100% of the content width while maintaining similar vertical padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow; participates in the two-column grid on medium and large screens and stacks to full-width above the booking panel on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The standard booking CTA is placed prominently at the top-right side of the hero area, inside the booking panel column. It sits within a rounded card that occupies roughly the right 7 of 12 grid columns (about 55–60% of the container width), aligned to the top edge of the service-info section’s column. The button itself is a large, pill-shaped element in a bright brand accent color (e.g., warm red or cyan), horizontally centered within the card or aligned slightly to the left edge for a more utilitarian feel. It appears about 16–24px below the top inner padding of the booking card, with ample whitespace above and below so it doesn’t feel cramped. To reflect the playful brand, the button has generous horizontal padding (~16–24px) and vertical padding (~12–16px), with clear, bold text like “Schedule This Service”. On smaller screens, when the layout stacks, this CTA appears immediately below the service info section but remains visually tied to the top of the booking controls, keeping it one of the first interactive elements a user sees.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the booking panel; no fixed behavior, but its position relative to the top of the right column remains consistent across breakpoints (stacked on mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the standard booking CTA, still within the right-hand booking card, the interaction-mode tabs form a horizontal strip that spans nearly the full width of the card. The tabs (e.g., “Schedule”, “Emergency”, “Consultation”) are displayed side by side with equal width segments or compact pill-shaped buttons aligned in a row. They sit about 16–24px below the CTA, with 8–16px of vertical padding inside the tab bar and a subtle bottom border or colored underline indicating the active mode. The strip respects the card’s internal padding, leaving at least 16px from the card’s left and right edges. Visually, the tabs act as a clear divider between the main CTA and the detailed forms, making the card feel organized and approachable. On small screens, the tabs may wrap or become horizontally scrollable but remain directly under the CTA and above the forms, preserving the top-down reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the booking card; controls which form is visible in the space immediately below it without altering overall page layout.
