component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zip validation control sits just below the interaction-mode tabs within the right-hand booking panel, occupying the full usable width of the card (minus 16–24px internal padding on each side). It is a short, single-row form element consisting of a rounded input field for the ZIP code and a compact validate/check button to its right. This block appears with about 16px of vertical spacing from the tabs above and 16–24px spacing before any time slots or additional fields below. Error or success messages appear in a single, short line directly under the input, in small but readable text, with 4–8px spacing. The overall footprint is shallow and visually light, ensuring it doesn’t overwhelm the user before they reach the main booking form. On small screens, the input and button may stack vertically but remain in this same relative position between the tabs and the rest of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline component within the booking card; it expands vertically to accommodate validation messaging but does not impact elements outside the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the standard scheduling mode of the booking panel, the available-time-slots loader occupies a defined block directly beneath the zip validation control and any essential scheduling fields (such as date selection). It spans the full internal width of the booking card and is visually grouped as a low-profile card section with subtle borders or background shading, consistent with the overall flat, friendly aesthetic. When fetching slots, this area shows skeleton placeholders that roughly mimic a grid of time-slot chips—several rounded rectangles arranged in rows with 8–12px gaps—giving a sense of the final layout without heavy visual weight. There is approximately 16–24px vertical space above and below this section to keep it separated from form inputs and buttons, and its height adjusts based on content but stays within the right column’s width. Once loaded, it transforms into the actual time-slot grid in the same footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and updates inline within the scheduling form region; height is flexible based on loading content but constrained to the right column and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency booking form occupies the main content region of the right-hand booking panel when the ‘Emergency’ tab is active. It appears directly beneath the zip validation control (if required for emergency) or beneath the tabs if ZIP is not needed, extending the full internal width of the card. Form fields are arranged in a single column with clear 16–24px vertical spacing between them, keeping the density low and maintaining the clean, approachable feel. Key inputs such as contact details, brief issue description, and preferred contact method are presented in a simple, top-to-bottom sequence, with labels aligned left and any helper text directly beneath inputs. At the bottom of this form area, a strong, full-width emergency submission button appears with slightly higher visual weight (e.g., bold text, accent color) but sized slightly smaller or visually restrained compared to the main standard booking CTA to avoid competition. The entire emergency form block is visually framed by the booking card’s padding, with at least 24px of padding before the card’s bottom edge. Only one form (standard, emergency, or consultation) occupies this space at a time, so the emergency form sits in the same spatial slot as the other modes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mode-dependent content within the right column’s card; it replaces other forms within the same vertical space without affecting the overall right-column position or page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation request form shares the same spatial container as the emergency and standard scheduling forms, appearing when the ‘Consultation’ tab is active. It is laid out as a vertical stack of fields with generous spacing, occupying the full width of the right-hand booking card beneath the tabs and any shared controls (like ZIP validation, if used). The form content is slightly lighter than the emergency form, emphasizing optionality and ease: a few key inputs (contact info, preferred date/time window, notes) are separated with 16–24px vertical gaps and substantial top/bottom padding to avoid visual clutter. A rounded, medium-emphasis submit button (e.g., accent color but less bold than the primary CTA at the top) is placed at the bottom, spanning most of the card width but leaving small side margins. The form’s overall height is similar to other modes so that the booking panel’s vertical footprint doesn’t change dramatically when switching tabs, maintaining a stable layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mode-dependent content within the booking card; it swaps in-place with other forms in the same right-column area and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a booking or request is successfully submitted (regardless of mode), the booking confirmation banner appears as a shallow, full-width band directly above the main hero content within the Service Detail area. It spans the entire width of the centered content container but is visually treated like a colored stripe across the page, echoing the cyan announcement bar at the very top of the site. The banner sits just below the global header and any site-wide cyan promo bar, with minimal vertical offset so it feels anchored near the top. It has a bright, friendly background color (e.g., cyan or soft green), bold confirmation text aligned left, and possibly a subtle icon on the left, with enough padding (12–16px top and bottom, 16–24px left and right) to be noticeable but not bulky. A dismiss or close control floats to the right side. When visible, it becomes the first thing a user’s eye hits before reading the service title again, but its limited height ensures that the two-column service and booking layout below is still partially visible. When dismissed, the underlying layout shifts up slightly but maintains overall proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a static, inline banner directly below the global header; it pushes the hero content down while visible and disappears without leaving empty space when dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related offers panel is placed in a distinct secondary section below the main hero/booking area. After approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace under the booking card and service info section, a new full-width white band begins, again with 48–64px of padding top and bottom. Inside this band, the content container is split into two columns: the related offers panel occupies the left column, this time slightly wider at around 60% of the container width (roughly 7 of 12 grid columns) to allow for a small grid or vertical stack of 2–4 offer cards. At the top of this column, a medium-sized section heading like “Related Offers” sits left-aligned, with 16–24px of spacing separating it from the cards below. Each offer appears as a small, rounded card with playful accent colors, modest padding (16–20px), and plenty of space between cards (16–24px). The panel’s content does not extend wider than the column, preserving a clear gutter between this column and the related articles on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main scroll flow; participates in a two-column layout on medium/large screens and stacks above the related articles list on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: To the right of the related offers panel in the same lower section, the related articles list occupies the remaining column—approximately 40% of the content container’s width (about 5 of 12 grid columns). It aligns with the top of the related offers panel, creating a balanced horizontal line. A concise heading like “Helpful Articles” appears at the top, aligned to the left edge of this column, with 16–24px of vertical space before the list. Articles are presented in a simple, vertical list with 12–16px spacing between items, each item consisting of a title (and optionally a short one-line description) with a subtle underline or right-facing arrow, aligning with the clean and approachable style. The list remains relatively short—3–5 items—to keep density low and maintain generous whitespace around and between entries. On mobile, this entire column stacks below the related offers panel, with the same internal spacing and heading hierarchy preserved, maintaining a clear sequential flow from offers to articles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the lower two-column section; stacks vertically under the related offers panel on small screens while retaining its spacing and alignment.

name: Financing Overview
title: Financing Options
description: Page for viewing, filtering, and sorting available HVAC financing plans, including 0% APR options, and navigating to detailed plan information.

core_features: Retrieve and display a list of active financing plans using getFinancingPlans, including each plan’s name, APR, term length, and eligibility information.
Filter plans by 0% APR only, using the isZeroAprOnly URL parameter to control the filter state and the filters argument passed to getFinancingPlans.
Filter plans by minimum term length in months, using the termMonthsMin URL parameter and passing it through the filters argument to getFinancingPlans.
Sort plans by criteria such as APR or term length using the sortBy URL parameter, forwarding that value to the getFinancingPlans sortBy argument.
Display a dynamic count of plans returned (using the total value from getFinancingPlans) and reflect that count in the UI.
Provide navigation from each listed plan to its detail page financing_plan.html by appending financingPlanId to the URL when users click a plan name or a 'Learn More' action.
Show per-plan eligibility guidance based on eligiblePurchaseTypes (e.g., whether the plan can be used for specific types of purchases), giving users clarity about which plans apply to their project.

Offer an optional project amount input that, when populated by the user, is used to calculate and display example monthly payments for each plan based on its APR and term length, without additional API calls.
user_workflows:
Browse all financing plans:
1. User navigates to financing.html without URL parameters.
2. Page reads absence of isZeroAprOnly, termMonthsMin, and sortBy and initializes default UI filter states (no 0% APR restriction, no minimum term filter, default sort order).
3. Page calls getFinancingPlans with an empty or neutral filters object and default sortBy value.
4. The returned plans and total are rendered in the plan list.
5. User can click any plan name or its 'Learn More' link to navigate to financing_plan.html?financingPlanId={id}.

Find only 0% APR plans:
1. User navigates to financing.html or arrives from index.html with isZeroAprOnly=true in the URL.
2. Page reads isZeroAprOnly=true from the URL and sets the '0% APR only' control to active.
3. Page calls getFinancingPlans with filters including a zero-APR-only condition derived from the URL parameter and the current termMonthsMin (if any).
4. User reviews the filtered list; total is updated to reflect the number of matching plans.
5. User may further adjust minimum term or sort order, which updates URL parameters and triggers a new getFinancingPlans call.

Filter by minimum term length and sort by APR:
1. User visits financing.html; initial state is loaded as in the first workflow.
2. User enters or selects a minimum term value in months in the filter control.
3. On change or submit, the page updates termMonthsMin in the URL query string and calls getFinancingPlans with filters including that minimum term and the current 0% APR filter state.
4. User selects a sort option (e.g., sort by APR) in the sort control.
5. Page updates the sortBy URL parameter and calls getFinancingPlans again with the same filters and new sortBy value.
6. User inspects the updated plan list and navigates to a chosen plan’s detail page if desired.

Use the page as a financing eligibility and payment reference:
1. User opens financing.html and, if desired, enters an estimated project amount (for example, the expected cost of a new HVAC system) in the project amount input field.
2. Page does not call any additional APIs; instead, it uses the entered project amount together with each plan’s APR and termMonths from the current getFinancingPlans result to calculate an example monthly payment per plan.
3. User can optionally filter to isZeroAprOnly=true and set a termMonthsMin value; getFinancingPlans is called again, and example monthly payments are recalculated on the client using the new plans list and the same project amount.
4. User reviews eligiblePurchaseTypes on each plan to see whether the plan can be used for their intended purchase (e.g., new air conditioner vs. full HVAC system).

Deep-link to specific filtered financing views:
1. A user receives or bookmarks a URL such as financing.html?isZeroAprOnly=true&termMonthsMin={someValue}&sortBy={someSortKey}.
2. When the user visits this URL, the page parses all three parameters and sets UI controls (0% APR toggle, minimum term field, sort dropdown) accordingly.
3. Page calls getFinancingPlans with filters derived from isZeroAprOnly and termMonthsMin, and the sortBy value from the URL.
4. The user sees plans already filtered/sorted according to the shared link and can adjust filters, which updates the URL for further sharing.
interactions: Toggle 0% APR only filter: User interacts with a boolean control (e.g., checkbox or toggle). The component updates the isZeroAprOnly value in the URL query string and triggers a refreshed getFinancingPlans call with filters that request zero-APR-only plans when active.

Set minimum term length: User inputs or selects a minimum term in months. On change or on filters form submission, the termMonthsMin URL parameter is updated and getFinancingPlans is called with a filters object that includes the selected minimum term.

Change sort order: User selects a sort option (e.g., by APR or by term length) from a dropdown. The component updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and triggers getFinancingPlans with the chosen sortBy value.

Enter estimated project amount: User types an estimated project cost into a numeric input. The value is stored in page state (not in the URL unless desired) and used to compute example monthly payments for each plan using APR and termMonths from the current getFinancingPlans response. No additional interface calls are made for this interaction.

View plan eligibility details: User expands or focuses on a plan in the list to see eligiblePurchaseTypes and textual guidance explaining which types of purchases can use that plan. This uses only data returned for that plan; no additional API calls are made.

Navigate to plan detail: User clicks on a plan’s name or a dedicated 'Learn More' action. The component constructs a URL of the form financing_plan.html?financingPlanId={plan.id} and navigates there using standard link navigation.

Refresh plan list via filter form submission: If the filters are wrapped in a form, submitting the form prevents default page reload, updates all relevant URL parameters (isZeroAprOnly, termMonthsMin, sortBy), and then calls getFinancingPlans with a combined filters object containing the current 0% APR flag and minimum term, and the chosen sortBy value.

Handle empty results: If getFinancingPlans returns total equal to zero, the plan list component shows a state indicating no plans match the current filters and provides controls to clear or adjust filters. No assumptions are made about which filters caused the empty result; the logic simply reflects the current URL/filters state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and the response from getFinancingPlans. On initial load, the page reads isZeroAprOnly, termMonthsMin, and sortBy from the URL. If a parameter is present, its value directly sets the corresponding UI control and is used to build the filters and sortBy arguments for getFinancingPlans. If a parameter is absent, the page uses its internal default UI state (e.g., no 0% APR restriction, no minimum term filter, default sort) but does not add any corresponding filter constraints to getFinancingPlans. Whenever the user changes a filter or sort control, the page updates the URL query string using the current UI values: isZeroAprOnly is set or removed based on the 0% APR control; termMonthsMin is set or removed based on the minimum term control; sortBy is updated based on the sort control. After the URL is updated, the page calls getFinancingPlans({ filters, sortBy }) where filters is an object derived solely from isZeroAprOnly and termMonthsMin, and sortBy is the string from the URL/state. The getFinancingPlans response provides plans and total; the plans array populates the list, and total is displayed as the number of available plans. The estimated project amount, if present, is maintained in in-memory page state (and may optionally be reflected in the URL, but this is not required) and is used only for client-side calculations of example monthly payments per plan. No other APIs or persistent storage mechanisms are used; all navigation to detailed plan views is done via query parameters in the destination URL (financing_plan.html?financingPlanId={id}).

components.functionality: Manages financing filter and sort controls, synchronizes them with URL parameters, and triggers retrieval of filtered/sorted financing plans via getFinancingPlans.

Displays the list of financing plans returned by getFinancingPlans, including key attributes such as name, APR, term, eligibility information, and example monthly payment (when available). Handles empty states and uses total to display the number of matching plans.

Represents a single financing plan within the list, showing plan-specific details (name, termMonths, aprPercent, isZeroApr, eligiblePurchaseTypes) and computing an example monthly payment using the current estimated project amount if one has been provided.

Provides contextual guidance about what types of purchases can typically use financing, driven by the eligiblePurchaseTypes values present in the currently loaded plans and describing, in general terms, how financing applies to new systems or other project types.

Allows the user to enter an estimated project or purchase amount and uses that value together with each plan’s termMonths and aprPercent from the current getFinancingPlans response to compute example monthly payments shown in the plan list.
Summarizes the current result set and filters, displaying the total number of plans returned by getFinancingPlans and reflecting the active filter and sort states derived from URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global cyan announcement bar at the very top, then the white header band with logo/nav, then the main Financing Overview content as a series of full-width sections inside a centered content container. The first main section acts as an 'About-style' hero for financing, using a two-column split with guidance text on the left and an interactive calculator card on the right (mirroring the About layout). Below that, a full-width filters + summary band leads into a vertically stacked list of financing plans.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global cyan announcement bar at the very top, then the white header band with logo/nav, then the main Financing Overview content as a series of full-width sections inside a centered content container. The first main section acts as an 'About-style' hero for financing, using a two-column split with guidance text on the left and an interactive calculator card on the right (mirroring the About layout). Below that, a full-width filters + summary band leads into a vertically stacked list of financing plans.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a generous, sectioned layout. The hero-style financing section (guidance + calculator) gets the most vertical space with 48–64px padding top and bottom, and a 40–45% (left) / 55–60% (right) split. The filters/summary section beneath it is medium height, keeping controls visible but not heavy, with about 24–32px vertical padding. The financing plan list occupies the remaining vertical space with a long scroll area; each list item card gets 16–24px padding and 16–24px vertical gaps. Overall content stays within a centered max-width container aligned to the 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-moderate density: large, readable headings; short, scannable descriptive text; form controls spaced out so nothing feels cramped. The hero section feels open and marketing-like. Filters are grouped but not tightly packed, using inline layouts where possible and sufficient breathing room. The list items are information-rich but broken into clear columns/rows with whitespace between key data points, echoing the spacious, friendly feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left of the hero financing guidance (section title, short copy), then moves to the top-right calculator card (primary interactive element). From there it drops down slightly left to the filters band, scans right across filter controls, then down into the results summary and into the first plan card. Within each list item, the eye starts at the left (plan name, key badges) then moves right to APR and the CTA. A persistent bottom-left chat widget (global, not part of these components) anchors the lower-left corner as in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar cyan announcement bar and clean white header with logo and nav, the Financing Overview page opens into a bright, white hero-style section framed inside a centered content container. On the left, a bold, friendly heading explains financing options in a compact panel, while on the right a large, rounded card-style calculator sits as an inviting, colorful block—both aligned on a shared horizontal baseline and padded generously from the top and bottom edges. This two-column band feels airy and marketing-driven. Scrolling down, a full-width filters section spans the container, with filter controls arranged in a shallow, cyan-accented band and a slim results summary bar immediately beneath it, visually attached like a footer to the filters. Beneath this, a vertical stack of financing plan cards fills the page, each card stretching most of the container width with soft corners and clear left-right segmentation of information and CTAs. The overall effect is a light, approachable layout that mirrors the mockup’s About section structure but re-purposed for interactive tools and filtered results, while preserving the playful local-brand character and clear Z-shaped reading path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing usage guidance panel sits at the top of the main content area, forming the left column of the hero-style section directly beneath the global header. It occupies roughly 40–45% of the content container width, aligned flush to the left edge of the grid. The panel itself is mostly text: a large, friendly page title (e.g., “Financing Overview” or similar) at the top, with a short 2–3 line paragraph beneath, both left-aligned. Vertical spacing between heading and body follows the base scale (about 16–24px). There is generous whitespace around this block: approximately 48–64px padding from the top of the hero section to the heading, and a similar amount of space between the bottom of the body text and the section’s bottom edge. The right edge of this panel aligns visually with a vertical grid line, leaving a clear gutter between it and the calculator card to the right. Overall, it reads as a calm, text-first column that balances the more visually heavy calculator, echoing the 'About° on left, illustration on right' pattern from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll, positioned in a fixed two-column hero section that scales responsively: on large screens it remains left-aligned; on smaller breakpoints it stacks above the calculator with centered text but retains generous vertical padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing project amount calculator forms the right column of the same hero section, visually paired with the guidance panel. It occupies roughly 55–60% of the content container width, extending from the central gutter to the right margin. The calculator appears as a prominent card with rounded corners and a subtle shadow or soft border, sitting on the white background so it feels like a distinct, interactive object (similar in visual weight to the penguin illustration card in the mockup). It is vertically centered relative to the guidance panel’s text block, so the top of the card aligns roughly with or slightly below the main heading on the left, while its bottom edge drops a bit lower, making it the visually dominant element in the hero section. Inside the card, form fields are arranged in a vertical stack with clear spacing (12–16px between inputs), and the primary CTA button (e.g., “Calculate”) is positioned at the bottom right of the card, using a bright brand color and pill-shaped style. The card maintains about 24–32px internal padding on all sides, with a bit more space at the top for a mini-title or label. The right edge of the card aligns with the content container’s right boundary, mirroring the logo/nav alignment above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, but on scroll it simply moves off-screen with the section. On smaller screens, it stacks below the guidance panel and expands to near-full container width, maintaining its card styling and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing filters form sits directly below the hero section as the first element of the 'results control' band. It spans nearly the full width of the content container (about 90–100% of the inner width, respecting side margins), centered horizontally. Vertically, it forms a shallow band with 24–32px padding above and below the controls, separated from the hero section by about 32–48px of whitespace. Inside this band, filter controls (e.g., dropdowns for term length, credit score, special offers) are arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens, grouped logically (for example, left cluster for project type/amount filters, right cluster for APR/term filters), following the mockup’s tendency to keep things clean and linear. Each control has at least 16px horizontal spacing from its neighbors to avoid crowding. On smaller screens, these controls wrap or stack into two rows while maintaining a rhythmic, grid-aligned appearance. The background of this band remains white, but with subtle cyan accents (underlines, focus states, or small tags) to connect visually with the top announcement bar. The bottom of the filters band aligns with and visually 'sits on top of' the results summary bar beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially scrolls normally; on larger screens it can become a sticky sub-header for the results area, pinning to the top of the viewport just below the main site header as the user scrolls down the list, preserving easy access to filters without feeling heavy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing results summary appears immediately below the filters form as a slim, full-width bar aligned to the same content container. It shares the same horizontal footprint as the filters (about 90–100% of container width), and visually feels like the footer of the filters section—no extra gap between them, or a very subtle 4–8px separation. The bar height is relatively short, approximately 40–56px tall, with 12–16px top and bottom padding. The background uses a light tint (e.g., a very pale cyan) to distinguish it gently from the white page and to signal its status as a live summary. Text is left-aligned, stating the number of matching plans and perhaps highlighting key filter outcomes (e.g., how many 0% APR options). On the right side of the bar, a compact link or button (e.g., “Clear filters” or “Sort by”) is aligned vertically centered, creating a left-to-right reading flow that echoes the header/nav bar pattern. The top edge of the first plan card aligns with the bottom edge of this summary bar, maintaining a clean visual stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky element tied to the filters: when the filters band becomes sticky at the top of the viewport, the summary bar stays attached immediately below it, forming a combined sticky header for the results list. On small screens where sticky behavior may be constrained, it scrolls normally but retains its compact height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan list occupies the main lower portion of the page, starting directly beneath the results summary bar. It is a vertical stack of card-style list items contained within the same centered content column, spanning approximately 100% of the inner width on mobile and about 80–90% of the inner width on larger screens, leaving comfortable side margins. The top of this section is separated from the summary bar by a modest 16–24px gap, enough to distinguish it but still feel connected. Each list item card is separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, maintaining a consistent rhythm down the page. The list area itself has 32–48px padding at the bottom, providing breathing room before the footer. The background remains white to keep focus on the individual cards, while consistent left alignment of card content creates a clean reading path. This section can extend vertically as long as needed; the sticky filters/summary at the top keep context while users scroll through multiple plans.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main content; the list scrolls under the sticky filters/results-summary band. On smaller devices, the list items stretch close to full width while maintaining consistent margins relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each financing plan list item is presented as a horizontally oriented card within the financing plan list. The card spans most of the container width, aligning left and right with the filters and summary above, and uses a white background with a soft border or subtle shadow and rounded corners to fit the playful, approachable brand. Inside the card, content is arranged in a clear left-right split: the left 60–65% of the card holds the plan name at the top (larger text), followed by 1–2 lines of concise descriptive copy and small badges or tags (e.g., “0% APR”, “No payments for 6 months”) arranged in a horizontal row. The right 35–40% forms a compact summary column: the primary rate or monthly payment is displayed prominently near the top-right, with secondary details (term length, estimated monthly payment) beneath in smaller type, and the primary CTA button (e.g., “View Details” or “Apply”) anchored at the bottom-right. Vertical padding inside the card is about 16–24px, with 16px side padding. The card content is vertically balanced so that the CTA button aligns visually across multiple cards, creating a clean visual column of buttons on the right as users scroll. On hover (desktop), slight elevation or outline change matches the modern, friendly UI language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list; on small screens, the internal layout becomes stacked: key data (name, rate, term) moves to the top, the CTA button spans full width at the bottom, and left/right split collapses into a vertical flow, preserving breathing room and readability.

name: Financing Plan Detail
title: Financing Plan
description: Displays details for a selected financing plan and lets users submit a financing application for an HVAC purchase.

core_features: Load and display full details for a financing plan identified by the financingPlanId URL parameter using getFinancingPlanDetails.
Show key plan attributes such as APR, term length, any purchase amount limits, and eligible purchase purposes based on the FinancingPlan data.
Display detailed terms, eligibility information, and dynamically returned example payment scenarios from getFinancingPlanDetails.
Provide an in-page 'Start Application' / 'Apply Now' action that scrolls or jumps to the application form section.
Render a financing application form bound to the selected plan, capturing purchase amount, purchase purpose, personal and contact information, and optional SSN last 4 digits.
Validate user input against required fields and business rules (including plan-specific min/max purchase amount if provided) before submit.
Submit the financing application via createFinancingApplication, passing all required parameters and the current financingPlanId.
Show success or error feedback after createFinancingApplication, including next steps returned by the interface.
Provide navigation back to the financing options overview (financing.html).
user_workflows: View plan details and decide to apply: 1) User arrives at financing_plan.html with a financingPlanId URL parameter. 2) Page calls getFinancingPlanDetails using financingPlanId. 3) On success, the page shows the plan name, APR, term, eligible purchase types, purchase amount constraints, detailed terms, eligibility summary, and example payment scenarios. 4) User reviews the information, then clicks the 'Start Application' / 'Apply Now' button. 5) Page scrolls to or focuses the application form section tied to the same financingPlanId.

Start application from financing overview or homepage: 1) User clicks a financing plan link on financing.html or index.html that navigates to financing_plan.html?financingPlanId=... . 2) financing_plan.html loads the specified plan via getFinancingPlanDetails. 3) If financingPlanId is invalid or the plan is inactive, the page shows an error state with a link back to financing.html. 4) If valid, the user sees the plan details and can proceed with application.

Submit a financing application: 1) With the plan details visible, the user fills out the application form fields: purchaseAmount, purchasePurpose (chosen from allowed options), name, street address, city, state, ZIP, phone, email, optional SSN last 4 digits, and preferred contact method. 2) On clicking 'Submit Financing Request' / 'Continue Application', client-side validation runs (required fields, numeric checks, and checks against plan.minPurchaseAmount / plan.maxPurchaseAmount when present). 3) If validation fails, the form shows inline errors and prevents submission. 4) If validation passes, the form is disabled and createFinancingApplication is called with the current financingPlanId and all collected field values. 5) On successful response (success = true), the page shows a confirmation message, the application status, the financingApplicationId, and nextSteps. The URL may be updated (e.g., adding applicationStatus and financingApplicationId parameters) to reflect the submitted state. 6) On failure (success = false or network error), the page shows an error message and keeps the user on the form, allowing correction and resubmission.

Return to an application confirmation state via URL: 1) After a successful submit, the page can update the URL (e.g., using history.pushState) to include applicationStatus and financingApplicationId query parameters without reloading. 2) If the user reloads or revisits a URL that includes financingPlanId plus applicationStatus and financingApplicationId, the page first loads plan details via getFinancingPlanDetails, then initializes the page in a 'post-submission' state (showing a confirmation panel) while still allowing the user to view plan terms.
Navigate back to financing options: 1) At any time, the user can click 'Back to financing options'. 2) The browser navigates to financing.html (no additional parameters required by this page).
interactions: Initial load interaction: On page load, the script reads financingPlanId from the query string. If missing, the page immediately shows an error state instructing the user to select a financing plan from financing.html and renders a link to financing.html. If present, the page calls getFinancingPlanDetails(financingPlanId), showing a loading state until the response arrives or an error occurs.

Plan details interaction: When getFinancingPlanDetails returns successfully, plan details populate the summary and terms sections. Example payments are rendered as a list/table using the examplePayments array, each showing purchaseAmount, monthlyPayment, termMonths, and totalPaid. If examplePayments is empty or not provided, the example payments component remains hidden.

Apply button interaction: When the user clicks the 'Start Application' / 'Apply Now' button, the page scrolls smoothly to the application form section and focuses the first input (e.g., purchaseAmount). The button is disabled if plan data is not loaded or if the plan is inactive based on plan.isActive.

Form field interactions: As the user types or changes fields in the application form, basic validation occurs on blur or in real time: purchaseAmount must be a valid number and, if plan.minPurchaseAmount or plan.maxPurchaseAmount are defined, must fall within that range; required text fields must not be empty; email must match an email format pattern; ZIP and phone must satisfy basic format checks; ssnLast4 (if provided) must meet length/format requirements. The purchasePurpose dropdown is populated from plan.eligiblePurchaseTypes if present, with a fallback option when necessary, and supports only values that map to the FinancingApplication.purchasePurpose enum. The preferredContactMethod dropdown allows only the values email, phone, and text.

Form submission interaction: When the user clicks the submit button, the page performs full validation. If any validation fails, the page shows field-specific errors and an optional global error banner, and does not call createFinancingApplication. If validation passes, the submit button and form fields are disabled, a submitting indicator appears, and createFinancingApplication is called with the current financingPlanId and all form values. The ssnLast4 value is sent only if the user has entered it.

Submission response interaction (success): If createFinancingApplication returns success = true, the page shows a confirmation/summary component displaying the response status, financingApplicationId, message, and nextSteps. The page may mark the form as 'submitted' (e.g., hiding or disabling it) while still allowing the user to view their entered values in read-only mode. The script can update the URL query (e.g., adding applicationStatus=submitted and financingApplicationId from the response) without reloading the page, so the state is restorable on reload.

Submission response interaction (failure): If createFinancingApplication returns success = false, or if the call fails due to a network or server error, the page shows an error message component with the returned message when available. The form is re-enabled so the user can correct fields and resubmit. The URL is not updated to a submitted state.
Back navigation interaction: When the user clicks 'Back to financing options', the browser navigates to financing.html. No interface is called by this event from this page.

Error and missing-plan interactions: If getFinancingPlanDetails fails (e.g., plan not found, plan inactive, or network error), a dedicated error component appears, explaining that the requested financing plan cannot be displayed. The application form and apply button are hidden or disabled because no valid plan is available. The error state always includes a link or button back to financing.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state is driven by URL query parameters. financingPlanId is required: financing_plan.html?financingPlanId=... . On load, the page reads financingPlanId. If it is missing or empty, the page enters a 'no-plan-selected' error state and does not call getFinancingPlanDetails. If financingPlanId is present, the page enters a 'loading-plan' state and calls getFinancingPlanDetails(financingPlanId). On success, the state becomes 'plan-loaded', which enables the plan detail sections and the application CTA. On failure, the state becomes 'plan-load-error'. After a successful application submission, the page can update the URL (e.g., using history.pushState) to include applicationStatus and financingApplicationId as additional query parameters: financing_plan.html?financingPlanId=...&applicationStatus=submitted&financingApplicationId=... . These parameters are read on subsequent loads: if applicationStatus indicates a completed submission and financingApplicationId is present, the page initializes in a 'plan-loaded-with-submission' state, in which it still calls getFinancingPlanDetails(financingPlanId) but also renders the confirmation/status component as active. The application form may be shown in read-only or minimized mode in this state, depending on business rules. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; only URL parameters plus live API responses (from getFinancingPlanDetails and createFinancingApplication) determine the page state.

components.functionality: Reads financingPlanId from the URL query string and, when present, invokes getFinancingPlanDetails to fetch the plan, its termsText, examplePayments, and eligibilitySummary. Manages loading, error, and loaded states and exposes the resulting data and state flags to child components.
event_handlers: onInit (reads financingPlanId from URL and calls getFinancingPlanDetails)
onReloadPlan (re-calls getFinancingPlanDetails with current financingPlanId when triggered, e.g., retry after error)

components.functionality: Displays the functional page heading, the name of the loaded financing plan, key attributes such as APR and term length, and a 'Back to financing options' navigation link. If plan loading fails or no plan is selected, shows a simple error title instead of plan-specific information.
event_handlers: onBackClick (navigates to financing.html via standard browser navigation)

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the financing plan using the FinancingPlan fields and supplemental metadata from getFinancingPlanDetails. This includes APR, term length, purchase amount limits if present, whether the plan is zero APR, and eligible purchase purposes. It may also display the eligibilitySummary text for quick scanning.

Displays the full terms and conditions text associated with the financing plan, leveraging termsText from getFinancingPlanDetails. If termsText is unavailable or the plan fails to load, this section remains hidden or shows an appropriate message.

Renders a list or table of example payment scenarios returned by getFinancingPlanDetails. Each item shows the example purchaseAmount, monthlyPayment, termMonths, and totalPaid. If the examplePayments array is empty or not provided, the component does not render.

Provides a prominent 'Start Application' / 'Apply Now' button that allows users to jump to the application form after reviewing plan details. The button is enabled only when a plan is successfully loaded and active.
event_handlers: onApplyClick (scrolls the viewport or sets focus to the financing-application-form component)

components.functionality: Captures all required information to start a financing application for the selected plan. Fields include purchaseAmount, purchasePurpose (dropdown limited to allowed enum values and plan.eligiblePurchaseTypes when available), applicantName, streetAddress, city, state, zip, phone, email, optional ssnLast4, and preferredContactMethod (email, phone, or text). Performs validation and, on successful validation, submits the application via createFinancingApplication.
data_binding: FinancingPlan.name (for internal display or confirmation only)
event_handlers: onInit (pre-populates purchasePurpose options based on FinancingPlan.eligiblePurchaseTypes and enum constraints)
onFieldChange (updates internal form state and clears related validation errors)
onFieldBlur (runs field-level validation for the changed field)

onSubmit (runs full form validation, and on success calls createFinancingApplication with financingPlanId, purchaseAmount, purchasePurpose, applicantName, streetAddress, city, state, zip, phone, email, ssnLast4, preferredContactMethod; disables the form while awaiting response)
onValidationError (displays inline error messages and prevents API call)
onReset (optional; clears fields if allowed, except for plan-bound context)

components.functionality: Shows the result of the latest createFinancingApplication submission, including success or error state. On success, it displays the financingApplicationId, status, message, and nextSteps returned by the interface. On failure, it displays an error message and guidance to retry. It also respects URL parameters like applicationStatus and financingApplicationId when present to initialize in a post-submission state.
event_handlers: onApplicationSubmitted (called after a successful createFinancingApplication response to update this panel's displayed data and to optionally update the URL with applicationStatus and financingApplicationId)
onDismissStatus (optional; hides or collapses the status panel if the user chooses)

components.functionality: Displays an error message when financingPlanId is missing from the URL or when getFinancingPlanDetails fails or returns an inactive plan. Offers a clear link back to financing.html and suppresses the display of application-related components while no valid plan is available.

Centralized component that shows transient informational messages during form submission and API interactions, such as loading indicators while createFinancingApplication is in progress, general error messages when the API call fails, and confirmations when actions succeed. It aggregates status from both getFinancingPlanDetails and createFinancingApplication.
event_handlers: onCloseMessage (clears or hides the current global message)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow under the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header band. The main plan detail page starts with a hero-style section that mirrors the mockup's About layout: a two-column split where the left side contains the financing plan header (title, rate, key info) and the right side contains a visually prominent data block (example payments) acting as the 'illustration card'. Below the hero, content stacks in clearly separated full-width sections: plan summary, plan terms, then an application area where a full-width CTA bar introduces the financing application form and accompanying status panel. System messages sit at logical choke points near the top of the content and around the form.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow under the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header band. The main plan detail page starts with a hero-style section that mirrors the mockup's About layout: a two-column split where the left side contains the financing plan header (title, rate, key info) and the right side contains a visually prominent data block (example payments) acting as the 'illustration card'. Below the hero, content stacks in clearly separated full-width sections: plan summary, plan terms, then an application area where a full-width CTA bar introduces the financing application form and accompanying status panel. System messages sit at logical choke points near the top of the content and around the form.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All content sits in a centered max-width container (approx. 1040–1200px) on a white background, following a 12-column grid. The hero section gives roughly 40–45% of the width to the left column (financing-plan-header) and 55–60% to the right column (example-payments-list), with generous vertical padding (48–64px top and bottom). Summary and terms sections each occupy nearly the full content width but maintain wide side padding. The CTA bar spans the full content width as a distinct band, with medium height. The application form takes about 60–65% of the width on the left in the lower part of the page, with the status panel occupying the remaining 35–40% on the right on larger screens; on small screens they stack. Error and feedback banners are shallow but full-width within the content container. The data loader overlays the whole main content region when active.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-moderate content density. Use short, scannable text blocks and avoid wall-of-text sections. Apply generous vertical spacing between sections (48–64px) and moderate spacing between related elements within a section (16–24px). Cards like the example payments list, CTA bar, form panel, and status panel have roomy internal padding (16–24px) and sufficient breathing room from other elements. Typography is large and readable, and sections are visually separated using whitespace rather than heavy borders.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve a loose Z-pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top-left with the financing-plan-header (plan name and key rate/promo info), then moves right to the more colorful, card-like example-payments-list. From there, the eye drops down to the left again to read the financing-plan-summary-section, sweeps across to the terms, then diagonally down to the application-cta-bar band that visually spans the page. From the CTA bar, the left side of the lower page is dominated by the financing-application-form, while the right side is anchored by the financing-application-status-panel. System messages briefly interrupt this flow only when necessary, appearing above the main content or immediately above the form. Overall, the flow feels simple, marketing-focused, and approachable rather than dense or transactional.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global cyan announcement bar and white header/navigation band, the financing plan detail page opens into a bright, spacious hero section on a white background. Centered within the page’s content width, the hero is split into two columns: on the left, a bold plan title and key details establish what this financing option is; on the right, a colorful, card-like block shows example monthly payments and terms, echoing the mockup’s large illustration card. Beneath this, wide, low-density sections for the plan summary and the detailed terms stack vertically, each with generous breathing room and clear headings. A full-width CTA band then runs across the page, inviting the user to start their application. Directly underneath, the bottom portion of the page forms a subtle two-column layout, with the main application form as a large, friendly card on the left and a compact status/next-steps panel on the right. System banners appear near the top of the content or above the form when needed, while a data-loading overlay can temporarily veil the main layout on first load. The overall effect is a playful yet professional financing detail page that feels light, local, and easy to understand—guiding the user visually from understanding the plan to confidently submitting their application.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-transparent white overlay that covers the entire main content area of the page—from just below the global header band down to the bottom of the viewport. It stretches the full width of the content container and vertically at least one full viewport height, dimming the underlying hero, sections, and form while data is fetched. At its center, both vertically and horizontally, a simple spinner or playful loader graphic is displayed, sized modestly (roughly 48–64px in diameter) with ample empty space around it. Underneath, the hero and other components may be pre-laid out but visually subdued by the overlay, maintaining the sense of a structured page even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-position overlay within the viewport, pinned beneath the global header but above all main content components. It scrolls with the page only if the underlying content is taller than the viewport; otherwise, it appears static until dismissed when loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan header is the left column of the hero-style section at the top of the main content. It is positioned directly below any present system plan-load-error-banner and sits approximately 48–64px beneath the global header band, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. Horizontally, it occupies about 40–45% of the content width (roughly 5 out of 12 columns), leaving a wider right column. The header includes the plan name in a large, friendly typeface, with subtitle text and key highlight badges or short bullet lines stacked below. Vertical spacing between the title, subtitle, and any metadata is generous (16–24px). The top of this column visually aligns with the top of the example-payments-list on the right, creating a balanced two-column hero. Bottom spacing within the hero ensures at least 48px of white space before the next full-width section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal scroll flow, participating in a two-column grid at medium and large breakpoints. On smaller screens, it stacks above the example-payments-list, becoming a full-width element with centered or left-aligned text and additional vertical spacing to keep the hero airy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan summary section sits immediately below the hero region formed by the financing-plan-header and example-payments-list. It is a full-width block within the content container, starting approximately 48–56px beneath the bottom edge of the hero columns. It spans nearly the entire content width (10–12 columns) but maintains comfortable horizontal padding so text never feels edge-to-edge. A bold, medium-sized heading appears at the top-left of this section, with a short paragraph or a few bullet points beneath it, arranged in a single column with 16–24px vertical spacing between elements. The whitespace around the summary keeps it light—roughly 32–48px padding at the top and bottom of the section—before transitioning into the terms section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section in the vertical flow. On all screen sizes it remains a single-column text block, simply adjusting line length and padding for readability on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing plan terms section follows directly beneath the summary section, separated by about 32–48px of vertical whitespace. It also spans the full width of the main content container, mirroring the summary’s alignment for visual consistency. A clear, friendly heading is anchored at the top-left, with the detailed terms presented underneath—either as short paragraphs, a list, or simple rows. Content is arranged in a single column to keep density low, with 12–16px spacing between individual term lines and 16–24px between grouped subsections. The section has substantial top and bottom padding (approximately 48px each), giving the legal or detailed content room to breathe and preventing it from feeling heavy. At the bottom of this section, there is enough whitespace (32–48px) before the application-cta-bar, reinforcing its role as a distinct stage in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block in the stacked layout. On all screen sizes it remains vertically oriented, with potential soft dividers (light lines or subtle background tint rows) used sparingly to preserve the clean, approachable aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The example payments list forms the right column of the hero section, directly to the right of the financing-plan-header. It begins at the same vertical position as the header and extends downward, occupying roughly 55–60% of the content width (about 7 out of 12 columns). Visually, it reads as a prominent card or panel: a softly rounded rectangle with a subtle shadow or border, set on the white background. Inside, example monthly payment amounts and terms are displayed in a simple, friendly layout—perhaps one standout large payment figure near the top and a short list of scenarios or line items below—each separated by 8–16px of vertical space. The card has comfortable internal padding (16–24px on all sides) and is given breathing room around it so it doesn’t crowd the header (at least 24px gutter between the left and right hero columns). The bottom of the card may extend slightly lower than the text column on the left, but the overall hero still feels balanced and anchored by ample whitespace around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the two-column hero. On smaller screens, it stacks directly beneath the financing-plan-header, expanding to full width and maintaining generous top margin (24–32px) so that it still feels like a distinct, prominent card rather than text continuation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application CTA bar appears as a full-width, horizontally oriented band that sits below the terms section and above the financing application form. It is separated from the terms by 40–56px of vertical whitespace, making it a clear transition point from information to action. Within the content container, the bar spans the full width but features a soft background tint (e.g., light cyan) to stand out against the white page, with rounded edges if it’s treated as a pill-like banner. The left side (about two-thirds of the width) contains a short, bold prompt such as 'Ready to apply?' plus a single-line supporting sentence, while the right side (about one-third) aligns a primary rounded CTA button ('Start Application' / 'Apply Now') horizontally and vertically centered within the bar. The height of the bar is medium—approximately 80–120px—keeping content compact but airy, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. There is 32–48px of space between the bottom of this bar and the top of the form section, reinforcing its role as a visual stepping stone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the desktop layout, appearing in the natural scroll flow. On smaller screens, the text and CTA button stack vertically, with the button centered and separated from the text by 16–24px. Optionally, on very small devices the CTA bar can become sticky at the bottom of the viewport while the user scrolls through the form, but it still appears first in its inline position when the user reaches this stage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing application form occupies the main left column of the lower part of the page, directly beneath the application-cta-bar. It is positioned approximately 32–48px below the CTA bar and aligned with the left edge of the content container. On medium and large screens, it takes about 60–65% of the width (roughly 7–8 columns of the grid), forming a large, card-like panel with a white background, subtle shadow, and softly rounded corners to echo the playful brand. Inside, form fields are arranged in a single column with occasional side-by-side pairs for shorter inputs, each row separated by 16–24px vertically. Section headers within the form, if any, are spaced 24–32px above their respective fields. The top of the form clearly shows a concise heading like 'Financing Application' and possibly a short line describing approximate time to complete. There is generous padding inside the card (24–32px) and a comfortable margin at its bottom (48–64px) before the page ends, ensuring the form never feels cramped. In relation to the status panel on the right, the top of the form aligns with or slightly above the top of the status panel, emphasizing the form as the main task area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-column element at wider breakpoints; on small screens it becomes a full-width card stacked above the financing-application-status-panel. Within the form, the submit button is aligned left or centered at the bottom, with ample top margin (24–32px) separating it from the last input field.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The financing application status panel is positioned to the right of the financing-application-form in the lower page layout. On medium and large screens, it occupies roughly 35–40% of the content width (about 4–5 columns) and aligns its top edge with the top of the form, creating a balanced two-column arrangement. It appears as a smaller card with similar styling to the form—white background, soft corners, and mild shadow—so they read as a cohesive pair. Inside, the panel contains concise, high-level information such as application progress, required documents, or a summary of the selected plan. Content is structured in short blocks or bullet points with 12–16px spacing, and overall vertical padding of 16–24px. The panel’s height is shorter than the form in most cases, leaving some whitespace beneath it; this empty area visually emphasizes that the form is the primary interaction. On small screens, the status panel appears directly beneath the form with 24–32px of top margin, spanning 100% width while preserving its card-like shape.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-column sidebar at larger breakpoints, stacking below the form on smaller devices. It does not scroll independently; it moves with the page scroll as part of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan load error banner appears at the very top of the main content area, immediately below the global header band (and announcement bar, if present). It spans the full width of the centered content container and has a shallow height, similar to a standard notification strip. The banner uses a strong, high-contrast background color (e.g., soft red) while maintaining rounded corners and modest vertical padding (12–16px) to match the playful, friendly aesthetic rather than appearing harsh. Text is left-aligned, concise, and accompanied by a small error icon on the far left, with a close or retry action aligned to the right. There is 16–24px of whitespace between the bottom of this banner and the top of the financing-plan-header hero section, so the user clearly perceives it as an alert separate from the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline banner that occupies the topmost slot in the content area when present. It scrolls away with the page and does not remain fixed, preserving the simplicity of the layout. When dismissed or absent, the financing-plan-header shifts upward to occupy its vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global form and API feedback component appears as a contextual message zone within the application portion of the page. It is positioned directly above the financing-application-form card, inside the same content column and aligned to its left edge. When active, it renders as a medium-width banner or card (spanning the full width of the form column but not necessarily the entire page) with a lightly tinted background indicating success, warning, or error state (e.g., soft green for success, amber for info). The message content is short and to the point, with 12–16px padding on all sides, and 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the CTA bar above and the form below. This placement ensures that any feedback about submissions or API interactions is seen in direct context with the form the user just interacted with, without overcrowding the hero or informational sections above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element that appears and disappears within the flow above the form. It does not fix to the viewport; instead, the page scroll naturally determines whether it is visible once the user moves past the form area.

name: Blog / Resources
title: Blog / Resources
description: Lists HVAC blog and resource articles with search, tag filtering, sorting, and pagination, and links to article detail pages.

core_features: Display a paginated list of blog articles with titles, excerpts, publish dates (if available), and tags using data from getBlogArticles.
Provide a search field that filters articles by text query (e.g., for indoor air quality topics) and reflects the search term in the URL query parameter.
Provide tag-based filtering so users can narrow results by article tags (derived from BlogArticle.tags), including indoor air quality where available.
Allow users to sort articles based on a sortBy option (for example, newest, relevance, or popularity), with the selected sort stored in the URL.
Highlight featured or popular articles by using BlogArticle.isFeatured and/or sortBy values returned via getBlogArticles.
Enable navigation to article detail pages by clicking article titles or dedicated 'Read more' interactions, passing articleId in the URL.
Keep all search, filter, sort, and pagination state in URL parameters (query, tag, sortBy, page) so the state is shareable and restorable.
user_workflows:
1. User arrives on blog.html from header/footer navigation without URL parameters.
   - The page reads URL parameters (none present) and applies default state (empty query, no tag selected, default sortBy, page = 1).
   - The blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles with the derived parameters and a configured pageSize.
   - The article-list displays the returned articles; pagination-controls are calculated based on total and pageSize.
   - The search-filter-bar components (search-form, tag-filter-bar, sort-dropdown) are initialized to reflect the current URL state.

2. User arrives with pre-filtered content from another page (e.g., index.html links to blog.html?tag=indoor_air_quality or blog.html?query=air%20quality).
   - The page parses query and/or tag from the URL.
   - The search input is pre-filled with query (if present); the tag filter shows the selected tag derived from the URL.
   - blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles using the provided query/tag plus sortBy (default if missing) and page (default 1 if missing).
   - Results show only matching articles; indoor air quality content is visible when the corresponding tag or query is used.

3. User performs a text search from within the blog page.
   - The user types text into the search-form input and submits.
   - The search-form triggers an event to blog-state-controller with the new query value.
   - blog-state-controller updates the URL: sets query to the submitted value, clears page to 1, preserves current tag and sortBy, and uses history.pushState/replaceState.
   - blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles with the updated parameters.
   - article-list and pagination-controls re-render with the new results.

4. User filters articles by tag (e.g., to see indoor air quality related posts).
   - The user selects or toggles a tag in the tag-filter-bar (tag options are generated based on BlogArticle.tags from the current dataset or preconfigured tag metadata).
   - tag-filter-bar emits the chosen tag to blog-state-controller.
   - blog-state-controller updates the URL tag parameter accordingly, resets page to 1, keeps current query and sortBy, and updates the browser URL.
   - blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles with the new tag filter.
   - article-list updates to show only articles whose tags match the selected tag; search-form and tag-filter-bar reflect the active filters.

5. User changes the sort order of articles.
   - The user selects a sort option in the sort-dropdown (e.g., newest, relevance, or popularity).
   - sort-dropdown emits the new sortBy value to blog-state-controller.
   - blog-state-controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, preserves query and tag, and writes the new state to the URL.
   - blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles with the updated sortBy.
   - article-list re-renders in the new order; sort-dropdown reflects the current sortBy choice.

6. User navigates through pagination.
   - The user clicks a page number, Next, or Previous in pagination-controls.
   - pagination-controls emits the requested page number to blog-state-controller.
   - blog-state-controller updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving query, tag, and sortBy, and then calls getBlogArticles with the new page.
   - article-list updates to display the articles for the selected page; pagination-controls highlight the current page.
   - If the user reloads or shares the URL, the same page of results is loaded via the parameters.

7. User views featured/popular articles.
   - On initial load (or on any state change), blog-state-controller may make an additional getBlogArticles call dedicated to highlighting articles (for example, using a specific sortBy value).
   - From the returned articles, blog-state-controller filters for items with BlogArticle.isFeatured = true, or uses the top subset of the response based on sortBy.
   - featured-articles-strip displays these highlighted articles separately from the main listing.
   - The user can click any featured article title to navigate to article.html?articleId=... just like in the main list.

8. User opens an article detail page.
   - From article-list or featured-articles-strip, the user clicks a title or 'Read more'.
   - The click handler reads the BlogArticle.id and navigates to article.html?articleId=<id>.
   - The blog page does not load additional data for the detail view; it simply delegates to the article.html page via the URL.

9. User clears filters and returns to the full list.
   - The user clicks a 'Clear filters' action in the search-filter-bar (if implemented) or manually removes query/tag values.
   - blog-state-controller updates the URL to remove query and tag parameters (or set them to empty values), sets page to 1, and preserves or resets sortBy according to the default behavior.
   - getBlogArticles is called with the cleared filters, and article-list shows a broader article list again.
interactions: Typing in search-form input: onInput/onChange events update local component state without API calls until form submission (to avoid excessive requests).
Submitting the search-form: onSubmit prevents default form behavior, emits the new query value to blog-state-controller, which updates URL parameters and calls getBlogArticles.
Clicking a tag in tag-filter-bar: onClick/onTagSelect emits the selected tag to blog-state-controller; URL is updated (tag parameter), page is reset, and getBlogArticles is called.
Toggling a selected tag off: onTagClear or repeated selection clears the tag filter, updates URL (removing or emptying tag), and refreshes results via getBlogArticles.
Changing sort in sort-dropdown: onChange emits the selected sortBy option; blog-state-controller updates sortBy in the URL, resets page, and re-fetches via getBlogArticles.

Clicking pagination controls: onPageChange (from clicking page numbers, Next, or Previous) passes the new page number to blog-state-controller; URL page parameter is updated and getBlogArticles is called.

Clicking an article title or 'Read more' in article-list or featured-articles-strip: onClick reads BlogArticle.id from the bound data and triggers window.location navigation to article.html?articleId=<id>.

Page load or URL change (back/forward navigation): onLoad/onPopState triggers blog-state-controller to re-parse query, tag, sortBy, and page from the URL and call getBlogArticles with matching parameters so the UI reflects the URL state.
Optional 'Clear filters' button: onClick clears query and tag values in the URL, sets page to 1, may reset sortBy to its default, and triggers a new getBlogArticles call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for state: query, tag, sortBy, and page. On initial load and on browser navigation events (popstate), blog-state-controller parses these parameters from window.location.search and constructs a state object. Defaults are applied if parameters are missing: query defaults to an empty string, tag defaults to no tag filter, sortBy defaults to a chosen default sort (e.g., newest), and page defaults to 1. After state is derived, blog-state-controller calls getBlogArticles with { query, tag, sortBy, page, pageSize }. The response's articles and total values are stored in memory and bound to article-list and pagination-controls. Any user interaction that modifies query, tag, sortBy, or page does so by requesting a state update through blog-state-controller. blog-state-controller then synchronizes the new state to the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState, ensuring that the URL always reflects the current state. Only after updating the URL does it invoke getBlogArticles with the new parameter set. Derived values such as totalPages are calculated from getBlogArticles.total and the pageSize used in the request, but these are not written back to the URL. If the user uses the browser's Back or Forward buttons, the popstate handler re-parses the URL and calls getBlogArticles again so that search, filter, sort, and pagination always match the visible URL. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client-side storage; refreshing or sharing the URL fully restores the results based solely on the query parameters and the API response.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Blog / Resources page that parses URL parameters (query, tag, sortBy, page), manages in-memory state, invokes getBlogArticles with those parameters (and a configured pageSize), and distributes the resulting articles and metadata to other components.

Provides a text input and submit action for searching blog articles by keyword. Initializes its input value from the query URL parameter and, on submit, requests the controller to update the query state and reload article results.

Displays available article tags and allows the user to select a tag to filter the article list (including indoor air quality tags when present). The set of tags is derived dynamically from BlogArticle.tags within the current or aggregated results. The currently active tag is initialized from and synchronized with the tag URL parameter.

Allows the user to choose how to sort the article list (for example by newest, relevance, or popularity). The selected sort option is initialized from the sortBy URL parameter and is emitted to the controller when changed so that results are re-fetched.

Shows a subset of highlighted articles, such as those marked as featured or determined by popularity, separate from the primary listing. Receives its list of BlogArticle items from the controller based on additional or filtered getBlogArticles requests. Supports navigation to article detail pages.

Renders the main list of blog articles returned by getBlogArticles for the current query, tag, sortBy, and page. Each item shows article metadata (e.g., title, excerpt, tags, published date if available) and provides actions (title click or 'Read more') that navigate to article.html with the appropriate articleId.

Provides controls for navigating between pages of articles based on total results and pageSize. Computes totalPages from the total value returned by getBlogArticles and the pageSize used in the request. Reflects the current page from the page URL parameter and emits page change events when the user selects a different page.

Composite functional wrapper that coordinates search-form, tag-filter-bar, sort-dropdown, and an optional 'Clear filters' action so they behave as a single control area. It forwards user interactions to blog-state-controller to update URL parameters and trigger new getBlogArticles calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header/navigation, the blog page uses the same linear, top-to-bottom, full-width section stacking as the mockup. The main blog content is centered in a 12-column grid with a clear content container. At the very top of the blog content, a slim state controller row sits left-aligned, followed by a unified search/filter bar spanning the width of the content. Directly beneath that, a featured-articles strip runs full-width within the container, and below it a multi-column article list fills most of the page. Pagination sits at the bottom, centered. Within the search/filter area, elements are arranged horizontally (search left, sort right) with a second horizontal row of tags beneath, reflecting the mockup’s two-row, horizontally split hero pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global cyan announcement bar and white header/navigation, the blog page uses the same linear, top-to-bottom, full-width section stacking as the mockup. The main blog content is centered in a 12-column grid with a clear content container. At the very top of the blog content, a slim state controller row sits left-aligned, followed by a unified search/filter bar spanning the width of the content. Directly beneath that, a featured-articles strip runs full-width within the container, and below it a multi-column article list fills most of the page. Pagination sits at the bottom, centered. Within the search/filter area, elements are arranged horizontally (search left, sort right) with a second horizontal row of tags beneath, reflecting the mockup’s two-row, horizontally split hero pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page follows the mockup’s generous spacing: each major section (filters, featured strip, article grid, pagination) gets 48–64px vertical padding or margin to create clear breathing room. Horizontally, the content container spans roughly 80–88% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~1120–1200px) and is centered. Within the search/filter bar, the search input occupies roughly 60–65% of the width, the sort dropdown 20–25%, with the rest left as whitespace. The featured articles strip uses a slightly taller vertical allocation (around 48–64px padding) to feel prominent. The article grid uses roughly equal column widths (e.g., 3 columns at ~30–32% each with 16–24px gutters).
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains low-to-moderate. Controls are grouped but not crowded: the state controller, search field, tag chips, and sort control have comfortable horizontal spacing with 16–24px gaps. Vertical spacing between rows is generous (16–24px), echoing the roomy About section in the mockup. Article cards feature clear padding (16–24px inside each card) and limited text to keep them scannable. The overall impression is a friendly, marketing-style resources page rather than a cramped, data-heavy interface.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow adapts the mockup’s Z-pattern.
