The eye starts at the upper-left of the blog content area on the blog-state-controller tabs, then moves right across the unified search/filter bar (from search input to sort dropdown). It then drops down slightly to the left edge of the featured-articles strip, scanning horizontally across featured cards. From there, the eye moves down again to the main article grid, starting at the top-left card and scanning right and then down through rows. Finally, attention lands on the centered pagination controls at the bottom. The persistent chat widget in the global bottom-left corner remains a soft tertiary anchor but doesn’t compete with main blog interactions.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s cyan announcement bar and white header, the blog/resources page opens into a clean, white content area centered in a wide, 12-column grid. A slim row of state controls (like tabs for All Articles, Guides, etc.) sits aligned to the left, giving a clear entry point into the resource library. Directly beneath it, a pill-like, white search and filter bar spans the full content width: a large search field anchors the left side, while a compact sort dropdown sits on the right, mirroring the left-right balance seen in the About section’s text/image split. A second row of rounded tag chips runs underneath, stretching across the width and wrapping as needed, with plenty of breathing space around them. Dropping down, a lightly tinted featured-articles strip extends across the container, with a few highlighted article cards arranged horizontally, giving a friendly, promotional feel reminiscent of a hero illustration. Below that, the main article list forms a neat card grid in 2–3 columns, surrounded by ample white space, each card separated by consistent gutters. At the very bottom, a centered row of pagination controls sits with generous top and bottom margin, providing a calm, clear endpoint. Throughout, bright accent colors, rounded shapes, and roomy spacing maintain the playful, approachable brand aesthetic while keeping the layout simple and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-state-controller appears at the top of the blog content area, directly below the page’s hero/title (or the first heading for the blog section) and just above the search-filter-bar. It is a shallow horizontal strip aligned to the left within the centered content container, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container width and leaving open white space to the right. The controls themselves (tabs or pill buttons such as “All”, “How-To Guides”, “Maintenance Tips”) are arranged in a single row, each pill separated by 8–16px gaps. Vertical padding above and below this row matches the mockup’s modest header spacing (about 16–24px), making the controller feel integrated but not heavy. The entire row sits on the same white background as the page, with no heavy box around it, so it feels like a subtle, inline filter just before the more prominent search/filter band below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; it scrolls away with the content and does not stick to the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-filter-bar is the first visually prominent utility section of the blog content, sitting immediately below the blog-state-controller with about 24–32px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width of the centered content container and reads like a soft, pill-shaped band or card laid on the white canvas. The bar uses a two-row vertical stack inside: the top row holds the search-form on the left and the sort-dropdown on the right, arranged in a horizontal split reminiscent of the About section’s text/image composition; the bottom row holds the tag-filter-bar stretching full-width. The bar has 16–24px internal padding on all sides, rounded corners consistent with the brand’s bubbly CTAs, and possibly a very subtle shadow or light border to differentiate it from the background. Overall height is moderate but feels spacious because of the vertical rhythm: around 16–24px between the top row (search + sort) and the tag chips row. This entire composite bar sits on the white page background, with 32–40px of clear whitespace below before the featured-articles-strip begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport once scrolled into position (beneath the main site header), maintaining its width within the content container; as the user scrolls down the list of articles, the bar gently pins under the header to keep search and filters readily accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the search-filter-bar’s top row, the search-form is positioned on the left, occupying approximately 60–65% of the bar’s width on desktop. It is a single, wide input field with rounded corners that visually matches the brand’s CTA style, about 48px tall with 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. A search icon sits inside at the far left, followed by placeholder text; the input extends toward the center of the bar, leaving a comfortable 24–32px gap before the sort-dropdown area. The search-form aligns with the left edge of the content grid and creates a strong horizontal starting point for the user’s eye, analogous to the bold “About°” heading in the reference mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the search-filter-bar; resizes responsively, shrinking toward full-width (100%) on smaller screens and stacking above the sort-dropdown when the viewport is narrow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tag-filter-bar forms the second row inside the search-filter-bar, positioned directly beneath the search-form and sort-dropdown with roughly 16px of vertical spacing. It spans the full width of the content container inside the bar. Individual tags appear as small, rounded chips (e.g., “Heating”, “Cooling”, “Energy Savings”) arranged in a left-aligned horizontal row that wraps to multiple lines if needed. Each chip has 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding, separated from each other by 8px gaps, reflecting the playful, pill-shaped visual language. There is generous vertical breathing room above and below the chips (about 8–12px inside the bar), so even multiple rows of tags don’t feel cramped. The bar visually acts as a continuation of the search area, reinforcing a cohesive filter cluster in the upper part of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the search-filter-bar; expands vertically as more tags wrap, pushing the rest of the content down while maintaining the sticky behavior of the overall bar on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-dropdown is placed in the top row of the search-filter-bar on the far right, horizontally aligned with the search-form. It occupies roughly 20–25% of the bar’s width on desktop but can shrink to its content’s minimum width, with ample whitespace between it and the right edge of the container (16–24px). The control is a compact, rounded dropdown with a visible label like “Sort by” and a selected value (e.g., “Newest”); its height matches the search-form (around 48px) to maintain a clean baseline. On wide screens, this creates a clear left-right tension: large field left, small control right, with open space around the dropdown to avoid feeling crowded. On narrower screens, the sort-dropdown stacks below or above the search-form, still within the same bar, preserving the low-density, relaxed layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the search-filter-bar; aligns to the right on larger viewports and reflows beneath or above the search-form on smaller screens to maintain readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-articles-strip sits directly below the search-filter-bar, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 32–40px to clearly distinguish it as a new section. It spans the full width of the centered content container but sits on a subtly tinted background (e.g., a very light cyan or warm grey) to echo the visual emphasis of the mockup’s illustrated About area. Inside, there is 32–48px of vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. One to three featured article cards are arranged horizontally in a row: on desktop, each card takes up about 30–32% of the container width with 16–24px gaps between cards; on smaller screens, the cards stack vertically with consistent spacing. Each card is a rounded rectangle with an illustration or thumbnail near the top and a short title and a few lines of text below, mirroring the playful, visual-forward feel of the mockup’s penguin illustration card. This strip feels like a co-primary focal section just below the filters, encouraging users to explore highlighted resources before diving into the full list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal document flow; does not stick, but its tinted background and internal spacing give it strong visual separation from both the filter bar above and the general article list below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list is positioned directly below the featured-articles-strip, with approximately 40–48px of vertical spacing to create a clear transition from featured content to the full catalog. It occupies the full width of the centered content container and uses a card-based grid layout. On desktop, articles are arranged in 3 columns, each card spanning roughly 4 of 12 grid columns (about 30–32% width) with 16–24px gutters horizontally and 24–32px between rows. On medium screens, the layout can shift to 2 columns; on small screens, it collapses to a single column. Each article card is a simple, flat white tile with rounded corners, 16–24px internal padding, a small thumbnail or icon at top (optional), followed by the article title, a brief one- or two-line summary, and meta info (date, category). The generous spacing and clear separation between cards echo the mockup’s low-density aesthetic, making the list easy to scan. The grid extends downward, occupying most of the page’s vertical space until it approaches the pagination section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow; the grid reflows responsively based on viewport width, increasing or decreasing the number of columns while maintaining consistent gutters and card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of the blog content area, directly beneath the article-list with a generous top margin of about 40–48px to visually separate them from the last row of cards. They are centered horizontally within the content container, forming a single, concise row. The controls use rounded buttons (e.g., numbered circles or pills for pages, plus previous/next arrows) with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding each, spaced 8–12px apart, reflecting the brand’s friendly, pill-shaped CTAs. There is ample whitespace both above and below the pagination row (at least 24–32px below) so it feels like a calm stopping point. On mobile, the controls may simplify to fewer visible page numbers while staying center-aligned.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the article list and does not remain fixed once scrolled past.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single blog article with full content, recommended HVAC services, and navigation to related resources and service consultation pages.

core_features: Read the articleId from the URL and load the corresponding BlogArticle via getArticleDetails.
Display full article metadata including title, publish date, and tags from the BlogArticle entity.
Render the complete article content string and dynamically attach contextual calls-to-action based on recommendedServices from getArticleDetails.
List recommended HVAC services returned in recommendedServices with clear actions to view details and request a consultation on service_detail.html using appropriate URL parameters.
Display relatedArticles returned by getArticleDetails as navigable links to other article.html pages using their articleId.
Provide a navigation control to return to the blog listing (blog.html) without relying on local storage.
user_workflows: Read article and request consultation from an in-article CTA: 1) User arrives on article.html with an articleId URL parameter from blog.html or index.html. 2) The page controller parses articleId and calls getArticleDetails(articleId). 3) Once data is returned, the page displays the article title, metadata, and full content. 4) The article content component analyzes the content together with recommendedServices to insert or display contextual CTA buttons or links referencing each recommended Service. 5) The user clicks a contextual CTA (e.g., to learn about or request consultation for a recommended indoor air quality service). 6) The click navigates the browser to service_detail.html with serviceId set to the selected Service.id and mode set to consultation in the URL query string.

Navigate from one article to another via related articles: 1) User arrives on article.html with a valid articleId. 2) The page loads the primary BlogArticle and the relatedArticles from getArticleDetails. 3) The related articles list component renders links for each related BlogArticle. 4) The user clicks a related article link. 5) The browser navigates to article.html with the clicked article’s id as the articleId URL parameter. 6) The page controller runs again, calling getArticleDetails with the new articleId and updating all components with the new data.

Return from an article to the blog listing: 1) User is viewing article.html and finishes reading. 2) The user clicks the back-to-blog navigation control. 3) The browser navigates to blog.html without additional parameters. 4) The user can then select another article, which will again navigate back to article.html with the chosen articleId.
interactions: On page load, the controller component reads the articleId from the URL query string and triggers getArticleDetails(articleId).
On successful data load, the article header component binds to BlogArticle fields (such as title and publishedAt) and renders them.

The article content component binds to BlogArticle.content and the recommendedServices array. It processes the article content string and associates sections of text or markers with generated CTA elements that, when clicked, navigate to service_detail.html with the appropriate serviceId and mode=consultation.

The recommended services list component renders each Service from recommendedServices with one or more action buttons. Clicking an action button triggers navigation to service_detail.html?serviceId=<Service.id>&mode=consultation.

The related articles list component renders each BlogArticle from relatedArticles as a link to article.html?articleId=<BlogArticle.id>. Clicking any link updates the current URL and causes the page to reload data for the new articleId.
The back-to-blog link component navigates the user to blog.html when clicked.
If getArticleDetails fails (for example, due to an invalid articleId or network error), an error state component displays an appropriate message and offers a navigation link back to blog.html.
While getArticleDetails is in progress, a loading state component indicates that article data is being fetched and disables interactions dependent on article data until loading is complete.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the articleId URL query parameter (e.g., article.html?articleId=<value>). On initial page load or when the URL changes to a new articleId, the controller component extracts articleId from the query string. If articleId is present, it calls getArticleDetails(articleId) and sets the page into a loading state. While loading, components that depend on BlogArticle, recommendedServices, or relatedArticles display loading indicators or remain hidden. On successful response, the loading state is cleared and the resolved BlogArticle, recommendedServices (array of Service), and relatedArticles (array of BlogArticle) are stored in in-memory page state and bound to the child components. If getArticleDetails returns an error or no article, the page enters an error state and shows an error component with a link to blog.html. When the user clicks a related article link, the browser navigates to article.html?articleId=<related.id>; this URL change clears existing article state, re-enters loading state, and triggers a new getArticleDetails call. When the user clicks any recommended service CTA, the browser navigates to service_detail.html with serviceId set to the selected Service.id and mode set to consultation in the URL (service_detail.html?serviceId=<Service.id>&mode=consultation). No state is stored in localStorage; all state is derived from the current URL and the latest getArticleDetails response.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads the articleId from the URL, calls getArticleDetails, manages loading and error states, and supplies BlogArticle, recommendedServices, and relatedArticles data to child components.
Displays a loading indicator while getArticleDetails is in progress and hides itself once data has been successfully loaded or an error occurs.
Shows an error message when getArticleDetails fails or returns no article for the given articleId and provides a link to navigate back to blog.html.
Displays the article title and optional metadata such as publish date and tags based on the loaded BlogArticle.

Renders the full article body from BlogArticle.content and, using the recommendedServices array, dynamically generates and inserts contextual service CTAs (buttons or links) within or adjacent to relevant sections of the content. Each CTA is configured to navigate to the corresponding service detail page in consultation mode.

Displays a focused list of recommended services (from recommendedServices) related to the article, each with an action such as viewing details or requesting a consultation, which navigates to service_detail.html with serviceId and mode=consultation.
Shows a list of related articles returned in relatedArticles, each rendered as a link to article.html with the article's id as the articleId parameter, enabling quick navigation between articles.
Provides a simple navigation control that takes the user back to the main blog listing page at blog.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence as the mockup: global cyan announcement bar and main header (existing site chrome), then this page’s content begins with a small back‑to‑blog link, followed by a full‑width article header section, then the article body, then a full‑width recommended services CTA band, and finally a related articles section. Within this vertical stack, the article header and recommended services sections both reuse the mockup’s two‑column, left‑text / right‑visual pattern, while the article body uses a single, comfortably narrow text column inside the same centered content container.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence as the mockup: global cyan announcement bar and main header (existing site chrome), then this page’s content begins with a small back‑to‑blog link, followed by a full‑width article header section, then the article body, then a full‑width recommended services CTA band, and finally a related articles section. Within this vertical stack, the article header and recommended services sections both reuse the mockup’s two‑column, left‑text / right‑visual pattern, while the article body uses a single, comfortably narrow text column inside the same centered content container.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All page content sits inside a centered max‑width container (roughly 80–90% of viewport width up to ~1120–1200px) aligned to a 12‑column grid. Sections (article header, content, recommended services, related articles) have generous vertical padding of about 48–64px top and bottom. The article header and recommended services CTA section both use an asymmetric split similar to the About section: approximately 5/12 width for the primary text column on the left and 7/12 width for the visual or CTA content on the right. The article content itself is slightly narrower (about 7/12–8/12 of the page width) for readability and is aligned with the left column of the header, leaving breathing room on the right.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low to moderate, echoing the marketing feel of the mockup. The article header has a prominent title and a few short metadata lines rather than heavy copy. The article body uses a single text column with generous line height and spacing between paragraphs and headings (16–24px). Service CTAs and related article cards are clearly separated by 16–24px gaps, and each section is wrapped with open white (or lightly tinted) space so nothing feels crowded, even when the article text is long.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a gentle Z‑pattern similar to the mockup. The eye lands first on the top‑left back‑to‑blog link, drops slightly to the bold article title on the left, then moves right toward any header‑side visual/context card. From there, the eye naturally moves down to the start of the article text (still left‑aligned within the same container), scanning down the single column. After finishing the text, attention is pulled rightward and slightly down into the high‑contrast recommended services CTA band, where the left text column introduces the CTAs and the right column of buttons/cards becomes a strong focal area. Finally, the gaze moves down into the related articles grid, which fans out horizontally to provide multiple next‑step options.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing cyan announcement bar and main navigation, the article detail page opens within a centered content container on a clean white background. A small, understated back‑to‑blog link sits at the top left of this container, giving users an immediate way back without competing visually with the main content. Directly beneath it, a spacious article header section occupies a large vertical slice of the viewport: on larger screens, the left 40–45% contains the article’s category pill, publish date, and a large, friendly title with a short summary, while the right 55–60% holds a soft‑edged card for any featured illustration or article context, echoing the playful service brand. Below this, the layout narrows into a single, comfortable text column that lines up with the left part of the header, allowing the article body to read like a clean editorial page with ample white space to the right. Once the article text ends, the page expands again into a full‑width, lightly tinted recommended services band: left‑aligned explanatory copy and a heading sit on the left, with a stack of rounded, colorful service CTAs on the right, making it an inviting conversion area without feeling aggressive. Underneath, a white related articles section presents a short heading and a grid of article cards, providing a calm, structured way to continue exploring. Throughout, spacing is generous, edges are soft and rounded, and the structure feels consistent with the playful, approachable HVAC brand seen in the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-controller is a non-visual wrapper that occupies the full width of the main content container beneath the global announcement bar and header. It aligns with the same centered max-width as the rest of the page content and effectively reserves the vertical space where the article header, article body, recommended services, and related articles will appear. Depending on the data state, it conditionally displays either the loading indicator, the error message, or the full article layout (header, content, CTAs, and related articles) in this same region, ensuring the page structure does not jump horizontally and only changes vertically as needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural, in-flow controller for child components with no visible UI; it spans the full content width and manages which child component (loading, error, or full article stack) is visible at any time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the article-loading-indicator appears in the main article area directly below the site header, occupying the same horizontal bounds as the content container (centered, up to ~1120–1200px wide). It is vertically padded so that its top edge sits roughly 48–64px below the main site header, mirroring the spacing of the article header. Within this area, a single loading spinner or playful loading illustration (in brand colors) is centered both horizontally and vertically within a block that takes up around 40–50% of the visible viewport height. A short loading message is placed just below the spinner, with about 16px of spacing. The surrounding space remains mostly empty white, matching the low-density, clean feel of the mockup and clearly signaling that the article content will appear here once ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays in the normal document flow in place of the article header and content, effectively reserving that space. It keeps a minimum height so the layout does not compress excessively during loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs, the article-error-message replaces the main article area within the same centered content container beneath the site header. It appears approximately 48–64px below the header and sits within a medium-width card, about 50–60% of the container width (roughly 6/12–7/12 of the content width), centered horizontally to feel balanced. The card has rounded corners and a subtle shadow or border, with internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides. A short, prominent error heading appears at the top of the card, followed by a brief explanation and optional retry or back-to-blog button(s) arranged in a small horizontal or stacked group with 16–24px spacing. Outside the card, ample white space surrounds the message, keeping the layout light despite the error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in-flow in place of the article header and content, centered within the main content container. It does not overlap the site header or footer and expands vertically as needed for the error copy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header section begins directly below the back-to-blog link, within the centered content container. It spans the full width of that container and has generous top and bottom padding of roughly 48–64px, creating a spacious white band reminiscent of the About section in the mockup. Inside, the content is arranged in a two-column layout on desktop: the left column occupies about 5/12 of the container width and the right column about 7/12. In the left column, a small, softly colored category pill or label sits at the very top, followed by the publish date in a lighter style. Below this, the article title is large and bold, taking up 2–3 lines with comfortable 16–24px spacing from surrounding elements. A short summary or subtitle can appear below the title, constrained to keep to 2–3 lines to maintain low density. In the right column, aligned vertically around the middle of the title block, sits a friendly context card or featured image area: a rounded rectangle with a faint cyan or pale brand-color background, possibly containing a simple illustration (e.g., HVAC/penguin graphic) or quick service highlight. This right-hand card matches the visual weight and scale of the illustration in the mockup’s About section, serving as a co-primary focal point. On tablets and mobile, the layout collapses into a single column: the left text content appears first at full width, followed by the right-side card stacked below with 24–32px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the standard vertical flow beneath the page header and back link, expanding vertically with title length. On larger screens it maintains the two-column split; on smaller breakpoints it stacks to a single column while preserving padding and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content section is placed directly beneath the article-header, separated by about 32–48px of vertical whitespace to create a clear but gentle transition. It resides in the same centered content container but uses a narrower readable column: roughly 7/12 to 8/12 of the container width (max ~680–760px), aligned to the left edge of the header’s left column. This alignment means that on desktop there is a comfortable margin of empty white space on the right, preserving the airy, marketing-style feel rather than creating a dense, full-width editorial layout. The article body text flows top-to-bottom, with headings separated from paragraphs by about 16–24px and between sections by around 32px. Inline images or callouts are constrained to the same text column width and carry 16–24px of vertical spacing before and after. The bottom of the article-content section has around 48px of padding before the next section starts, ensuring the end of the article is clearly perceived before the CTA band begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally under the article header and above the recommended services section, expanding vertically based on content length while keeping a fixed maximum width for readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended-services-cta-list appears immediately below the article content, separated by approximately 48–64px of white space. It is presented as a full-width band that stretches edge-to-edge of the viewport but contains its content within the same centered max-width container as the rest of the page. The band’s background uses a very light brand tint (e.g., pale cyan) to subtly echo the top announcement bar and differentiate this section without overpowering it. Vertical padding inside the band is generous, around 48–64px top and bottom. Within the container, the layout returns to a two-column structure: the left column occupies about 5/12 of the content width and contains a concise heading like “Need help with your heating or cooling?” plus a short, friendly paragraph (1–3 lines) that ties the article topic to specific services. The right column, about 7/12 in width, holds a vertical stack of 2–4 service CTAs. Each CTA is a full-width card within this column, with rounded corners, 16–24px internal padding, and 16–24px gaps between cards. The CTAs may include a short service name, a one-line description, and a prominent rounded button (e.g., “Schedule a Visit”), making this section a strong but still friendly conversion area. On smaller screens, the band’s content stacks: the left explanatory text appears first at full width, followed by the CTA cards stacked below with consistent vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a distinct, in-flow full-width section with a tinted background. It anchors itself between the article body and the related articles, expanding vertically based on the number of CTAs and wrapping to a single column layout on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list sits below the recommended services band, separated from it by approximately 40–56px of vertical space. It returns to a plain white background and the same centered max-width container, visually tying back to the article content area. At the top of this section, a modest heading like “Keep Reading” or “Related Articles” is left-aligned with the article text column, with about 16–24px of space beneath it. Below the heading, a grid of related article cards is arranged in low to moderate density: on desktop, 3 cards per row (each roughly 3/12–4/12 of the container width with consistent horizontal gutters); on tablets, 2 per row; and on mobile, a single column. Each card is a small, rounded rectangle with 16–24px of internal padding and 16–24px gaps between cards both horizontally and vertically. Cards may include a small category label, title, and a brief line of preview text, keeping them light and scannable. The section has about 48px of padding at the bottom before the page transitions into any global footer, ensuring a clear visual endpoint to the article detail flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays as an in-flow section after the recommended CTAs, adjusting its grid layout responsively while maintaining the same container alignment and section-level spacing as the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-blog-link is placed at the very top of the article detail page’s content area, directly beneath the global header and announcement bar. It aligns to the left edge of the main content container and sits about 32–40px below the bottom of the site header, before the article-header section begins. The link is small and understated compared to the article title—perhaps presented as a simple text link with a left-pointing arrow icon and a short label like “Back to all articles.” There is 16–24px of vertical space between this link and the top of the article-header content. Its scale and weight keep it clearly visible to users who look for navigation, but it does not compete with the article title or CTAs, respecting the clean, low-density design aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow, fixed to the top-left of the content container. It remains a simple inline element with minimal height, shifting to full-width with left alignment on smaller screens while preserving its role as a subtle entry/exit control for the article section.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Provides company background and service coverage details, using live service area data to reassure visitors and route them to Contact or Schedule Service pages.

core_features: Load and display a dynamic summary of all active service areas and total ZIP codes covered using getServiceAreasSummary.
List service areas with optional drill-down into ZIP codes so users can confirm coverage for their location.
Provide a ZIP coverage check form that validates a user-entered ZIP against the service areas returned by getServiceAreasSummary.
Highlight coverage and trust reassurance text when a visitor arrives with a ZIP code in the URL, using getServiceAreasSummary to confirm coverage.
Offer clear navigation to Contact and Schedule Service pages, passing along relevant context such as ZIP code and referrer (from=about) via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Visitor lands on About Us from navigation or search
1. User opens about.html (no specific query parameters required).
2. Page initializes and calls getServiceAreasSummary.
3. Once data is loaded, the page displays: a) total number of service areas, b) totalZipCodes value, and c) a structured list of areas.
4. User can optionally expand any service area row to see the ZIP codes returned for that area.
5. User reads additional static or CMS-managed company background, certifications, and guarantees.
6. User clicks either a 'Contact Us' or 'Schedule Service' call-to-action.
7. Browser navigates to contact.html?from=about or schedule_service.html?from=about, preserving any known ZIP (zip=...) as a URL parameter if available.

Workflow: Visitor checks if their ZIP code is covered
1. User opens about.html (optionally about.html?zip=... if linked from another page).
2. Page calls getServiceAreasSummary and stores the areas and totalZipCodes.
3. ZIP coverage form initializes; if a zip query parameter exists, it pre-fills the form with that ZIP and triggers a coverage check against the loaded areas.zipCodes.
4. User can modify the ZIP input and submit the form.
5. On submit, the form checks whether the entered ZIP matches any zipCodes within the loaded service areas.
6. The result is shown on-page: a coverage confirmation or non-coverage message and, if covered, the matching service area name(s).
7. The page updates the browser URL to include or update zip=[enteredZip] (using standard navigation or history APIs, no localStorage).
8. User proceeds by selecting either 'Contact Us' or 'Schedule Service'; the selected ZIP is appended to the target URL as a query parameter (e.g., contact.html?from=about&zip=[enteredZip]).

Workflow: Visitor comes from another page with a known ZIP and wants to schedule service
1. User clicks an About Us link elsewhere on the site, which opens about.html with a zip query parameter (e.g., about.html?zip=[knownZip]).
2. Page loads and calls getServiceAreasSummary.
3. Once areas are loaded, the page automatically checks whether the zip parameter is in any area.zipCodes.
4. If covered, the page displays a short coverage confirmation next to the company information and marks the matching area as highlighted in the areas list.
5. User reviews company information and sees that their ZIP is covered.
6. User clicks the 'Schedule Service' button.
7. Browser navigates to schedule_service.html?from=about&zip=[zipFromURL].
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load.
- Behavior: The data-fetching component calls getServiceAreasSummary with no parameters.
- Result: Page state is updated with the list of ServiceArea entities and totalZipCodes; dependent components re-render to display this information.

Interaction: Expand/collapse service area details
- Trigger: User clicks on a service area row or an associated toggle control.
- Behavior: Component toggles an internal expanded/collapsed state for that area; when expanded, the list of zipCodes from the corresponding ServiceArea is displayed.
- Result: User can inspect specific ZIP codes within each service area without affecting the URL or other components.

Interaction: ZIP coverage form submit
- Trigger: User enters a ZIP and submits the form (button click or Enter key).
- Behavior: Form handler prevents default submit, reads the ZIP value, and checks it against all zipCodes in the loaded ServiceArea data.
- Result: The component sets internal state to either 'covered' (with matching area IDs/names) or 'not_covered'; a message is shown, and the URL's zip query parameter is created or updated to match the entered ZIP.

Interaction: Auto-check ZIP from URL
- Trigger: Page load when about.html includes a zip query parameter.
- Behavior: After getServiceAreasSummary resolves, a controller component reads the zip parameter, runs the same coverage check logic as the form, and stores the result.
- Result: The ZIP input field is pre-filled, a coverage result is displayed, and any matching service areas are highlighted in the service area list.

Interaction: Click 'Contact Us' CTA
- Trigger: User clicks the Contact button or link on the About Us page.
- Behavior: Navigation logic builds a URL to contact.html, including from=about and, if available in current state, zip=[currentOrCheckedZip].
- Result: Browser navigates to contact.html?from=about[&zip=...] without additional API calls.

Interaction: Click 'Schedule Service' CTA
- Trigger: User clicks the Schedule Service button or link.
- Behavior: Navigation logic builds a URL to schedule_service.html, including from=about and, if available, zip=[currentOrCheckedZip].
- Result: Browser navigates to schedule_service.html?from=about[&zip=...].
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About Us page uses only URL query parameters and in-memory state (no localStorage) to manage context:
- Recognized URL parameters:
  - zip: Optional. If present, it is treated as the visitor's ZIP. After getServiceAreasSummary data is loaded, the page checks if this ZIP exists in any ServiceArea.zipCodes. The ZIP coverage form is pre-filled with this value, a coverage result is calculated, and matching areas are highlighted. Any change to the ZIP via the form updates this parameter in the URL.
  - from: Optional. Tracks referrer context (e.g., from=nav, from=footer, from=special_offer). When navigating out to contact.html or schedule_service.html, the page sets from=about so that downstream pages know the user came from About Us.
  - areaId: Optional. If present, the page will, after loading areas from getServiceAreasSummary, attempt to match this value to ServiceArea.id. If a match is found, that area is scrolled into view and marked as expanded/highlighted. This parameter can be added via internal links or external campaigns.
- Internal state:
  - serviceAreas: The full array returned by getServiceAreasSummary.areas.
  - totalZipCodes: Value from getServiceAreasSummary.totalZipCodes.
  - selectedZip: The current ZIP being displayed in the coverage checker. Sourced from the zip URL parameter initially, and updated when the user submits the ZIP form.
  - zipCoverageResult: Derived state indicating whether selectedZip is covered, and if so, which ServiceArea.id values match. Updated whenever selectedZip or serviceAreas change.
  - expandedAreaIds: A set/array of ServiceArea.id values that are currently expanded in the UI for ZIP detail viewing. May be initialized with areaId from the URL.
These states inform rendering and navigation, but they do not alter any server-side data. All external data for this page comes exclusively from getServiceAreasSummary.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getServiceAreasSummary, stores the resulting areas and totalZipCodes in page-level state, and exposes them to child components via props or a shared store.
Displays a concise summary of coverage based on the data from getServiceAreasSummary, including the number of active service areas and the totalZipCodes value.

Renders a list of all service areas returned by getServiceAreasSummary. For each ServiceArea, shows its name and allows expansion to reveal its zipCodes. Also responds to highlighting or auto-expansion for areas referenced by URL parameter areaId or indicated as matching the current ZIP coverage result.

Allows the user to input a ZIP code and checks whether it is covered by any of the ServiceArea entries loaded via getServiceAreasSummary. It shows a coverage status message and the names of matching areas and updates the page URL's zip parameter.

Shows the current ZIP coverage status (covered or not covered) based on the latest check performed by the zip-coverage-form or auto-check from URL. Highlights any matching service area names and provides reassurance text when covered.

Displays company background, experience, certifications, licenses, guarantees, and team descriptions. Content is static or CMS-managed and does not depend on runtime data models, but is placed alongside dynamic service area and coverage information for trust-building.

Provides buttons/links to navigate to contact.html and schedule_service.html. When clicked, constructs target URLs including from=about and, if a selectedZip is present in page state, appends zip=[selectedZip].

Parses URL query parameters (zip, from, areaId) on load, initializes related internal state (selectedZip, initialReferrer, expanded or highlighted area), and updates the URL parameters when key state values such as selectedZip change.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom, section-based flow anchored by the existing site chrome: a full-width cyan announcement bar at the very top, a white header band with logo and navigation beneath it, then the About Us content as a large hero-style section, followed by a distinct Service Area section. Within the About hero, content uses a two-column split: left for the company-info content and CTAs, right for the illustration (part of the brand system, not one of the listed components). The Service Area section also respects the 12-column grid and uses a similar left–right split: left for interactive controls (ZIP form and status), right for service area summary and list. Data/control components (loader, URL controller) are non-visual and live logically at the top of the Service Area section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom, section-based flow anchored by the existing site chrome: a full-width cyan announcement bar at the very top, a white header band with logo and navigation beneath it, then the About Us content as a large hero-style section, followed by a distinct Service Area section. Within the About hero, content uses a two-column split: left for the company-info content and CTAs, right for the illustration (part of the brand system, not one of the listed components). The Service Area section also respects the 12-column grid and uses a similar left–right split: left for interactive controls (ZIP form and status), right for service area summary and list. Data/control components (loader, URL controller) are non-visual and live logically at the top of the Service Area section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered content container (~70–80% of the viewport width or a max width around 1080–1200px), with full-width background bands for each section. The cyan announcement bar is shallow (roughly 40–56px tall) but high contrast. The white header is medium height (around 72–96px), giving breathing room to logo and nav. The About hero section is tall and spacious: ~48–64px padding top and bottom inside the content container, with the left column occupying roughly 5/12 of the grid and the right illustration column 7/12. The Service Area section uses similar vertical padding (48–64px) and an internal grid where the interactive column is about 4–5/12 wide and the list/summary column about 7–8/12, with clear vertical margins (16–24px) between stacked elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low to moderate: each section feels open, with generous white space around text blocks and panels. Headings are large and have 16–24px breathing room from body copy. Panels (summary, list, info banners) are card-like with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between them. The Service Area list can be denser but still uses adequate line spacing and grouping. No component fills the entire viewport edge-to-edge with text; instead, each element sits within clear margins, maintaining the airy, marketing-style feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains the observed Z-pattern. At the page level, the eye starts at the top-left of the cyan bar and moves right to any call-to-action, then drops to the logo and scans across the main nav CTA, then down to the bold About heading in the left column, then across to the illustration on the right. Within the About hero, the CTAs under the About text provide a horizontal focal point leading users to Contact or Schedule pages. In the Service Area section, attention begins at a left-aligned heading and summary panel, then slides right to the service list, then down-left to the ZIP coverage form, and finally right again to the updated status banner and detailed service list. A persistent chat widget in the bottom-left of the viewport (outside these components) lightly anchors the lower-left as a tertiary focal point.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s cyan announcement bar and roomy white navigation header, the About Us page opens into a bright, full-width white hero section. In the center of the screen, a wide content container is split into two columns: on the left, the company-info section presents a bold “About” style heading, a short, readable paragraph, and a row of rounded call-to-action buttons leading to Contact and Schedule Service; on the right, a large penguin-themed illustration card fills slightly more space, giving the hero a playful, approachable character. Generous vertical padding keeps the hero feeling open and uncluttered. Scrolling down, a new banded section (white or subtly tinted for separation) introduces the Service Area content. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading and summary panel reassure visitors that service area data is live and current. Below this, the layout splits again: on the left, a compact column holds the ZIP coverage form and a color-accented status banner stacked with comfortable gaps; on the right, a taller card houses a structured list of cities or ZIPs served. Everything sits within the same centered grid, with 16–24px spacing between elements and 48–64px between sections. Invisible infrastructure components (loader and URL controller) support this Service Area section without occupying visual space. The overall effect is clean, friendly, and easy to scan, with the main story about the company flowing naturally into precise, reassuring coverage details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The company-info-section sits in the main About hero section, within the centered content container, aligned to the left side. It occupies roughly 5 of 12 columns (about 40–45% of the container width) on desktop. There is 48–64px padding above and below this section from the previous header and the next section, and about 32px of horizontal breathing room from the container’s left edge. Inside this panel, the heading is large and left-aligned at the very top, followed by 16–24px of vertical space, then a concise paragraph in a readable width (no more than ~60–70 characters per line). The text block stops before mid-height of the hero so that there is room beneath it for the about-navigation-ctas, keeping the column visually balanced. The right column of this hero (not part of this component) contains the illustration card and occupies about 7/12 of the grid, creating a strong horizontal contrast between text-heavy left and image-heavy right. The company-info-section’s card edges align vertically with the interactive coverage section below to preserve a consistent grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow; it scrolls with the page and reflows from two-column to stacked layout on smaller screens (company-info-section stacking above the illustration).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-navigation-ctas component is positioned directly below the main paragraph content inside the left column of the About hero, still within the company-info-section’s vertical bounds. It is left-aligned with the text block and sits approximately 24px below the last line of body copy. The CTAs appear as 2–3 horizontally arranged rounded buttons (e.g., Contact, Schedule Service), with 16px gaps between them and 16–24px internal padding in each button. The row of buttons spans roughly 60–70% of the left column width, leaving some white space on the right side of the column to avoid crowding the central gutter toward the illustration. Vertically, there is at least 32–40px of space between this CTA row and the bottom paddings of the hero section, so the CTAs feel anchored but not cramped against the section boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable with its parent section; on tablet and mobile, the buttons wrap to multiple lines and center-align within the company-info-section while maintaining 16px vertical spacing between rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-areas-summary-panel is the visual entry point to the Service Area section and sits at the top of that section, inside the centered content container, directly below the About hero. There is 48–64px vertical space between the bottom of the hero and the top of this panel. The summary panel is left-aligned and spans roughly 8 of 12 columns (around 65–70% of the container width), giving it a strong but not overwhelming presence. It appears as a low-density card or band with 16–24px internal padding: a left-aligned heading at the top, a single short line or two of text describing real-time coverage data, and possibly a small icon to reinforce trust. There is 16–24px of vertical space between this panel and the components that follow (zip-coverage-form and service-areas-list), making it feel like a header/intro for the section rather than part of the control area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Service Area section; it anchors the section and maintains its width until the viewport narrows, then expands to full container width on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zip-coverage-form is placed in the Service Area section below the service-areas-summary-panel, aligned in the left-hand column of a two-column layout. This left column occupies about 4–5 of 12 columns (roughly 35–40% of the container width) and starts approximately 24px below the summary panel. The form itself is a compact card with 16–24px internal padding and a simple vertical stack: a small, bold label at the top, followed by an input field for the ZIP code spanning nearly the full width of this column, and a prominent rounded submit button aligned right or full-width under the input with 12–16px vertical spacing. The card has enough vertical padding (24–32px) so it does not feel cramped despite being functionally focused. Horizontally to its right, aligned on the same top baseline, the service-areas-list occupies the larger right column, reinforcing the Z-pattern: users look at the form on the left, then scan right to the broader list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the flow of the Service Area section; on smaller viewports it reflows to appear above the service-areas-summary-panel or directly beneath it, stacked full-width, with extra vertical spacing (24px) from neighboring components to preserve clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zip-coverage-status-banner sits immediately below the zip-coverage-form within the same left-hand column of the Service Area section, maintaining the same width as the form card. There is 16–24px vertical spacing between the bottom of the form and the top of this banner. The banner appears as a low-to-medium height info strip or card with a soft background color (e.g., pale green for covered, pale red/orange for not covered) and rounded corners to match the playful visual style. It spans nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–24px internal padding and a single line or two of text explaining whether the entered ZIP is covered, possibly including a small icon on the left. The banner should not visually overpower the form; it sits at about 60–70% of the form card’s height so it feels like a succinct feedback element rather than a full panel. Vertically below the banner, there is at least 24–32px of space before the bottom padding of the Service Area section, keeping the column open.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and updates dynamically within the layout, but its physical position remains fixed below the form. If no ZIP has been entered, it can either collapse entirely (no height) or show a neutral, low-contrast message. On mobile, it remains stacked directly under the form as both expand to full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-areas-list is positioned in the Service Area section to the right of the zip-coverage-form and zip-coverage-status-banner. It occupies the wider right-hand column of a two-column grid (about 7–8 of 12 columns, roughly 60–65% of the container width) and aligns its top edge horizontally with the top of the zip-coverage-form, sitting about 24px below the service-areas-summary-panel. The list is contained within a clean, card-like panel that follows the same padding conventions: 16–24px inner padding and rounded corners. Inside, a small heading or label sits at the top, followed by the list of cities/neighborhoods/ZIPs organized in either a multi-column text layout or grouped by subheadings, with comfortable 8–16px vertical spacing between items or groups. The card usually extends vertically farther than the left column, creating a tall right-hand panel that visually balances the denser text with generous internal spacing and possibly a light dividing line between groups. There is 32–48px of visual breathing room between the outer right edge of this card and the container edge, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the page. If the list is long, it may either scroll with the page naturally or use an internal scroll area, but the card’s top alignment and width remain consistent. On tablets and mobile, it reflows beneath the left column components, becoming full-width and maintaining at least 24px vertical spacing from the ZIP form/status stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-areas-loader is a non-visual, data-fetching component logically anchored to the Service Area section. Conceptually, it sits at the top of this section, above or just behind the service-areas-summary-panel, controlling when the summary panel, list, and status banner receive data. If a loading state is visually represented, it would appear as a subtle inline indicator (e.g., a small spinner or "Loading coverage…" text) centered within the service-areas-summary-panel or at the top of the service-areas-list card, occupying a small vertical space (around 24–32px height) while preserving existing padding and alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily invisible infrastructure. Any optional visual representation of loading is temporary and occupies space within existing panels rather than adding a new structural element to the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-controller is completely non-visual and does not occupy its own space in the layout. It is conceptually attached to the Service Area section, monitoring and updating URL parameters when a user interacts with the zip-coverage-form or filters the service-areas-list. Its influence is seen only in how the visible components reflect URL-driven state (e.g., pre-filled ZIP, auto-scrolled into view), not through any separate UI element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial footprint; it is an invisible controller that runs in the background. It does not change the positioning or sizing of other components, only their state and content.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing contact options, submitting general inquiries, and understanding the HVAC service coverage area, with quick navigation to key actions.

core_features: Display core contact options (phone, email, physical address, service hours) so users know how to reach the company and when.
Provide a general contact form that submits inquiries through submitContactMessage, including name, email, phone, subject, message, and preferredContactMethod.
Support automatic pre-filling of the contact form subject field using the incoming subject URL parameter from other pages.
Display a dynamic summary of HVAC service areas and ZIP code coverage by calling getServiceAreasSummary.
Provide quick action links to schedule_service.html, plans.html, and specials.html so users can move directly from a contact context to key actions.
user_workflows: General inquiry with prefilled subject from another page: (1) User clicks a contact-related link on product.html, plans.html, or index.html that opens contact.html with a subject URL parameter. (2) On page load, the contact-page-shell reads the subject parameter and passes it to the contact-form component. (3) The contact-form initializes its subject field with this value, while leaving other fields empty. (4) User enters name, email and/or phone, selects a preferredContactMethod, and types their message. (5) User clicks the form submit button. (6) contact-form validates required fields and, if valid, calls submitContactMessage with the current field values. (7) On success, contact-form notifies page-status-banner with the submitContactMessage response, and optionally updates the URL query string to include submitted=1 and messageId=<returned id>. (8) page-status-banner shows a success confirmation and an estimated response time derived from the API response. (9) If the user reloads the page with submitted=1 and messageId present, page-status-banner re-renders the confirmation message without re-calling submitContactMessage.

General inquiry started directly on the contact page (no prefilled subject): (1) User lands on contact.html without a subject URL parameter (e.g., from a footer Contact link or external directory). (2) contact-form initializes with all empty fields. (3) User fills in name, email and/or phone, optionally types a subject, selects preferredContactMethod, and writes a message. (4) On submit, contact-form validates the form. (5) If validation passes, contact-form calls submitContactMessage. (6) On success, page-status-banner shows a generic confirmation and estimated response time. (7) If the API response indicates failure or there is a network error, page-status-banner shows an error state and leaves the form data in place so the user can retry.

Reviewing service coverage before contacting: (1) User scrolls to the service-areas-summary-panel on contact.html. (2) On first render, service-areas-summary-panel calls getServiceAreasSummary. (3) When the response arrives, the panel displays a summary of areas (using the returned ServiceArea items) and the total number of ZIP codes. (4) If the user wants more detail, they can expand an area row (e.g., via a toggle) to see the full ZIP code list for that area. (5) If getServiceAreasSummary fails, the panel shows an error message and a retry button. (6) On clicking the retry button, the panel calls getServiceAreasSummary again.

Using contact page as a launch point for transactional actions: (1) User opens contact.html and decides to take direct action instead of sending a general inquiry. (2) If they want to schedule a visit, they click the Schedule Service quick-link in the quick-links-panel, which navigates to schedule_service.html. (3) If they want to explore maintenance plans first, they click the View Maintenance Plans quick-link, which navigates to plans.html. (4) If they want to see current promotions, they click the View Special Offers quick-link, which navigates to specials.html. (5) Navigation is handled via simple URL changes without additional API calls from this page.

Emergency vs non-urgent contact usage: (1) User lands on contact.html and sees an informational notice indicating that emergencies should be handled by phone and that the form is for non-urgent requests. (2) User determines whether their issue is urgent. (3) For urgent issues, user uses the phone number displayed in contact-details-section and does not submit the form. (4) For non-urgent questions, user proceeds to fill in and submit the contact form as in other workflows.
interactions: User edits contact form fields (name, email, phone, subject, message, preferredContactMethod). Each change updates the contact-form internal state without immediately calling any interface.
User submits the contact form via the primary submit button; this triggers validation and, if valid, calls submitContactMessage with the current form values.
User may click a reset or clear button (if provided) to clear all contact-form fields and remove any client-side validation errors. This does not call submitContactMessage.
User clicks the Schedule Service quick-link; the browser navigates to schedule_service.html with no additional query parameters defined by this page.
User clicks the View Maintenance Plans quick-link; the browser navigates to plans.html with no additional query parameters defined by this page.
User clicks the View Special Offers quick-link; the browser navigates to specials.html with no additional query parameters defined by this page.

On initial render of the service-areas-summary-panel, the component automatically calls getServiceAreasSummary. The user can trigger a refresh by clicking a Retry or Refresh button if the initial call fails.

User expands or collapses individual service area rows in the service-areas-summary-panel to view or hide the associated ZIP codes. This is a purely client-side interaction using the data already returned by getServiceAreasSummary.
User may arrive or reload the page with a subject URL parameter; this is read on page initialization and used to pre-populate the contact-form subject field.

User may arrive or reload the page with submitted=1 and messageId=<id> in the URL; page-status-banner reads these values on initialization and shows a persisted success message without resubmitting the form.

If submitContactMessage returns a non-success result or errors, page-status-banner displays an error state and may present a Retry button that causes contact-form to re-submit using the same field values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page uses URL parameters and in-memory component state to manage behavior. The subject URL parameter, when present (e.g., contact.html?subject=Encoded+Text), is read once on page initialization by the contact-page-shell and passed to the contact-form as an initialSubject prop. contact-form uses this initialSubject only for its initial state; any later changes in the subject field do not automatically update the URL. If no subject parameter is present, the subject field starts empty. To track submission results, contact-form and page-status-banner use a combination of the submitContactMessage response and optional URL parameters. After a successful submission, the page may update the query string (using history.pushState or similar) to include submitted=1 and messageId=<returned id>, while preserving any existing subject parameter, so the URL might look like contact.html?subject=...&submitted=1&messageId=.... On subsequent page loads or refreshes, page-status-banner checks the submitted parameter: if submitted=1 and messageId is present, it renders a success confirmation state even before any new submitContactMessage call. This allows the confirmation state to persist across reloads without storing anything in localStorage or other persistent client storage. If submitted is not present or has a different value, page-status-banner defaults to a neutral state. getServiceAreasSummary does not require or modify any URL parameters; its result is only stored in the internal state of service-areas-summary-panel. The quick-links-panel triggers navigation to schedule_service.html, plans.html, and specials.html without appending any additional parameters from contact.html, leaving those destination pages responsible for their own URL-based state. No state is stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; all client-side state beyond the URL parameters exists only in memory during the current page lifetime.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup, reads URL parameters, and wires together the contact form, status messages, contact details, service area summary, and quick links. It parses the subject, submitted, and messageId parameters from the query string and passes them as props or initialization values to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL parameters and provide initialSubject, initialSubmittedState, and initialMessageId to child components.
onSubmissionSuccess: Receive submission metadata from contact-form and optionally update the URL with submitted=1 and messageId=<id> using history.pushState.

components.functionality: Collects general contact information and non-urgent inquiries, validates input, and submits the data through submitContactMessage. It supports a prefilled subject based on an initialSubject prop from the URL and allows the user to choose a preferredContactMethod.
data_binding: URLParam:subject (used as initial value for subject field)
event_handlers: onChangeName: Update internal name state as user types.
onChangeEmail: Update internal email state and optionally run basic format validation.
onChangePhone: Update internal phone state and optionally run basic format validation.
onChangeSubject: Update internal subject state, overwriting any prefilled value.
onChangeMessage: Update internal message state as user types.
onChangePreferredContactMethod: Set internal preferredContactMethod state to one of the allowed values (email, phone, text).

onValidate: Run client-side checks (e.g., required fields such as name, at least one contact detail like email or phone, message, and a valid preferredContactMethod) before submission. Produce a structured validation result.

onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitContactMessage with { name, email, phone, subject, message, preferredContactMethod }. Handle the returned promise, then emit success or error events for page-status-banner and contact-page-shell.
onReset: Clear all form fields and validation errors to their initial empty state except for any fixed informational text. Does not re-read URL parameters.

components.functionality: Displays the current status related to contact form submission, including success confirmation with an estimated response time, or error messages if submission fails. It can also initialize itself based on URL parameters indicating a past successful submission.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URLParam:submitted and URLParam:messageId to determine if a prior success state should be displayed when the page first loads.
onSubmissionSuccess: Receive the submitContactMessage response from contact-form, store success, messageId, message, and estimatedResponseTimeHours, and render a success banner.
onSubmissionError: Receive error information (either from submitContactMessage returning success=false or from network errors) and render an error banner with optional guidance to retry.
onCloseStatus: Allow the user to dismiss the status banner; this updates internal visibility state but does not change URL parameters.

components.functionality: Displays non-interactive core contact details: primary phone contact, general email address, physical office or mailing address, and standard service or office hours, along with a short note distinguishing emergency phone contact from non-urgent form submissions.
event_handlers: onClickPhoneLink: If the phone number is rendered as a clickable link (e.g., tel:), trigger the browser’s default dialing behavior on supported devices.
onClickEmailLink: If the email is rendered as a mailto: link, open the user’s default email client with the address prefilled.

components.functionality: Retrieves and displays a summary of HVAC service coverage areas and ZIP codes by calling getServiceAreasSummary. Presents a high-level view (list of area names and counts) with optional expansion to see full ZIP lists for each area, and handles loading and error states.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getServiceAreasSummary when the component mounts to fetch the current service area summary.
onRetryFetch: Re-call getServiceAreasSummary if the initial or a prior fetch failed.
onToggleAreaExpansion: Toggle a boolean expanded state per ServiceArea item to show or hide its zipCodes list.
onFilterActiveAreas: Optionally filter displayed areas to only those with isActive=true, using only the data returned by the interface.

components.functionality: Provides action-oriented navigation from the contact context to scheduling service, reviewing maintenance plans, or viewing special offers. It does not use any data interfaces and only triggers client-side navigation to the specified pages.
event_handlers: onClickScheduleService: Navigate the browser to schedule_service.html without adding additional query parameters.
onClickViewMaintenancePlans: Navigate the browser to plans.html without adding additional query parameters.
onClickViewSpecialOffers: Navigate the browser to specials.html without adding additional query parameters.

components.functionality: Displays a concise notice explaining that the contact form is intended for non-urgent inquiries and that urgent or emergency HVAC issues should be handled via direct phone contact, referencing the phone information in contact-details-section.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom section flow with a fixed/floating global header and centered content. Inside the contact page, content is organized into a top intro/status band, a main two-column 'hero' contact section, and a secondary full-width service-area panel below. The main contact section mirrors the mockup’s About layout: a left column for explanatory/supporting content (emergency notice, contact details, quick links) taking roughly 5/12 of the width, and a right column for a visually dominant module (the contact form) taking roughly 7/12 of the width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom section flow with a fixed/floating global header and centered content. Inside the contact page, content is organized into a top intro/status band, a main two-column 'hero' contact section, and a secondary full-width service-area panel below. The main contact section mirrors the mockup’s About layout: a left column for explanatory/supporting content (emergency notice, contact details, quick links) taking roughly 5/12 of the width, and a right column for a visually dominant module (the contact form) taking roughly 7/12 of the width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above-the-fold real estate is dedicated primarily to the contact interaction: a shallow status banner, then a tall, generously padded two-column contact section occupying a large vertical slice of the viewport. The emergency notice and contact details in the left column get enough height and breathing room but remain visually lighter than the form card. The service-areas summary sits in its own full-width band below as a medium-height panel. Vertical section padding is in the 48–64px range; inner block spacing and gaps between components use 16–32px increments, following the 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is kept low to moderate in density: short headings, succinct support text, and clearly separated blocks. The form is grouped into logical clusters with visible spacing between fields. Panels like emergency notice, contact details, and quick links use generous padding and whitespace around icons/text so nothing feels crowded. Overall text is large and legible with comfortable line height, reflecting the approachable marketing feel of the mockup rather than a tightly packed tool.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow adapts the mockup’s Z-pattern. After the global cyan announcement bar and header, the eye lands on the page title at the top-left, then quickly catches the page-status banner spanning the content width. From there, the eye drops into the main two-column area: first to the left-column emergency notice (high-contrast accent) and contact options, then sweeps right to the larger, more prominent contact form. After completing the form or scanning details, the gaze moves downward to the service-areas panel that spans the width, providing closure with coverage information. A persistent chat widget (outside these components) anchors the bottom-left as a subtle tertiary focal point, consistent with the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits within a clean, white canvas framed by a full-width cyan announcement bar at the very top and a white header band with the logo and navigation locked to the top of the viewport. Below this global chrome, the main contact content is centered inside a max-width container, leaving soft margins on the left and right. At the top of this content area, a clear 'Contact' heading and a brief line of copy introduce the page, followed immediately by a slim, colored status banner that stretches across the content width. Beneath that, a large, airy two-column section echoes the mockup’s About layout: on the left, stacked cards for emergency notice, contact details, and quick links sit within a 5/12-width column; on the right, a taller, 7/12-width contact form card dominates the space, visually acting like the illustration card in the original mockup. The entire section has generous top and bottom padding so it feels like a hero-style interaction zone. Scrolling down, a full-width service-areas summary panel appears as its own section with light background contrast and a simple data-driven layout, still centered within the same content width. Throughout, rounded buttons, playful accent colors, and ample whitespace keep the page friendly and approachable, with a clear visual hierarchy that guides the user from urgent needs to general inquiries to coverage clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-shell defines the overall page frame inside the existing site chrome inspired by the mockup. At the very top of the viewport, a full-width cyan announcement bar runs edge to edge, relatively shallow (around 48px tall), used for site-wide promotional or service messages. Directly beneath it, a white header band spans the full width, containing the logo on the left and navigation items on the right, with a bold, rounded red CTA aligned to the far right, mirroring the mockup’s 'SUBSCRIPTIONS' button. Below this persistent header, the contact-page-shell provides a centered content container with a max-width of roughly 1120–1200px, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid and horizontally centered on the page. Left and right margins scale down responsively but typically show about 5–10% of the viewport as whitespace on large screens. The shell sets a base vertical padding of 64px from the bottom of the header to the start of the contact content, ensuring the page title and top banner don’t feel cramped. All other components (status banner, emergency notice, contact form, contact details, quick links, service areas) are placed within this central column, stacked vertically as distinct sections with 48–64px spacing between major bands and 16–24px spacing between related blocks. The background remains white throughout, with individual cards providing subtle shadows or borders to separate content while preserving the clean, uncluttered aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Provides the main flow layout within a fixed/floating global header; content scrolls vertically within the shell while the cyan announcement bar and header remain pinned to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-status-banner appears near the top of the main content column, just below the 'Contact' page heading and short introductory copy, and above the two-column contact section. It spans the full width of the centered content container (roughly 100% of the max-width), but remains relatively shallow in height, around 48–64px. The banner uses a lightly tinted background (for example, a pale cyan or soft yellow) with rounded corners and 16–24px of internal vertical padding, aligning visually with the cyan strip at the very top of the page but more subdued. The text is horizontally centered on smaller screens, but on desktop it is left-aligned with any icon or status indicator aligned to the left margin. There is a 24px gap below the introductory text and 32px of space below this banner before the start of the main two-column contact area, preserving the airy spacing of the mockup.
