The banner feels like a thin stripe of information that the eye easily catches when scanning downward from the header but does not overpower the main contact section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level banner within the scrollable content column; appears and disappears with scrolling and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency-notice is the first block in the left column of the main two-column contact section, occupying roughly the top-left quadrant of the main interaction area. Within the 5/12-width left column, the emergency notice is presented as a prominent card or pill-shaped panel with a bold accent color (for example, a warm red or orange) that contrasts against the white background. The card uses 24–32px of internal padding and spans nearly the full column width, leaving only 16px of horizontal gutter space on each side to the content container edge. A concise heading like '24/7 Emergency Heat & AC' appears at the top, with a slightly smaller line of supporting text and a primary 'Call Now' button aligned to the right or bottom-right within the card, echoing the top CTA in the mockup’s announcement bar. There is a 32px gap between the bottom of this emergency card and the next block (contact-details-section) in the column. Vertically, the top of the emergency notice aligns roughly with the top of the form card in the right column, creating a strong horizontal line, so when users glance from the left emergency callout they can sweep directly across to the right-hand form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card anchored at the top of the left column of the main two-column section; it scrolls with page content and resizes responsively to full width above the form on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the dominant element in the main contact section, occupying the entire right column in the two-column layout. This right column spans approximately 7/12 of the content width on desktop, making the form visibly larger and more visually weighty than the individual cards on the left. The form is placed at the same vertical starting line as the emergency-notice to its left, giving the section a clean, aligned top edge. It is contained within a white card with lightly rounded corners and a soft shadow or subtle border, set against the white page background. The card uses 32px top/bottom and 32–40px left/right padding, giving each form field plenty of breathing room. Form fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on screen width, but generally follow a single-column stack with 16–24px vertical gaps between groups. The form title ('Send us a message' or similar) appears at the top-left of the card, with a brief one-line reassurance about response time beneath it. The submit button is a large, rounded CTA aligned to the bottom-right of the card on wider screens and centered on smaller screens. There is a 64px margin below the form card before the start of the service-areas-summary-panel, keeping the main interaction chunk clearly separated. On tablet and mobile, the layout collapses so that the form spans 100% width and appears directly below the emergency-notice, maintaining its primary importance while preserving vertical spacing (24–32px) between these blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level card that participates in a responsive two-column layout; on wide screens it occupies the entire right column, and on narrow screens it becomes a full-width block stacked beneath the left-column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-section sits directly below the emergency-notice within the left column of the main two-column contact area. It is a secondary card that spans nearly the full width of the 5/12 column, leaving consistent 16px gutters to the sides. There is roughly 32px vertical space between the bottom of the emergency-notice card and the top of this contact-details card. The content is arranged in a simple, low-density layout: a clear heading such as 'Contact Details' at the top-left, followed by stacked lines of information (phone numbers, email, office hours) with 8–16px spacing between lines. Iconography (phone, mail, clock) may sit to the left of each piece of text, but kept small so the card stays text-driven and uncluttered. The card uses 24px internal padding and a very light background tint or hairline border to distinguish it from the page background. Below this section, a 24px gap separates it from the quick-links-panel, which continues the vertical stack in this left column. On smaller screens, the contact-details-section appears below the emergency-notice and above quick-links-panel in the single-column flow, each with the same 24–32px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static informational card that occupies a fixed place in the left column stack; it flows with the column content and becomes a full-width block when the layout stacks on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links-panel is positioned immediately below the contact-details-section in the left column, continuing the vertical stack within that 5/12-width area. It is set 24px beneath the contact-details card, maintaining the rhythm of spacing. This panel functions like a compact navigation block, listing key actions such as 'Schedule Maintenance', 'Request a Quote', or 'View FAQs'. Visually, it is a clean card with 24px internal padding and enough vertical spacing (12–16px) between each link item to keep the density low. Links may be arranged in a vertical list, each potentially styled as a text link with a small right-pointing arrow or as compact rounded pills that align left, reflecting the playful but minimal aesthetic. The panel’s width matches the card above, maintaining column alignment and gutters. It is slightly less visually intense than the emergency card—using a neutral or lightly tinted background—so it reads as helpful navigation rather than urgent action. On mobile, the quick-links-panel continues the single-column sequence, appearing below contact-details-section and above the service-areas-summary-panel with 24–32px spacing between these blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation block embedded in the left column stack; no sticky behavior, simply scrolls with the page and expands to 100% width in single-column layouts on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-areas-summary-panel is placed as its own full-width section below the main two-column contact area. After the bottom edges of the left and right column content (contact form and quick links) align, there is a generous 48–64px vertical gap before this panel begins, clearly separating it as a new section. The panel spans the full width of the central content container (roughly 1120–1200px max-width, centered) and may sit on a subtly tinted background band that stretches edge to edge of the viewport to distinguish it from the main contact area, similar to how a hero or feature section might be separated in marketing sites. Inside, the content could be arranged in a simple two-column layout or a single-column stack depending on complexity, but kept visually light: a heading like 'Service Areas' on the top-left, a short explanatory paragraph beneath it, and a concise list or grid of cities/regions served. Any map or iconography remains modest so the section still feels low-density and in line with the flat, simple UI style. Internal top and bottom padding for this panel is around 48–64px, allowing it to breathe like a dedicated section. At the bottom, additional 64px of padding separates this panel from the site footer, mirroring the vertical rhythm established at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static data panel forming its own section in the vertical flow; spans the same centered content width but can sit on a full-bleed tinted background band for separation. It scrolls normally with no fixed behavior.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Combined policy page that presents privacy practices, website terms of use, and service disclaimers, with deep-link navigation to specific sections via the 'section' URL parameter.

core_features: Display consolidated policy content for privacy, terms of use, and service disclaimers in clearly separated sections.
Support deep-link navigation to a specific policy section using the 'section' query parameter (e.g., policies.html?section=privacy).
Provide an on-page table of contents that allows users to switch between sections and updates the URL 'section' parameter accordingly.
Automatically scroll and focus to the relevant section on initial page load based on the 'section' URL parameter.

Explain, within the privacy policy section, how data provided through bookings, installation quote requests, financing applications, orders, and other forms is collected, used, and stored (content sourced dynamically, not hardcoded).
Explain terms of use covering website usage, limitations of liability, and any user responsibilities related to online bookings, offers, and purchases (content sourced dynamically, not hardcoded).

Explain service disclaimers, including emergency response expectations, appointment time windows, and promotional offer conditions, with text managed via backend/CMS (no hardcoded policy text in the frontend).
user_workflows:
Workflow: Access policies from the footer
1. User clicks the 'Policies' link in the global footer, which opens policies.html without a 'section' parameter.
2. The page controller reads the URL, finds no 'section' parameter, and selects a default section as configured by the backend (for example, the first configured policy section).
3. The table of contents displays all available policy sections, highlighting the default active section.
4. The main policy section viewer renders the content for the active section and optionally collapses non-active sections.
5. User may click other sections in the table of contents to view them; the URL is updated with the corresponding 'section' parameter and the page scrolls to the selected section.

Workflow: Deep link from checkout (privacy focus)
1. On checkout.html, the user clicks a legal link (for example, near the payment or contact form) that navigates to policies.html?section=privacy.
2. On load, the page controller parses the 'section' parameter value from the URL and sets the active section accordingly.
3. The page automatically scrolls to the privacy section and ensures it is expanded and focused for accessibility.
4. The user reviews the privacy content, including how order and payment-related data is handled (actual policy text provided by backend/CMS).
5. When finished, the user uses the browser back button or the site navigation to return to checkout.

Workflow: Deep link from financing plan page (terms/disclaimers)
1. On financing_plan.html, the user clicks a link such as 'View full terms and policies', which navigates to policies.html with a predefined 'section' parameter (e.g., ?section=terms or ?section=disclaimers, depending on the implementation).
2. The policies page reads the 'section' parameter and activates the corresponding section.
3. The main policy section viewer scrolls to and expands that section, so the user can read financing-related terms, data usage, and disclaimers about eligibility and approvals (content provided dynamically).
4. The user can switch to the privacy section via the table of contents if they also want to see how their financing application data is handled.
5. The URL is updated whenever they change sections, so they can bookmark or share the exact section they were viewing.

Workflow: User navigates between sections on the policies page
1. The user arrives on policies.html (with or without a 'section' parameter).
2. The table of contents component shows available sections (e.g., privacy, terms, disclaimers) with the current active section highlighted based on the page state.
3. The user clicks another section in the table of contents.
4. The click handler updates the internal active section state, updates the browser URL 'section' query parameter (using pushState/replaceState), and scrolls to the section anchor.
5. The selected section content becomes active (expanded/visible) while other sections may remain visible or be collapsed depending on configuration.
6. If the user refreshes the page, the 'section' parameter persists in the URL, so the same section is reloaded as active.

Workflow: User copies or bookmarks a direct link to a specific policy section
1. The user navigates to a specific section using the table of contents, which updates the URL 'section' parameter.
2. The user copies the full page URL from the browser address bar or bookmarks the page.
3. Later, when the user or another person opens that URL, the page controller reads the 'section' parameter and scrolls directly to the same section, ensuring a consistent deep-linked experience.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Browser loads policies.html (with or without a 'section' parameter).
- Behavior: The page controller parses the current URL's query string to retrieve the 'section' parameter value.
- If the parameter matches one of the configured section identifiers, that section is set as active; otherwise, the controller falls back to a backend-configured default section.
- The controller then instructs the policy section viewer to scroll to the active section's anchor and sets appropriate focus for accessibility.

Interaction: Click on table of contents item
- Trigger: User clicks a section link inside the table of contents component.
- Behavior: The click handler prevents default full-page navigation (if needed) and calls a handler in the page controller with the clicked section id.
- The controller updates its active section state, updates the URL 'section' query parameter using the History API (pushState/replaceState), and informs the policy section viewer to scroll to the relevant section.
- The table of contents component updates visual highlighting to indicate the new active section.

Interaction: Manual URL edit / navigation via browser controls
- Trigger: User manually changes the URL 'section' parameter, or navigates using browser back/forward where previous states had different 'section' values.
- Behavior: The browser triggers a navigation or popstate event; the page controller reads the updated 'section' parameter.
- If the new value maps to a known section, the controller updates the active section and scrolls accordingly.
- If the value does not map to a known section, the controller activates the configured default section and may optionally clear the invalid parameter from the URL.

Interaction: Optional in-page section anchor links
- Trigger: Within a long section (for example, privacy), the content may contain internal anchor links that jump to subsections related to specific data types (such as bookings, installation quote requests, financing applications, or orders).
- Behavior: Clicking such an internal link updates the scroll position to the specific subsection and may optionally update the 'section' parameter to a more specific identifier if supported by the configuration.
- The page controller ensures that these subsection anchors remain within the overarching active section, so the section-level state remains consistent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of external state for this page is the 'section' URL query parameter (policies.html?section=...). On initial load, the page controller reads this parameter to determine which policy section should be considered active. The mapping between 'section' parameter values and actual sections (such as privacy, terms, disclaimers, and any additional configured sections) is provided by a backend or configuration API; the frontend does not hardcode this mapping or any policy text. If the 'section' parameter is missing or does not match any known section id, the page controller selects a default section defined by configuration returned from the backend. The active section state is stored in memory within the page (not in localStorage) and is propagated to child components (table of contents, section viewer). Whenever the active section changes via user interaction, the controller updates the browser's URL to reflect the new 'section' parameter, ensuring the URL is always in sync with the current view. Browser navigation events (back/forward) are handled by re-reading the 'section' parameter and updating the active section and scroll position accordingly, so the user can rely on standard navigation behavior. No other persistent client-side storage is used for policy state.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Policies page. It parses the 'section' URL parameter, determines the active policy section using configuration from a backend/CMS, manages active section state, and coordinates scrolling/focus behavior. It passes section definitions and current active section id to child components such as the table of contents and the section viewer. It also updates the browser URL when the user switches sections, so deep links remain consistent.

Displays a structured list of available policy sections (e.g., privacy, terms, disclaimers) as returned by the PolicyContentAPI through the page controller. Highlights the currently active section. When the user clicks a section in the list, it notifies the page controller to change the active section and triggers URL and scroll updates.

Renders the content of each policy section defined by the backend, using anchors for each section id that can be targeted via the 'section' query parameter. It ensures that the active section is expanded and scrolled into view when instructed by the page controller. Non-active sections may optionally be collapsed or minimized based on configuration provided by the backend.

Within long sections such as the privacy policy, this component manages internal anchor links to specific subsections (for example, subsections that describe data handling for bookings, installation quote requests, financing applications, or orders). It adds link targets within the section content and can update the scroll position when a link is clicked, without altering the top-level 'section' parameter unless configured to do so.

Abstracted helper component (or service) responsible for synchronizing the active section state with the URL 'section' parameter. When the active section changes, it updates the URL query string using the History API, without forcing a full-page reload. When the browser history changes (back/forward), it re-parses the 'section' parameter and requests the page controller to adjust the active section state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; About section appears to use a two-column split (approximately 5/12 text and 7/12 image).
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, the Policies page content is presented as a single, full-width white section that follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. Inside a centered content container (roughly 70–80% of viewport width, max ~1120px), the page uses a two-column layout similar to the About section: a narrower left column for navigation (table of contents) and a wider right column for the active policy content. The main policy heading and short intro sit at the top of the right column, with subsection anchor links directly beneath, and the detailed policy text flowing below. The URL-sync behavior is handled invisibly by the controller layer, without altering the visual hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing cyan announcement bar and white header band, the Policies page content is presented as a single, full-width white section that follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. Inside a centered content container (roughly 70–80% of viewport width, max ~1120px), the page uses a two-column layout similar to the About section: a narrower left column for navigation (table of contents) and a wider right column for the active policy content. The main policy heading and short intro sit at the top of the right column, with subsection anchor links directly beneath, and the detailed policy text flowing below. The URL-sync behavior is handled invisibly by the controller layer, without altering the visual hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The policies content section has generous vertical padding (about 56–64px top and bottom) to echo the roomy About section. Horizontally, the content container is centered with 16–24px side padding on small screens and a fixed max-width on larger screens. Within the two-column layout, the left TOC column occupies roughly 30–35% of the content width (about 3–4 of 12 grid columns), and the right policy content column occupies about 65–70% (8–9 of 12 columns), mirroring the About section’s text/image weighting where the right side feels visually heavier. A 32px gutter separates the columns. Inside each column, headings, lists, and text blocks are spaced with 16–24px gaps to maintain the mockup’s open, breathable look.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is kept low to moderate despite the policy-heavy subject. Blocks of text are broken into shorter sections with clear headings and comfortable line height. The TOC list on the left is compact but not cramped, with clear vertical spacing between items. The right column prioritizes readable typography: medium-to-large headings, generous paragraph spacing (16–20px), and no more than 60–70 characters per line where possible. Subsection anchors are surfaced as a small, clearly separated control band under the main heading to help scanning without crowding the page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow keeps a soft Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. After the user’s eye passes the top header, it lands first on the large “Policies” heading in the upper-left of the main content area (right column’s top-left). From there, the eye moves slightly right and down to the subsection anchor links band, then across and down into the main policy text. As the user scrolls, the sticky TOC on the left column becomes a persistent secondary focal point guiding navigation, while the main reading area on the right remains the primary focus. The deep-link URL sync happens silently in the background, reinforcing this flow without introducing new visual elements.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the bright cyan announcement bar and the clean white header with logo and navigation, the Policies page opens into a calm, airy white section that fills most of the viewport height. A centered content container defines the reading area, with generous top and bottom padding so the content feels framed rather than cramped. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a tidy table of contents runs vertically, offering quick access to Privacy Policy, Terms of Use, and Disclaimers; on the right, a larger content panel presents the current policy with a bold, friendly page title at the top, a short explanatory blurb, and a row of pill-like subsection links immediately underneath. Below this band, the main policy text scrolls in a softly padded column with clear headings and ample white space. As the user scrolls, the left-hand TOC stays in view, subtly pinned beneath the header, while the right-hand content area scrolls normally. The entire composition feels consistent with the playful, approachable brand—rounded edges, simple flat styling, and plenty of breathing room—while still delivering a structured, professional policy experience. URL-based deep linking updates silently in the background, ensuring the visible layout remains clean and focused on the two-column reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller functions as the structural wrapper for the main Policies content area directly below the global site header. Visually, it manifests as a full-width white section spanning edge-to-edge of the viewport, but constrains all interactive content to a centered inner container that is approximately 70–80% of the viewport width with a max-width around 1120px. This inner container has generous vertical padding—about 56–64px from the top (below the header) and 48–56px at the bottom—mirroring the vertical breathing space of the About section. Within this container, the controller arranges children on a responsive 12-column grid. On desktop and tablet landscape, it establishes a two-column layout: the left column (for the table of contents) spans roughly 3–4 grid columns (about 30–35% of the container width), starting flush from the container’s left edge; the right column (for the policy section viewer and its subsection anchors) spans the remaining 8–9 grid columns (about 65–70% of the width). A clear gutter of about 32px separates the two columns. On smaller screens, the controller stacks these components vertically, with the TOC appearing above the main policy content, separated by 24px of vertical space. The controller ensures consistent spacing between sections: around 24px between the TOC and any adjacent elements, and 24–32px between the main heading region and the start of detailed content. It also reserves an unobtrusive margin at the bottom-left and bottom-right edges so floating widgets (like the brand’s chat bubble) do not overlap the primary reading zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a fluid, responsive container that anchors its content under the fixed/floating site header. It scrolls with the page and defines the grid and stacking behavior for the TOC and section viewer. It does not itself stick or float, but ensures internal components have the correct offsets so sticky elements (like the TOC) do not clash with the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents is placed in the left column of the main content area, starting near the top of the Policies section. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 30–35% of the centered content container, beginning flush against the container’s left padding. Vertically, its top edge aligns just below the top of the right column’s main heading region, creating the sense that both columns start together. The TOC is presented as a slim, vertical card or panel with subtle rounding on corners to match the brand’s friendly CTA style, and internal padding of approximately 16–24px. Each TOC item (e.g., Privacy, Terms, Disclaimers) is listed in a vertical stack with 12–16px separation, maintaining a clean, low-density feel. Overall height is flexible, expanding as items are added, but typically occupying the upper half to two-thirds of the viewport height; it does not stretch unnecessarily into blank space. On wide screens, the TOC remains visually lighter than the main content panel, using softer typography and smaller font size to read as navigational support rather than a competing focal point. On small screens, it spans the full width of the container and appears above the main content, with a bottom margin of around 24px before the page title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On medium and large screens, the TOC behaves as a sticky element within its left column: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it pins to the viewport with its top edge sitting just below the global header (leaving a 16–24px buffer so it doesn’t touch the header). It scrolls within the page but stays in view as long as its column is taller than the TOC content. On small screens, it loses the sticky behavior and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content to avoid obstructing the limited vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy section viewer dominates the right column, serving as the primary reading area. Horizontally, it occupies about 65–70% of the centered content container, starting to the right of the TOC with a consistent 32px gutter between them. It extends from near the top of the Policies section downwards, and typically fills the majority of the visible vertical space, creating a tall, scrollable panel of content. The viewer begins with a prominent section header area: a large, friendly page title (e.g., “Policies” or the active policy name) aligned to the left, with roughly 8–16px of space below it, followed by a brief explanatory paragraph or meta information (e.g., “How we handle your data” or “Last updated” line). Below this intro block, separated by about 24px, the subsection anchor links are rendered as a horizontal band (powered by the anchor-link-list component). Under that band, the main policy text is presented in clear blocks with generous line spacing and 16–24px vertical gaps between headings and paragraphs. The text runs in a single column with a comfortable measure (aiming for around 60–70 characters per line). On mobile, this viewer stretches to 100% of the container width and appears below the full-width TOC, with the same internal structure but slightly tightened vertical spacing to fit smaller screens while retaining an airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page and serves as the main scrollable reading area. It respects the global header offset at the top of the page and coordinates with the URL-sync behavior so that scrolling to anchors positions headings just below the header. Aside from accommodating the sticky subsection anchor band (if enabled), the viewer itself is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subsection anchor links appear within the right column, inside the policy-section-viewer, positioned directly below the main policy heading and introductory paragraph. They form a horizontal strip that spans roughly 80–100% of the content width of the right column, centered within that column with 16–24px side padding. Visually, they resemble a row of pill-shaped buttons or compact tabs, reflecting the site’s rounded CTA style but with a lighter, secondary emphasis. Individual anchors (e.g., “Information We Collect”, “Cookies”, “Your Rights”) are arranged left to right with 8–12px horizontal gaps and 8px vertical padding inside each pill, wrapping to multiple lines if the viewport is narrow. There is about 16–24px of space above this band (from the intro text) and 24px of space below it before the main body text begins, clearly separating it as a navigational control area. On mobile, the band becomes a multi-line pill cloud or a lightly scrollable horizontal row, but always remains visually distinct and not over-dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the policy-section-viewer, these anchors can optionally behave in a locally sticky manner on larger screens: once the user scrolls past their initial position, the band pins to the top edge of the right content column, just below the global header, ensuring quick access to subsections while reading. If this behavior is used, it respects a small offset (about 8–16px) beneath the header. On smaller screens, the anchor band scrolls with the content without sticking, to avoid consuming too much vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL synchronizer is non-visual and does not occupy visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it resides at the page-controller level, attached to the main Policies section, listening to scroll events and navigation interactions from the TOC and subsection links. It updates the browser’s URL with the correct `section` parameter or hash as the user scrolls into different policy blocks or clicks anchor links. It also interprets incoming URLs to initialize the correct active policy and subsection when the page loads or when the URL changes, ensuring the correct part of the policy text is scrolled into view. All of this occurs behind the scenes without introducing any new visual element or altering spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical; it has no visual presence and does not affect the physical layout. It behaves as an overlay behavior tied to scroll and navigation events, coordinating anchor positions with the header offset so that linked sections appear neatly at the top of the visible content area when navigated to.
